EDCS-05600951.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a formal guest-friendship (hospitium) between people from Hispania Citerior (Clunienses) and a Roman equestrian officer and his family, dated by the consuls to 21 BCE.

EDCS-05600952.translation: To the departed spirits. Anna, a most dutiful mother, sets this up for her daughter Albinia, aged 16, and for herself.
EDCS-05600952.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a mother for her deceased daughter and for herself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05600956.translation: To Aia Caela, daughter of Peregrinus, aged 56, ME had this made for his mother.
EDCS-05600956.summary: A tombstone commemorating Aia Caela, daughter of Peregrinus, who lived to be 56, erected by ME (likely a son or relative).

EDCS-05600957.translation: To Argea Longina, daughter of Gaius, aged 35. Qemea, her daughter, had this made for her mother.
EDCS-05600957.summary: A tombstone for Argea Longina, daughter of Gaius, aged 35, erected by her daughter Qemea.

EDCS-05600958.translation: For Cassia Flavina, daughter of Cassius Flavius, aged 35.
EDCS-05600958.summary: A tombstone for Cassia Flavina, daughter of Cassius Flavius, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-05600959.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Primula, aged 27.
EDCS-05600959.summary: A tombstone dedicated to the departed, commemorating Primula, who died at the age of 27.

EDCS-05600960.translation: To Sempronius Severus, son of Sempronius, aged 15. His most dutiful son (?) had this made.
EDCS-05600960.summary: A tombstone for Sempronius Severus, son of Sempronius, aged 15, likely erected by his son.

EDCS-05600961.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Julia Metella(?), in remembrance.
EDCS-05600961.summary: A tombstone dedicated to the spirits of the departed, commemorating Julia Metella(?).

EDCS-05600966.text_edition: \n Vota feliciter susceperunt libentes patronis merentissimis et felicissimis et praestantissimis et pientissimis cives pientissimi et amicissimi Segisamonenses domino nostro Augusto Gordiano et Aviola consulibus Gaio Sempronio Flavo Valeriae Severinae patronae nostrae Gaio Severio Presso Gaio Valerio Lupo Gaio Turellio Cassiano Publicius Paratus Publicius Martialis libertus gentilis Publicius Maritimus libertus gentilis Publicius Mascellio libertus gentilis Publicius Mercator libertus gentilis Valerius Candidus pectenarius Valerius Quintio Iulius Morinus Baebius Valoddus fullo Antonius Missillus sutor Iulius Eufemus Amainius Helenus fullo Aevaristus servus gentilis Aemilius Secundus Pelagius clavarius Antistia Caliope Valeria Donata Botia Valeria Britta Va<l=E>(eria) Avana Octavia Severa \n
EDCS-05600966.translation: Congratulations for undertaking vows willingly for the most deserving, most fortunate, most excellent, and most dutiful patrons, by the most dutiful and most friendly citizens of Segisama, for our lord Augustus Gordianus and Aviola, in the consulship of Gaius Sempronius Flavus and Valeria Severina, our patroness, and Gaius Severius Pressus, Gaius Valerius Lupus, Gaius Turellius Cassianus, Publicius Paratus, Publicius Martialis (freedman, of the same gentile family), Publicius Maritimus (freedman, of the same gentile family), Publicius Mascellio (freedman, of the same gentile family), Publicius Mercator (freedman, of the same gentile family), Valerius Candidus the comb-maker, Valerius Quintio, Iulius Morinus, Baebius Valoddus the fuller, Antonius Missillus the shoemaker, Iulius Eufemus, Amainius Helenus the fuller, Aevaristus the slave (of the same gentile family), Aemilius Secundus, Pelagius the lock-keeper, Antistia Caliope, Valeria Donata, Botia Valeria Britta, Valeria Avana, Octavia Severa.
EDCS-05600966.summary: A dedicatory inscription from the citizens of Segisama to their patrons, including the emperor Gordian III and others, in the year 241 AD, listing numerous individuals and professions.

EDCS-05600984.translation: To the spirits of the departed, sacred. Marius Myron and his daughter Vaenico Tyche, the most dutiful, and also for Vaenico Tyche his wife, had this made.
EDCS-05600984.summary: A tombstone set up by Marius Myron for his daughter Vaenico Tyche and his wife, also named Vaenico Tyche.

EDCS-05600985.translation: To Mummius Valens. Mummius Pressus for his most excellent father.
EDCS-05600985.summary: A tombstone erected by Mummius Pressus for his father, Mummius Valens.

EDCS-05600987.translation: Publius Aurelius Tempestivos had this made from his own funds for his father Aurelius Tannepaeserus and his mother Asterdumaria.
EDCS-05600987.summary: A tombstone erected by Publius Aurelius Tempestivos for his parents, Aurelius Tannepaeserus and Asterdumaria.

EDCS-05600988.translation: To Publius Aemilius Ducto Barb(e)tanus, by his heir under the will, from Aemilia Placida, his daughter.
EDCS-05600988.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Aemilius Ducto Barb(e)tanus, set up by his daughter as heir.

EDCS-05600996.translation: For the health and safe return of Aburius Crescens, Florus Obanae willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-05600996.summary: This votive inscription records Florus Obanae fulfilling a vow for the well-being and return of Aburius Crescens.

EDCS-05600998.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Aemili Lupi Luci Aemili Lupi annorum XIII Lucius Aemilius Priscus pater et Domitia Severa mater fecerunt\n
EDCS-05600998.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Aemilius Priscus and Domitia Severa, father and mother, made this for Lucius Aemilius Lupus, aged thirteen.
EDCS-05600998.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Aemilius Lupus, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601004.translation: To the greatest god Jupiter. Valerius Aur(elius?) willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow with this altar.
EDCS-05601004.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, recording Valerius Aur(elius?) fulfilling a vow by dedicating an altar.

EDCS-05601009.translation: To Matugenu, son of Cadanus. Longinus, a sesquiplicarius (a cavalry rank) of the Vettonian auxiliary cavalry, placed this at his own expense.
EDCS-05601009.summary: This funerary monument for Matugenu, son of Cadanus, was erected by Longinus, a soldier in the Vettonian auxiliary cavalry.

EDCS-05601013.translation: Gaius Julius Optatus and Julius Que[ntus] and Iulius Lucen[tius] had this made while alive for Iulius [---] and Iulia Cal[-- -].
EDCS-05601013.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned during the lifetime of the deceased by Gaius Julius Optatus and Iulius Que[ntus] and Iulius Lucen[tius] for Iulius [---] and Iulia Cal[-- -].

EDCS-05601018.translation: Baebia Calybe is buried here. Her sons Baebius Successus and Epaphroditus, and the associates of Claudianus, had this made for their mother.
EDCS-05601018.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Baebia Calybe, set up by her sons and a group named the associates of Claudianus.

EDCS-05601020.translation: Gaius Julius Celtiber, son of Gaius Julius, is buried here.
EDCS-05601020.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Gaius Julius Celtiber, son of Gaius Julius, as being buried at this location.

EDCS-05601021.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Phyllis, slave of Iulia Nigra, is buried here. Vitalis took care to have this made for his companion. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601021.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Phyllis, a slave, set up by her companion Vitalis.

EDCS-05601023.summary: This is a fragment recording the name and rank (tribune) of Gaius Valerius Avitus.

EDCS-05601024.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Quintilia, likely a military comrade, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601037.translation: The family of Ocule(n)s fulfilled their vow to the god Airon.
EDCS-05601037.summary: This is a votive inscription by the family of Ocule(n)s, dedicated to the god Airon, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601039.translation: Lucilia, daughter of Lucius, aged 29, is buried here.
EDCS-05601039.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucilia, daughter of Lucius, who died at the age of 29, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601043.translation: Here lies Sempronia Clarissima, aged 39. Her devoted mother Claudia had her buried, may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601043.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Sempronia Clarissima, set up by her mother Claudia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601045.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lu() Acaccia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601077.text_edition: \n Caio Laetitio Marci filio Apalo IIviro quinquennali Lares Augustales et Mercurium piscatores et propolae de pecunia sua faciendum curaverunt idemque probaverunt\n
EDCS-05601077.translation: The Augustan Lares and Mercury, god of fishermen and merchants, arranged for this to be built at his own expense by Caius Laetilius Appalus, twice a magistrate with quinquennial powers, and they approved it.
EDCS-05601077.summary: This inscription records the construction of a public work, possibly a building or pavement, by Caius Laetilius Appalus, a local magistrate, for the "Augustan Lares and Mercury, god of fishermen and merchants," dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601078.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Tiberi filio Neroni patrono coloni\n
EDCS-05601078.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Nero, son of Tiberius, patron of the colonists.
EDCS-05601078.summary: This is a dedication honoring Tiberius Claudius Nero as patron of the colonists, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601079.text_edition: \n praefecto Imperatoris Caesaris IIviro designato accensus et pavimentum de sua pecunia faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-05601079.translation: The beadle arranged for the pavement to be built at his own expense and approved it.
EDCS-05601079.summary: This inscription records the construction of a pavement by an official (accensus), possibly for a magistrate or emperor, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601081.text_edition: \n Statia SCAT--- Luci filia Phiale salve\n
EDCS-05601081.translation: Greetings, Statia Phiale, daughter of Lucius SCAT---.
EDCS-05601081.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Statia Phiale, daughter of Lucius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601082.text_edition: \n Vergiliae Caesiae Martha liberta de suo faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-05601082.translation: Vergilia Caesiae, Martha, her freedwoman, arranged for this to be built at her own expense.
EDCS-05601082.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument or structure commissioned by Martha, a freedwoman of Vergilia Caesiae, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601091.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonia Sambarulla nnorum XV hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-05601091.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonia Sambarulla, aged 15, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601091.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia Sambarulla, a young girl who died at age 15, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601097.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Lucius Porcius Plutus IIIIIIvir Augustalis sua pecunia dedit\n
EDCS-05601097.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Lucius Porcius Plutus, one of the IIIIvir Augustalis, dedicated this with his own money.
EDCS-05601097.summary: A dedication to Emperor Hercules Augustus, made by Lucius Porcius Plutus, an official of the Augustalis college, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-05601098.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Varia Nigrina.
EDCS-05601098.summary: A brief funerary dedication to Varia Nigrina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601104.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that appears to record a personal name, possibly Postuminus, with an uncertain context.

EDCS-05601107.text_edition: \n Quinto Granio Quinti filio Galeria Clementi omnibus honoribus in republica functo Iuni Festus et Severus avunculo\n
EDCS-05601107.translation: To Quintus Granius Clement, son of Quintus, of the Galerian tribe, who fulfilled all public offices, Junius Festus and Severus, his uncle.
EDCS-05601107.summary: A funerary dedication from Junius Festus and Severus to their relative Quintus Granius Clement, who had held various public offices, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601108.text_edition: \n Capraria Valentis sequiplicani et Rogatae filia vixsit anis II hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-05601108.translation: Capraria Valens, wife of Seplicanus, and Rogata, her daughter, lived 2 years. She lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601108.summary: A funerary epitaph for Capraria Valens and her infant daughter Rogata, who died at age 2, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601109.text_edition: \n Lucius Domitius Eques annorum XXXV Sempronia Luci filia Campana uxor prior annorum XVIII hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-05601109.translation: Lucius Domitius, an Equites, aged 35, and Sempronia Campana, daughter of Lucius, his former wife, aged 18, lie here.
EDCS-05601109.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the burial of Lucius Domitius, a man of equestrian rank, and his first wife Sempronia Campana, both likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601110.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius H sibi et Corneliae Sphragidi uxori voto sum compos supe coniugis ut volui sum \n
EDCS-05601110.translation: Gaius Julius H dedicated this for himself and for his wife Cornelia Sphragis, fulfilling a vow as I wished for my spouse.
EDCS-05601110.summary: A dedication by Gaius Julius H for himself and his wife Cornelia Sphragis, made in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601111.translation: To [his] father Gaetulicus, who deserved well.
EDCS-05601111.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication to a deserving father named Gaetulicus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601112.translation: Lucius Saenius set this up for Onesimus, his father.
EDCS-05601112.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Saenius to his father Onesimus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601113.text_edition: \n nnorum LX hic situs est Munatia Restituta contubernali posuit\n
EDCS-05601113.translation: Munatia Restituta placed this here for her companion of 60 years.
EDCS-05601113.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a man who lived 60 years and was placed here by his companion, Munatia Restituta.

EDCS-05601115.text_edition: \n Terentiae Doryphoridi Sexti filiae Aemilia Scintilla filiae pientissimae et Sextus Terentius Lemnaeus sorori\n
EDCS-05601115.translation: To Terentia Doryphoris, daughter of Sextus. Aemilia Scintilla placed this for her most devoted daughter, and Sextus Terentius Lemnaeus for his sister.
EDCS-05601115.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Terentia Doryphoris and was erected by her mother, Aemilia Scintilla, and her brother, Sextus Terentius Lemnaeus.

EDCS-05601118.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Saufeius Cnaei libertus Hesychus annorum XVIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-05601118.translation: Gnaeus Saufeius Hesychus, freedman of Gnaeus, who lived 18 years, is buried here.
EDCS-05601118.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gnaeus Saufeius Hesychus, an 18-year-old freedman.

EDCS-05601119.text_edition: \n Thymele hic sita est Rusticus de suo faciendum quravit\n
EDCS-05601119.translation: Thymele is buried here. Rusticus took care to have this made at his own expense.
EDCS-05601119.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Thymele, with Rusticus commissioning the monument at his own expense.

EDCS-05601120.text_edition: \n Marcus Acilius Eros hic situs est pistor liberti libertaeque faciendum curaverunt quisquis in has partes quisquis percurris in illas precor uti dicas sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-05601120.translation: Marcus Acilius Eros is buried here. His freedmen and freedwomen took care to have this made. Whoever runs this way, whoever runs that way, I beg you to say: may the earth be light for you.
EDCS-05601120.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Marcus Acilius Eros, commissioned by his freedmen and freedwomen, and includes a common plea for passersby to wish him well.

EDCS-05601121.text_edition: \n Lucius Cornelius Germanusus Augustalis annorum LVII hic situs est Baebia --- \n
EDCS-05601121.translation: Lucius Cornelius Germanusus Augustalis, who lived 57 years, is buried here. Baebia [---]
EDCS-05601121.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Lucius Cornelius Germanusus Augustalis, aged 57, with a partially preserved mention of Baebia.

EDCS-05601122.text_edition: \n Caelia Quinti filia Gemella annorum XXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-05601122.translation: Caelia Gemella, daughter of Quintus, who lived 30 years, is buried here.
EDCS-05601122.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Caelia Gemella, daughter of Quintus, aged 30.

EDCS-05601123.text_edition: \n Marco Fulvio Propinquo patri et Marco Fulvio Prisco filio annorum XIX Iunia Crocale sua pecunia faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-05601123.translation: Iunia Crocale took care to have this made with her own money for her father Marcus Fulvius Propinquus and her son Marcus Fulvius Priscus, aged 19.
EDCS-05601123.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Marcus Fulvius Propinquus and his son Marcus Fulvius Priscus (aged 19), commissioned by Iunia Crocale.

EDCS-05601124.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Grattius Arescon nnorum L hic situs est\n
EDCS-05601124.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Grattius Arescon, who lived 50 years, is buried here.
EDCS-05601124.summary: This funerary inscription honors Grattius Arescon, aged 50, with an invocation to the underworld deities.

EDCS-05601125.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publicia Eraste annorum XIIII mensium X \n
EDCS-05601125.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publicia Eraste, who lived 14 years and 10 months.
EDCS-05601125.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Publicia Eraste, aged 14 years and 10 months, with an invocation to the underworld deities.

EDCS-05601126.text_edition: \n Grattio Grattia Plusias patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-05601126.translation: To Grattius Grattius Plusias for his father, who deserved well.
EDCS-05601126.summary: This is a dedication on a tombstone from a son to his well-deserving father.

EDCS-05601127.translation: To the Divine Spirits for Valeria Chryseis.
EDCS-05601127.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the Divine Spirits for Valeria Chryseis, likely on her tomb.

EDCS-05601128.text_edition: \n et Voconianus alumno pientissimo hic situs est\n
EDCS-05601128.translation: And Voconianus, most devoted pupil, is buried here.
EDCS-05601128.summary: This inscription records that Voconianus, a devoted pupil, is buried here.

EDCS-05601129.text_edition: \n Caecilia Gai filia Festa Marcus Valerius Marci filius Galeria Veranus annorum LXXXV hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-05601129.translation: Caecilia Festa, daughter of Gaius, and Marcus Valerius, son of Marcus, of the Galerian tribe, Veranus, aged 85, are buried here.
EDCS-05601129.summary: This tombstone records the burial of two individuals, Caecilia Festa and Marcus Valerius Veranus, who lived to be 85 years old.

EDCS-05601130.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelia Vernaculae vixit nnos XVIIII menses VIII\n
EDCS-05601130.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Cornelia Vernacula lived 19 years and 8 months.
EDCS-05601130.summary: This is a funerary inscription to the Divine Spirits, recording the lifespan of Cornelia Vernacula.

EDCS-05601131.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus pupus Publilius Placidus annos vixit XII\n
EDCS-05601131.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus, the child, Publilius Placidus lived 12 years.
EDCS-05601131.summary: This funerary inscription to the Divine Spirits records the short life of a child named Marcus Publilius Placidus.

EDCS-05601132.text_edition: \n Lucio Titio Ingenuo marito bene merito\n
EDCS-05601132.translation: To Lucius Titius Ingenuus, her husband, who deserved well.
EDCS-05601132.summary: This is a dedication on a tombstone from a wife to her well-deserving husband.

EDCS-05601133.summary: This is an inscription bearing the name of Emperor Caesar Augustus, likely on a public monument or building.

EDCS-05601134.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aufidia Prisca vixit nnos XX menses III Caecilia Cila mater\n
EDCS-05601134.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aufidia Prisca lived 20 years and 3 months. Caecilia Cila, her mother.
EDCS-05601134.summary: This funerary inscription, set up by Caecilia Cila, records the short life of her daughter Aufidia Prisca.

EDCS-05601135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saraucionis vixit annis XXXVIII Celerina sibi et contubirnali sic piissimo fecit\n
EDCS-05601135.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Saraucion lived 38 years. Celerina made this for herself and her most devoted companion.
EDCS-05601135.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Saraucion at 38 years old, and was made by Celerina for herself and her companion.

EDCS-05601137.text_edition: \n L NI TCCI IROE K CR\n
EDCS-05601137.translation: Nothing can be reliably translated due to severe damage and lack of context.
EDCS-05601137.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription with too little text to determine its content or purpose.

EDCS-05601142.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus repaired [this].
EDCS-05601142.summary: This inscription records the repair of a structure by Emperor Trajan, likely during his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-05601143.summary: This inscription simply records the name of Publius Talpicius Rusticus, possibly as a dedication or identification.

EDCS-05601162.translation: From the province of Hispania Citerior. For Lucius Fabius Silo, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, from Brigiaecinum, twice duovir, priest of Rome and the Emperors, elected to the fifth decuria of judges in Rome, and as flamen of the province of Hispania Citerior.
EDCS-05601162.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors Lucius Fabius Silo, a prominent local official and priest in Hispania Citerior, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05601181.summary: This very short inscription possibly refers to ownership or a dedication related to a wheel, possibly belonging to someone named Boaetus.

EDCS-05601197.translation: To the spirits of the departed. I, Sextus Perpenna Firmus, lived as I wished; therefore, I do not know why I am dead.
EDCS-05601197.summary: This is a humorous or philosophical epitaph for Sextus Perpenna Firmus, who died unexpectedly despite living as he pleased.

EDCS-05601211.text_edition: \n Titiniae Publi filiae Bastogaunini Marcus Licinius Neitinbeles coniugi\n
EDCS-05601211.translation: To Titinia Bastogaunina, daughter of Publius, as his wife, Marcus Licinius Neitinbeles.
EDCS-05601211.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Marcus Licinius Neitinbeles to his wife, Titinia Bastogaunina.

EDCS-05601212.text_edition: \n Lucio Licinio Secundo accenso patrono suo Lucio Licinio Surae primo secundo tertio consulatu eius IIIIIIviro Augustali coloniae Iuliae Urbis Triumphalis Tarraconis et coloniae Faventiae Iuliae Augustae Paternae Barcinonis ex decreto decurionum Barcinonensium\n
EDCS-05601212.translation: To Lucius Licinius Secundus, his adjutor and patron, under Lucius Licinius Sura during his first, second, and third consulships; as Augustalis duovir of the colony of Julia Urbs Triumphalis Tarraco and of the colony of Faventia Julia Augusta Patera, Barcino, by decree of the decurions of Barcino.
EDCS-05601212.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Licinius Secundus and lists his multiple civic and religious offices in the colonies of Tarraco and Barcino, likely dating to the period of Licinius Sura's consulships.

EDCS-05601213.text_edition: \n Lucio Licinio Secundo accenso patrono suo Lucio Licinio Surae primo secundo tertio consulatu eius IIIIIIviro Augustali coloniae Iuliae Urbis Triumphalis Tarraconis et coloniae Faventiae Iuliae Augustae Paternae Barcinonis ex decreto decurionum Barcinonensium\n
EDCS-05601213.translation: To Lucius Licinius Secundus, his adjutor and patron, under Lucius Licinius Sura during his first, second, and third consulships; as Augustalis duovir of the colony of Julia Urbs Triumphalis Tarraco and of the colony of Faventia Julia Augusta Patera, Barcino, by decree of the decurions of Barcino.
EDCS-05601213.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Licinius Secundus and lists his multiple civic and religious offices in the colonies of Tarraco and Barcino, likely dating to the period of Licinius Sura's consulships.

EDCS-05601214.text_edition: \n Lucio Licinio Secundo accenso patrono suo Lucio Licinio Surae primo secundo tertio consulatu eius IIIIIIviro Augustali coloniae Iuliae Urbis Triumphalis Tarraconis et coloniae Faventiae Iuliae Augustae Paternae Barcinonis Marcus Antonius Antullus cives sic Convena amico\n
EDCS-05601214.translation: To Lucius Licinius Secundus, his adjutor and patron, under Lucius Licinius Sura during his first, second, and third consulships; as Augustalis duovir of the colony of Julia Urbs Triumphalis Tarraco and of the colony of Faventia Julia Augusta Patera, Barcino; Marcus Antonius Antullus, citizen, to his friend Convena.
EDCS-05601214.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Licinius Secundus and also records a dedication from Marcus Antonius Antullus to his friend Convena, likely dating to the period of Licinius Sura's consulships.

EDCS-05601216.text_edition: \n Tito Iulio Titi filio Galeria Placido aedili IIviro flamini Valeriae Novellae Titus Iulius Placidus filius hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-05601216.translation: For Titus Iulius Placidus, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe, aedile, duovir, and flamen, and for Valeria Novella, Titus Iulius Placidus, son of Titus, this monument shall not pass to an heir.
EDCS-05601216.summary: A funerary monument for Titus Iulius Placidus, aedile and duovir, and Valeria Novella, stating it is not inheritable.

EDCS-05601217.text_edition: \n Lucio Iulio Luci filio Galeria veterano legionis II Iuliae Luci filiae R\n
EDCS-05601217.translation: To Lucius Iulius, son of Lucius, a veteran of the Second Iulian Legion, and his daughter Lucius.
EDCS-05601217.summary: A funerary dedication to Lucius Iulius, a legionary veteran, and his daughter.

EDCS-05601218.text_edition: \n Caio Domitio Luci filio Materno Acucensi huic ordo Barcinonensium honorem decurionatus dedit Domitio Flavo onia Maura Corn ]\n
EDCS-05601218.translation: To Gaius Domitius Maternus, son of Lucius, from Acucis; the council of Barcino granted him the honor of decurionate; to Domitius Flavus...
EDCS-05601218.summary: A record of civic honor, the decurionate, being bestowed upon Gaius Domitius Maternus by the town council of Barcino.

EDCS-05601219.text_edition: \n Lucio Caecilio Luci liberto Crescenti IIIIIIviro Augustali Caecilia Luci liberta Pieris viro et sibi hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-05601219.translation: To Lucius Caecilius Crescentius, freedman of Lucius, Augustalis Seviri; his wife Caecilia Pieris, freedwoman of Lucius, and for himself, this monument shall not pass to an heir.
EDCS-05601219.summary: A funerary monument for Lucius Caecilius Crescentius, an Augustalis Seviri, and his wife Caecilia Pieris, stating it is not inheritable.

EDCS-05601220.text_edition: \n L(ucio Pedanio L(uci)? l(iberto) Epicteto IIIIIIviro Augustali Acilia Arethusa marito optimo locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-05601220.translation: A plot was granted by decree of the decurions to Lucius Pedanius Epictetus, freedman of Lucius (?), Augustalis Seviri, for his most excellent husband.
EDCS-05601220.summary: A burial plot granted by town council decree to Lucius Pedanius Epictetus, an Augustalis Seviri, for his husband.

EDCS-05601221.summary: A dedication to Lucius Iulius, who held the high civic office of Augustalis Seviri.

EDCS-05601222.text_edition: \n Publius Antonius Publi filius Pudens Lixitanus sibi et Baebiae Titi libertae Novellae uxori Baebiae Placidae filiae et Corneliae Phaenusae uxori hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur nec locum sepulturae\n
EDCS-05601222.translation: Publius Antonius Pudens of Lixita, son of Publius, for himself and for Baebia Novella, freedwoman of Titus, his wife, and for Baebia Placida, his daughter, and for Cornelia Phaenusa, his wife, this monument and burial place shall not pass to heirs.
EDCS-05601222.summary: A funerary monument and burial plot established by Publius Antonius Pudens for his family, stipulating it is not inheritable.

EDCS-05601223.text_edition: \n Lucio Fabio Luci liberto Restituto Urcitano et Mariae Telete uxori ex testamento fecerunt\n
EDCS-05601223.translation: Made from the will for Lucius Fabius Restitutus of Urcitano, freedman of Lucius, and his wife Maria Telete.
EDCS-05601223.summary: A funerary monument erected according to a will for Lucius Fabius Restitutus and his wife Maria Telete.

EDCS-05601224.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemilio Cal Aemilia Flora marito optimo\n
EDCS-05601224.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Aemilia Flora for her most excellent husband, Aemilius Cal...
EDCS-05601224.summary: A dedication to the departed spirits for a husband, Aemilius Cal..., by his wife Aemilia Flora.

EDCS-05601225.summary: A dedication to a woman named Aemilia Philumene, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-05601226.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antigenide annorum XII Usulenia Atticilla mater filio bene merenti et sibi VIII\n
EDCS-05601226.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Usulenia Atticilla erected this for her son Antigenides, aged 12, who deserved well of her, and for herself.
EDCS-05601226.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named Antigenides, set up by his mother, Usulenia Atticilla, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601227.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Araniae Fortunatae Grania Maxima matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-05601227.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Grania Maxima erected this for her mother Araniae Fortunatae, who deserved well of her.
EDCS-05601227.summary: A funerary epitaph for a mother named Araniae Fortunatae, dedicated by her daughter, Grania Maxima, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atheneo Ursa conservo et marito bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-05601228.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Ursa erected this for her fellow slave and husband Atheneus, who deserved well of her.
EDCS-05601228.summary: A funerary epitaph for a man named Atheneus, dedicated by his wife and fellow slave, Ursa, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601229.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Callisteni annorum IIII dierum XXV Cornelius Thallion vernae karissimae et sibi\n
EDCS-05601229.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cornelius Thallion erected this for his extremely dear slave Callistenes, aged 4 years and 25 days, and for himself.
EDCS-05601229.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young slave named Callistenes, set up by his owner, Cornelius Thallion, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601230.text_edition: \n Quintus Cornelius Geminianus sibi et Quinto Cornelio Gemino patri Exorate matri Daphnidi et Terpsicore libertabus Caio Helvio Domestico amico\n
EDCS-05601230.translation: Quintus Cornelius Geminianus for himself, his father Quintus Cornelius Geminus, his mother Daphnis, and his freedwomen Terpsicore and Caio Helvio Domestico, his friend.
EDCS-05601230.summary: A funerary dedication by Quintus Cornelius Geminianus for himself and several family members and a friend, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601231.text_edition: \n Domitia Luci liberta Aucta sibi et Publio Fabio Primioni contubernbali hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur nec locus sepulturae\n
EDCS-05601231.translation: Domitia Aucta, freedwoman of Lucius, for herself and for Publius Fabius Primio, her companion in life; this monument shall not pass to an heir, nor shall the burial place.
EDCS-05601231.summary: A funerary dedication by Domitia Aucta, a freedwoman, for herself and her partner Publius Fabius Primio, stipulating the plot is not inheritable, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601232.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Eutyches, fellow slave.
EDCS-05601232.summary: A funerary epitaph for a fellow slave named Eutyches, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601233.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaminiae Celtiberae Valeria Crescentina amicae bene merenti\n
EDCS-05601233.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Flaminia Celtibera. Valeria Crescentina, her friend, erected this for her, who deserved well of her.
EDCS-05601233.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flaminia Celtibera, dedicated by her friend Valeria Crescentina, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601235.translation: Of Marcus Luccius Chilo, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-05601235.summary: An ownership inscription, likely indicating the owner of a tomb or property, for Marcus Luccius Chilo, freedman of Marcus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601236.text_edition: \n Titus Mamilius Primus sibi et Anniae Laietanae uxori et Valeriae Armoniae\n
EDCS-05601236.translation: Titus Mamilius Primus for himself, his wife Annia Laietana, and Valeria Armonia.
EDCS-05601236.summary: A funerary dedication by Titus Mamilius Primus for himself, his wife Annia Laietana, and Valeria Armonia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601237.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Numisiae Ursulae Aelius Cestinus maritus et Numisius Cestus filius\n
EDCS-05601237.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Numisia Ursula, Aelius Cestinus her husband and Numisius Cestus her son [dedicated this].
EDCS-05601237.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Numisia Ursula by her husband and son.

EDCS-05601238.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Peregrino Festiva conservo pientissimo bene merenti et sibi\n
EDCS-05601238.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Peregrinus, the most dutiful and deserving freedman, and for himself.
EDCS-05601238.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Peregrinus by his partner, and also includes space for the dedicator.

EDCS-05601239.text_edition: \n Lucio Pompeio Saturnino Iuliae Paschusae Iuliae Callyroe vixit annos XXI hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-05601239.translation: To Lucius Pompeius Saturninus, [and] to Iulia Paschusa, [and] to Iulia Calliroe. She lived 21 years. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-05601239.summary: This funerary monument is for Iulia Calliroe, who died at age 21, and also for Lucius Pompeius Saturninus and Iulia Paschusa, with a clause stating it will not pass to an heir.

EDCS-05601240.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Porciolae nni mensis I diei I Trocina Chreste fecit\n
EDCS-05601240.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Porciola. 1 month, 1 day. Trocina Chreste made this.
EDCS-05601240.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Porciola, with her age given in months and days, made by Trocina Chreste.

EDCS-05601241.text_edition: \n Caius Publicius Primus sibi et Coeliae Primigeniae uxori hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-05601241.translation: Gaius Publicius Primus for himself and for Coelia Primigenia his wife. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-05601241.summary: This funerary monument is for Gaius Publicius Primus and his wife Coelia Primigenia, with a clause stating it will not pass to an heir.

EDCS-05601242.text_edition: \n L(ucio Rufidio Pollentino amico optimo Rufidia PsaM \n
EDCS-05601242.translation: To Lucius Rufidius Pollentinus, his best friend. Rufidia P... M... [dedicated this].
EDCS-05601242.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Lucius Rufidius Pollentinus by Rufidia P... M... as a token of friendship.

EDCS-05601243.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Melippo Caelia Quartula fecit patri cupam bene merenti et Caelia Saturnina uxor marito optimo\n
EDCS-05601243.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caelia Quartula made this for Valerius Melippus her deserving father, and Caelia Saturnina [made this] for her most excellent husband.
EDCS-05601243.summary: This funerary monument records dedications by Caelia Quartula to her father Valerius Melippus and by Caelia Saturnina to her husband.

EDCS-05601245.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records a dedication to Philumene by Tius.

EDCS-05601249.text_edition: \n Filoromus Pantaracus Thorax Polystefanus Callimorfus Patinicus Limenius Euplium Caecilianus ficet sic\n
EDCS-05601249.translation: Filoromus, Pantaracus, Thorax, Polystefanus, Callimorfus, Patinicus, Limenius, Euplius, Caecilianus made this.
EDCS-05601249.summary: This inscription lists the names of multiple individuals who jointly commissioned or created a monument.

EDCS-05601250.text_edition: \n Apollini Augusto honori memoriaeque Luci Aemili Luci fili Quirina Celatiani Porcia Festa filii karissimi loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-05601250.translation: To Apollo Augustus, in honor and memory of Lucius Aemilius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, and Celatianus, and Porcia Festa, his dearest children, on land granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05601250.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Apollo Augustus, made in honor of Lucius Aemilius and his children, on land granted by local officials.

EDCS-05601251.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo vexillatio legionis VII geminae felicis sub cura Iuni Victoris legionis eiusdem ob natalem aquilae\n
EDCS-05601251.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, by the detachment of the Seventh Twin Legion, the Fortunate, under the care of Junius Victor of the same legion, on the birthday of the eagle.
EDCS-05601251.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by a military detachment of the Seventh Legion, celebrating the birthday of the legion's eagle standard, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05601252.translation: Norbanus(?), soldier of the Seventh Legion.
EDCS-05601252.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording the name and military affiliation of a soldier, possibly Norbanus, from the Seventh Legion.

EDCS-05601257.text_edition: \n Aulus Nunidius Auli libertus Salutaris Aulus Nunidius A(uli) l(ibertus) Montanus Valerio \n
EDCS-05601257.translation: Aulus Nunidius Salutaris, freedman of Aulus Nunidius, [also] Aulus Nunidius Montanus, freedman of Aulus, [dedicated this] to Valerius.
EDCS-05601257.summary: This inscription records the names of two freedmen, Aulus Nunidius Salutaris and Aulus Nunidius Montanus, who seem to be dedicating something to a Valerius, possibly a patron or official.

EDCS-05601258.text_edition: \n Caio Audieno Cai et |(Caiae) liberto Hilarioni puero probo Audiena Cai liberta Caritio mater tera faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-05601258.translation: Caritio, freedwoman of Gaius Audienus, mother, had this made for her good boy, Hilarion, freedman of Gaius Audienus and (Caia).
EDCS-05601258.summary: This monument was commissioned by Caritio, a freedwoman, for her son Hilarion, who was also a freedman, demonstrating familial devotion.

EDCS-05601259.text_edition: \n Cornelio filio Galeria Volteiano ex testamento Quintiae Fructae matris \n
EDCS-05601259.translation: To Cornelius, son of Quintia Fructa, his mother, in accordance with her will.
EDCS-05601259.summary: This inscription records a posthumous dedication or memorial made in honor of Cornelius, as stipulated in the will of his mother, Quintia Fructa.

EDCS-05601264.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records the name Maxima, possibly identified as a daughter.

EDCS-05601287.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesari Augusto Germanico imperatori\n
EDCS-05601287.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Emperor.
EDCS-05601287.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely set up during his reign (41-54 CE).

EDCS-05601289.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, pontiff, with tribunician power, proconsul twice, consul twice, father of the country, from Bracara, 27 miles.
EDCS-05601289.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording distances from Bracara, dedicated to Emperor Trajan Decius, who reigned from 249-251 CE.

EDCS-05601300.translation: [Emperor Caesar] Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, father of the country, pontiff maximus, with tribunician power for 18 years, consul for the third time, from Lugdunum (Lyons), 95 miles.
EDCS-05601300.summary: This milestone records a distance of 95 miles from Lugdunum, dedicated to Emperor Hadrian, dating to the period of his 18th tribunician power and third consulship (circa 134-135 CE).

EDCS-05601301.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the divine Nerva, Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, father of the country, pontiff maximus, with tribunician power for 18 years, consul for the third time, from Lugdunum (Lyons), 103 miles.
EDCS-05601301.summary: This milestone records a distance of 103 miles from Lugdunum, dedicated to Emperor Hadrian, including his full imperial titulature and dating to circa 134-135 CE.

EDCS-05601307.text_edition: \n Domino Nostro Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantio maximo Victori semper Augusto Augustobriga milia passuum\n
EDCS-05601307.translation: To Our Lord Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, greatest, victorious, ever Augustus, [dedication] of Augustobriga, [for] miles.
EDCS-05601307.summary: This inscription from Augustobriga is a dedication to Emperor Constantius, likely marking a road or a milestone, dated to his reign (mid-4th century CE).

EDCS-05601359.text_edition: \n Atta Abboiocum Rectugeni filia Luci uxor hic sita est sit tibi terra levis Lucius Nissicum Accuti filius hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-05601359.translation: Atta, daughter of Abboiocum and Rectugena, wife of Lucius, lies here. May the earth be light upon you. Lucius, son of Nissicum and Accutus, lies here. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-05601359.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of two individuals, Atta and Lucius, in a location likely in Hispania, with reciprocal wishes for peace in the afterlife, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601362.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Quadratus, son of Boddius, of the Vel(licum) tribe, aged 40. Malia, his wife, daughter of Magilo, erected this monument to Fulvius Pius and Pontius Proculus Pontianus.
EDCS-05601362.summary: This funerary epitaph records the erection of a monument by Malia for her husband Valerius Quadratus, son of Boddius, and also mentions Fulvius Pius and Pontius Proculus Pontianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601374.text_edition: \n Corneliae Aug-- uxori Dio Gai libertus\n
EDCS-05601374.translation: To Cornelia Aug[usta?], wife of Dio, his freedman.
EDCS-05601374.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication to Cornelia, wife of Dio, erected by his freedman, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601375.translation: To Hercules, Cornelius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-05601375.summary: This is a votive inscription to Hercules by Cornelius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601376.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemilio Flavo Eturicon missicio annorum LV Saturninus filius posuit sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-05601376.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aemilius Flavus, aged 55, missicius. His son Saturninus erected [this]. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-05601376.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Aemilius Flavus, described as 'missicius' (perhaps discharged from service or freed), and the erection of the monument by his son Saturninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601379.summary: This is a very fragmentary epitaph that likely records the name Clodia Urba and her age, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601382.text_edition: \n Pompeius cev annos hic situs est\n
EDCS-05601382.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph mentioning Pompeius and his age, recording his burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601383.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph mentioning Nnius and his age, concluding with a common farewell formula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05601386.text_edition: \n Quinto Iulio filio Galeria? Nigellioni aedili IIviro flamini Properato fratri Maximae matri Pompeia Gnaei liberta Glene et sibi\n
EDCS-05601386.translation: To Quintus Iulius, son, Galeria? Nigellio, aedile, duovir, flamen, to Properatus, brother, to Maxima, mother, Pompeia, Gnaeus' freedwoman, and to herself.
EDCS-05601386.summary: This complex dedication records a monument erected by Pompeia, a freedwoman, for her son Quintus Iulius, and also honours other family members and civic/religious officials, likely from a Roman provincial town in the Imperial period.

EDCS-05601387.text_edition: \n Lucius Porcius Nepos hic situs est annorum XXVII Porcia Luci filia Serana mater\n
EDCS-05601387.translation: Lucius Porcius Nepos rests here, aged 27. His mother Porcia, daughter of Lucius, placed this.
EDCS-05601387.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Porcius Nepos, dated to the Roman Imperial period, commemorating a young man and erected by his mother.

EDCS-05601389.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Drusi filius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII imperator VIII consul III pater patriae CCXXXVIII\n
EDCS-05601389.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fourth time, imperator eight times, consul three times, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-05601389.summary: A formal titulature inscription, likely from a public monument, for Emperor Claudius, detailing his numerous honors and offices around 44-45 CE.

EDCS-05601405.translation: To the departed spirits. Sera[--] is placed here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601405.summary: A fragment of a funerary epitaph, likely Roman Imperial period, invoking the gods of the underworld for the deceased named Sera[--].

EDCS-05601416.translation: To the Brigantian Mothers. Lucius Aelius Phainus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-05601416.summary: A votive inscription from the Roman Imperial period dedicated by Lucius Aelius Phainus to the Matres Brigeacis, likely a local or Celtic mother goddess cult.

EDCS-05601417.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Octavius Marcellus, son of Aemilianus, aged [3]3 years. Caecilia/[--] [placed this].
EDCS-05601417.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph, Roman Imperial period, for Lucius Octavius Marcellus, indicating he died at 33 years old and was likely commemorated by Caecilia.

EDCS-05601418.translation: To the departed spirits. Primitivus, slave of Sempronia Paternus, aged 12. Martialis, aged 40. Firmina, aged 9. Dorcas. They are buried here, to a companion, son, and daughter.
EDCS-05601418.summary: A communal funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period for several individuals, including slaves and family members, placed in a shared tomb.

EDCS-05601420.translation: To the departed spirits. Marciana and Nepotila placed this monument for their father Marcus Licinius Nepos, aged 50.
EDCS-05601420.summary: A funerary epitaph from the Roman Imperial period where daughters Marciana and Nepotila commemorate their father, Marcus Licinius Nepos.

EDCS-05601422.translation: To the departed spirits. Auga placed this stone for her son Sempronius, aged [--] years.
EDCS-05601422.summary: A funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period where a mother, Auga, commemorates her son, Sempronius.

EDCS-05601423.translation: Monument to the departed spirits. Saturninus placed this for himself, aged 60, and for Maurila, aged 50, and for his daughter Mauricina, aged 20.
EDCS-05601423.summary: A funerary monument inscription from the Roman Imperial period where Saturninus made provision for himself and his wife Maurila and daughter Mauricina.

EDCS-05601424.translation: To the departed spirits. Cornelia Materna placed this monument for her daughter Flavina, aged 25, on the Ides of March. [Also:] To the departed spirits. Cornelia Materna placed this stone in memory of herself, living, aged 44, on the Nones of July.
EDCS-05601424.summary: A double funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period where Cornelia Materna first commemorates her deceased daughter Flavina and later records a monument she erected for herself while alive.

EDCS-05601425.translation: To Pin... his wife, aged 56. I, Vid..., aged 50, set this up.
EDCS-05601425.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commemorating two individuals, one of whom, the wife, died at 56, and the other, who set up the monument, died at 50.

EDCS-05601427.translation: To the Divine Manes. Avita, seeing a vision and consulting, made this for her husband Hyginus.
EDCS-05601427.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument set up by Avita for her husband Hyginus, who was apparently prompted by a divine vision.

EDCS-05601436.translation: To Atthis, his fellow soldier. Anencletus set this up for Atthis, his fellow soldier, and for his Aemilia Dercinio with her Atthis. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601436.summary: This funerary epitaph records Anencletus setting up a monument for his contubernalis (a close companion, often sharing quarters, sometimes implying a relationship akin to marriage) Atthis, and also for Aemilia Dercinio and her Atthis.

EDCS-05601437.translation: To the Nymphs. Hilarus and Fuscus of Laxta [made this?] from their own peculium.
EDCS-05601437.summary: This inscription records Hilarus and Fuscus from Laxta dedicating something to the Nymphs from their private funds.

EDCS-05601438.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Crescens, son, to his excellent mother Vettiena Acta. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05601438.summary: This funerary epitaph records Crescens setting up a monument for his mother, Vettiena Acta.

EDCS-05601443.translation: To Diana, the Mistress. Cornelius Iulianus [in fulfillment] of a vow.
EDCS-05601443.summary: This inscription records Cornelius Iulianus dedicating something to the goddess Diana as fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-05601448.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXXV imperatori XX consuli XIII\n
EDCS-05601448.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 35th time, Imperator for the 20th time, Consul for the 13th time.
EDCS-05601448.summary: This is an official inscription recording the titles and honors of Emperor Augustus, likely associated with a public building or monument, dated to his 35th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-05601449.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Karino Pio Felici Augusto \n
EDCS-05601449.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carinus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-05601449.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Carinus, listing his imperial titles, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-05601451.translation: To our Lord Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesar, pious and fortunate Augustus [Caesar...] [III...] [??] Caesar [??] [??] [??] [??]IXE[??]R[??] [??]VO [??] of the prince [??] Pontifex Maximus [??] proconsul.
EDCS-05601451.summary: This incomplete inscription is a dedication to Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, listing his titles and offices, likely from a public monument and dating to his time as Caesar and later Augustus.

EDCS-05601452.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Galerio Valerio Maximiano nobilissimo Caesari \n
EDCS-05601452.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-05601452.summary: This is an official inscription dedicating something to Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, highlighting his title as most noble Caesar.

EDCS-06100374.text_edition: \n Apollini Augusto Sempronius Carus Silvini filius Lucretius Martialis Lucreti filius aediles de sua pecunia faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-06100374.translation: Sempronius Carus, son of Silvinus, and Lucretius Martialis, son of Lucretius, as aediles, took care to have this made at their own expense for Apollo Augustus.
EDCS-06100374.summary: This inscription records the construction of something at their own expense by two aediles, Sempronius Carus and Lucretius Martialis, dedicated to Apollo Augustus.

EDCS-06100385.text_edition: \n Cornelia Masueta nnm XXV Iulia Utina annorum L Lucius Coelius Notus ucsori sic posuit et socrii sic\n
EDCS-06100385.translation: Cornelia Masueta, aged 25, and Iulia Utina, aged 50. Lucius Coelius Notus erected this for his wife and in-laws.
EDCS-06100385.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by Lucius Coelius Notus for his wife, Cornelia Masueta, and his in-laws, including Iulia Utina.

EDCS-06400001.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Cornelius Luci filius Galeria Cinna IIvir murum longum pedes XC? --- ex decreto decurionum faciendum curavit idemque probavit\n
EDCS-06400001.translation: Gnaeus Cornelius Cinna, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, twice chief magistrate, had a wall 90 feet long made according to a decree of the council of decurions and approved it himself.
EDCS-06400001.summary: This inscription records the construction of a 90-foot wall, commissioned and approved by the twice chief magistrate Gnaeus Cornelius Cinna, following a decree by the decurions.

EDCS-06400005.text_edition: \n Lucius Iunius Luci filius Titi nepos Paetus Fortunae sacrum dedit\n
EDCS-06400005.translation: Lucius Iunius Paetus, son of Lucius, grandson of Titus, dedicated this sacred object to Fortuna.
EDCS-06400005.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred object to Fortuna by Lucius Iunius Paetus.

EDCS-06400006.text_edition: \n Cai Caesaris Augusti filii pontificis consulis designati principis iuventutis Lucius Iunius Luci filius Titi nepos Paetus sac(rum) dedit\n
EDCS-06400006.translation: Lucius Iunius Paetus, son of Lucius, grandson of Titus, son of the divine Caesar Augustus, pontiff, consul-designate, prince of the youth, dedicated this sacred object.
EDCS-06400006.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred object by Lucius Iunius Paetus, who lists an extensive set of honorific titles, including being the son of the divine Caesar Augustus.

EDCS-06400018.summary: This very short inscription likely records a dedication or ownership by Primilla, daughter of Publius.

EDCS-06400019.text_edition: \n Genio castelli Caius Voconius Phoebus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-06400019.translation: To the genius of the fort, Caius Voconius Phoebus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-06400019.summary: This inscription records Caius Voconius Phoebus fulfilling a vow to the genius of the fort.

EDCS-06400020.text_edition: \n Marcus Brosius Marci filius Sarapi dono dedit\n
EDCS-06400020.translation: Marcus Brosius, son of Marcus, gave this as a gift to Sarapis.
EDCS-06400020.summary: This inscription records Marcus Brosius, son of Marcus, donating an object to the god Sarapis.

EDCS-06400021.text_edition: \n Titus Hermes ISarapi et Isi in suo mansionem donavit libens merito de sua pecunia\n
EDCS-06400021.translation: Titus Hermes willingly and deservedly donated his mansion to Sarapis and Isis at his own expense.
EDCS-06400021.summary: This inscription records Titus Hermes donating his mansion to the gods Sarapis and Isis at his own expense.

EDCS-06400023.text_edition: \n Herius Cai filius Hispa legatus pro praetore pr \n
EDCS-06400023.translation: Herius Hispa, son of Caius, governor with the rank of praetor...
EDCS-06400023.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording Herius Hispa, son of Caius, in his capacity as a legate with the rank of praetor.

EDCS-06400025.text_edition: \n Lucio Numisio Cnaei filio Sergia Laeto aedili IIviro et IIviro quinquennali flamini Augustorum pontifici praefecto cohortis Musulamiorum flamini provinciae Hispaniae citerioris bis decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-06400025.translation: To Lucius Numisius Laetus, son of Gnaeus, twice aedile, twice duovir, quinquennial duovir, priest of the Emperors, prefect of the Musulamian cohort, twice provincial priest of the Nearer Spain by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-06400025.summary: This inscription records the extensive career of Lucius Numisius Laetus, likely on a public monument in Hispania Citerior.

EDCS-06400027.summary: This inscription is a simple dedication or memorial to Caesius, son of Caius.

EDCS-06400028.text_edition: \n Lucius Nosteius Luci libertus Servenus salve\n
EDCS-06400028.translation: Lucius Nosteius Servenus, freedman of Lucius, greetings.
EDCS-06400028.summary: This inscription is a simple greeting, likely part of a funerary or personal marker, for Lucius Nosteius Servenus.

EDCS-06400029.text_edition: \n Numerius Paquius Numerii libertus Diphilus monumentum fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-06400029.translation: Numerius Paquius Diphilus, freedman of Numerius, made this monument for himself and his family.
EDCS-06400029.summary: This inscription records the erection of a family tomb by Numerius Paquius Diphilus.

EDCS-06400030.text_edition: \n Annia Spuri filia Salvia hic sita est\n
EDCS-06400030.translation: Annia Salvia, daughter of Spurius, is buried here.
EDCS-06400030.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Annia Salvia.

EDCS-06400031.text_edition: \n Caius Antonius Publi filius Collina Balbus aedilis viveis parentibus\n
EDCS-06400031.translation: Gaius Antonius Balbus, son of Publius, of the Collina tribe, aedile, during his parents' lifetime.
EDCS-06400031.summary: This inscription records the public office of Gaius Antonius Balbus, likely on a monument dedicated while his parents were alive.

EDCS-06400033.text_edition: \n Caio Laelio Cai liberto Chresto Antonia Iucunda\n
EDCS-06400033.translation: To Gaius Laelius Chrestus, freedman of Gaius, from Antonia Iucunda.
EDCS-06400033.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Antonia Iucunda to her freedman Gaius Laelius Chrestus.

EDCS-06400034.translation: To Lucius Laetilius Priamus, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-06400034.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Laetilius Priamus, freedman of Marcus.

EDCS-06400035.translation: To Laetilia Martha, freedwoman of Marcus.
EDCS-06400035.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Laetilia Martha, freedwoman of Marcus.

EDCS-06400036.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Numisio Cn(aei) liberto Apro R\n
EDCS-06400036.translation: To Gnaeus Numisius Aper, freedman of Gnaeus.
EDCS-06400036.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gnaeus Numisius Aper, freedman of Gnaeus.

EDCS-06400037.text_edition: \n Lucius Subrius La IIIIIIvir Augustalis colonia Iulia Paterna Arelatis et Subria Luci liberta DA\n
EDCS-06400037.translation: Lucius Subrius, Augustalis, fourth member of the college of priests of the Julian Colony of Arelate, and Subria, freedwoman of Lucius, greet you.
EDCS-06400037.summary: This inscription records the greeting of Lucius Subrius, an Augustalis and member of a priestly college, and his freedwoman Subria, from the colony of Arelate.

EDCS-06400038.translation: Clodia Macaria, daughter of Caius, greet you.
EDCS-06400038.summary: This inscription is a simple greeting from Clodia Macaria, daughter of Caius.

EDCS-06400039.translation: To Lucius Fulvius Demosthenes, freedman of a woman.
EDCS-06400039.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Fulvius Demosthenes, a freedman, likely for a tomb.

EDCS-06400040.text_edition: \n Lucius Labicius |(mulieris) libertus Malcio Labicia |(mulieris) liberta Maura Labicia |(mulieris) liberta Scutia\n
EDCS-06400040.translation: Lucius Labicius Malcio, freedman of a woman; Labicia Maura, freedwoman of a woman; Scutia, freedwoman of a woman.
EDCS-06400040.summary: This inscription lists three individuals, Lucius Labicius Malcio, Labicia Maura, and Scutia, all freedpersons of women, likely associated with a funerary context.

EDCS-06400043.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Numisius Cnaei libertus Epigonus heic sepultus est ave salve\n
EDCS-06400043.translation: Gnaeus Numisius Epigonus, freedman of Gnaeus, is buried here; hail and farewell.
EDCS-06400043.summary: This inscription records the burial of Gnaeus Numisius Epigonus, a freedman, and includes a greeting and farewell.

EDCS-06400044.text_edition: \n Argentaria Cai filia Faustilla octavos cum iterum aetatis mihi curreret annus rapta conplexu sic coniugis hic iaceo cura quibus ius fasque fuit solacia mortis haec habeo vitae praemia quae tuleram hunc mihi defunctae monimenti inpendit honorem mater et incuras ipsa Nigella suas\n
EDCS-06400044.translation: When my eighth year of life was running again, snatched away from my husband's embrace, I lie here; I had the right and sacred duty to care for these, the consolations of death, the rewards of life which I had obtained; my mother Nigella, bearing her own cares, has bestowed upon me, the deceased, this honor of a monument.
EDCS-06400044.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Argentaria Faustilla, daughter of Caius, and commemorates her mother Nigella's efforts in erecting the monument.

EDCS-06400045.text_edition: \n Copiolae Fulviniae Rufillae servae Daphnus tonsor contubernali\n
EDCS-06400045.translation: To Daphnus the barber, companion of Copiola Fulvinia Rufilla, a slave woman.
EDCS-06400045.summary: This inscription dedicates a monument to Daphnus the barber, who was the companion (contubernalis) of Copiola Fulvinia Rufilla, a slave.

EDCS-06400047.text_edition: \n Quinti filius Gnaei nepos augur IIvir quinqennalis? \n
EDCS-06400047.translation: Son of Quintus, grandson of Gnaeus, augur, duovir for five years?
EDCS-06400047.summary: This inscription records the titles and lineage of an individual, possibly an augur and quinquennial duovir, whose name is missing.

EDCS-06400048.text_edition: \n Lucio Magio Gnaei filio Fabia Sabello aedili IIviro\n
EDCS-06400048.translation: To Lucius Magius Sabellus, son of Gnaeus, of the Fabia tribe, aedile, duovir.
EDCS-06400048.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Magius Sabellus, son of Gnaeus, honoring his public offices as aedile and duovir.

EDCS-06400051.text_edition: \n ila salve et vale salve ob monumentum visitum venis vale\n
EDCS-06400051.translation: [...ila] hail and farewell, hail and farewell, because you came to visit the monument.
EDCS-06400051.summary: This inscription appears to be a tomb inscription with a greeting and farewell directed at a visitor who came to see the monument.

EDCS-06400053.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurunculeia, a freedwoman, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400054.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for Bacchis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400055.text_edition: \n Aulus Baebius Antiochus Hilarus libertus fecit\n
EDCS-06400055.translation: Aulus Baebius Antiochus Hilarus, freedman, made [this].
EDCS-06400055.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Aulus Baebius Antiochus Hilarus, a freedman, erected the monument, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400056.text_edition: \n Cervae coniugi bene meritei sic sacravit maritus \n
EDCS-06400056.translation: To Cervia, his well-deserving wife, her husband dedicated [this monument].
EDCS-06400056.summary: This funerary epitaph records a husband dedicating a monument to his deserving wife, Cervia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400057.text_edition: \n Cornelia Anica Alabensis hic sita est\n
EDCS-06400057.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Anica Alabensis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400059.translation: Of Lucius Fabricius Lenaeus, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-06400059.summary: This is a funerary epitaph indicating ownership or dedication by Lucius Fabricius Lenaeus, a freedman, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400060.text_edition: \n Marcus Messius Marci libertus Samalo faber lapidarius\n
EDCS-06400060.translation: Marcus Messius Samalo, freedman of Marcus, stonemason.
EDCS-06400060.summary: This funerary epitaph identifies Marcus Messius Samalo, a freedman and stonemason, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400061.text_edition: \n Marcus Marci libertus Nicephor pistor hic situs est\n
EDCS-06400061.translation: Marcus Nicephor, freedman of Marcus, baker, lies here.
EDCS-06400061.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Marcus Nicephor, a freedman and baker, lies here, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400063.text_edition: \n Pompeia Nereis annorum XX hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-06400063.translation: Pompeia Nereis, aged 20, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-06400063.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Pompeia Nereis at the age of 20, with a common wish for a light burial, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400065.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Publicius Heraclida annorum V hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-06400065.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Publicius Heraclida, aged 5, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-06400065.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to the departed spirits and records the death of Quintus Publicius Heraclida at the age of 5, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-06400066.translation: Seia Anicula Probata made this for her patroness.
EDCS-06400066.summary: This epitaph records Seia Anicula Probata dedicating a monument to her patroness, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-06400067.text_edition: \n Sicinia Cai filia Secunda filia cum matre est hospes sei forte requiris heic sita quas rapuit mortis acerva dies sed prius eripuit matri qui in omnia pollet crudelis cassus filiolam e manibus paene inmatura morte ereptam sibi gnatan heu quantum mater fleverit indiciost nam postquam fletu et monumento hoc condecoravit gnatam per luctus reddidit ipsa animam\n
EDCS-06400067.translation: Siciencia Secunda, daughter of Caius, is buried here with her mother, if you seek her. Death, cruel and overwhelming, snatched her away, but first tore her from her mother's hands, this child who was so capable in all things. Alas, how much the mother wept is evident, for after she adorned her daughter with tears and this monument, she herself, through grief, gave up her soul, returning her daughter to the tomb.
EDCS-06400067.summary: This poignant epitaph records the tragic death of Sicinia Secunda, who died before her mother, and whose mother died shortly after, consumed by grief.

EDCS-06400068.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying Sicinia as the mother of Quintus, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-06400069.text_edition: \n Annius Silvester miles legionis VII geminae felicis hic situs est annorum XXX curante Clodio Crescente municipe suo\n
EDCS-06400069.translation: Annius Silvester, a soldier of the 7th Gemina Felix legion, lies here. He was 30 years old. This was arranged by his fellow townsman, Clodius Crescens.
EDCS-06400069.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Annius Silvester, a soldier of the Legio VII Gemina Felix, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-06400071.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription simply naming Lucius Turpilius Statius Maior, likely indicating a tomb or memorial.

EDCS-06400074.text_edition: \n Lucius Vinuleius Luci libertus Philogenes ave vale\n
EDCS-06400074.translation: Lucius Vinuleius Philogenes, freedman of Lucius. Greetings, farewell.
EDCS-06400074.summary: This is a short epitaph for Lucius Vinuleius Philogenes, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06400079.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph identifying a person as the son of Mutus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06400093.summary: This is a very brief inscription simply stating the name Gaius Numisius, possibly a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-06400095.text_edition: \n Caio Sediatoni Publi filio Rufo aedili liberti et libertae heredes domistici\n
EDCS-06400095.translation: To Gaius Sediatonius Rufus, son of Publius, formerly aedile, from his freedmen and freedwomen, and his household heirs.
EDCS-06400095.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the freedmen, freedwomen, and household heirs to Gaius Sediatonius Rufus, who held the office of aedile, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06400097.text_edition: \n Quintus Lusius Luci filius Senica mollem robusteis nondum formata iuventus aetatem Lusi viribus induerat cum carae exoptans complexum saepe sororis multa viae dum volt millia conficere caeditur infesto concursu forte latronum sic rapit hoc clades corpus acerba nimis illa aetas credo hoc tribuit tempore mortis ut bona non neminit seic mala ne timeat\n
EDCS-06400097.translation: Quintus Lusius Senica, son of Lucius, in the prime of his youth, had not yet fully acquired the strength of a man when, longing for the embrace of his dear sister, he was killed while trying to cover many miles on the road by a sudden, hostile attack of brigands. Thus this too-bitter disaster took his body. I believe this time of death grants this: that he may not remember evils, so he need not fear evils.
EDCS-06400097.summary: This epitaph laments the death of Quintus Lusius Senica, a young man killed by brigands while traveling, and reflects on the nature of death.

EDCS-06900004.translation: Tangini, daughter of Coponilla, has put this up.
EDCS-06900004.summary: This is a dedication from Tangini, daughter of Coponilla, possibly on a tombstone or a small monument.

EDCS-06900009.text_edition: \n Rufus miles legionis X geminae fecit\n
EDCS-06900009.translation: Rufus, a soldier of the Tenth Gemina Legion, made this.
EDCS-06900009.summary: This is a dedication by Rufus, a soldier of the Tenth Gemina Legion, likely for a tomb or a building.

EDCS-06900014.text_edition: \n Doviderus Amparami filius princeps Cantabrorum hic situs est Deobrigi filii posuerunt monumentum\n
EDCS-06900014.translation: Doviderus, son of Amparamus, chief of the Cantabrians, is buried here; his son Deobrigi erected the monument.
EDCS-06900014.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Doviderus, a chief of the Cantabrians, with his son Deobrigi as the dedicator.

EDCS-06900016.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Silvius pater Ursicino filio posuit annorum XXVIIII\n
EDCS-06900016.translation: To the spirits of the departed and in memory. Silvius erected this for his son Ursicinus, aged 29.
EDCS-06900016.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Silvius to his son Ursicinus, who died at the age of 29.

EDCS-06900017.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sulpicius Trophimus Maternae Lete uxori carissim(a)e adque pientissim[ae ------\n
EDCS-06900017.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sulpicius Trophimus erected this for his dearest and most dutiful wife, Materna Lete, [---]
EDCS-06900017.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Sulpicius Trophimus for his wife Materna Lete, with a lacuna indicating missing information.

EDCS-06900059.translation: Sempronius Maternus, a veteran, an altar
EDCS-06900059.summary: This is an incomplete inscription indicating that Sempronius Maternus, a veteran, dedicated an altar.

EDCS-06900060.text_edition: \n RN sacrum Lucius Cornelius Paternus Palax institui libens\n
EDCS-06900060.translation: Sacred to the gods. Lucius Cornelius Paternus Palax willingly set this up.
EDCS-06900060.summary: This is a dedication, likely of a religious object or structure, by Lucius Cornelius Paternus Palax.

EDCS-06900061.text_edition: \n Ego non cesso currere Gaius Valerius Verdullus vessttigat canes\n
EDCS-06900061.translation: I do not stop running. Gaius Valerius Verdullus, the dogs are chasing.
EDCS-06900061.summary: This is a humorous or boastful inscription by Gaius Valerius Verdullus, possibly related to hunting or athletic competition.

EDCS-06900065.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Gaius Valerius Verdullus as a painter.

EDCS-06900067.text_edition: \n Sempronius Nigrinus Agirsaris filius annorum XL hic situs est Aemilia Alla marito suo faciendum curavit sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-06900067.translation: Sempronius Nigrinus, son of Agirsaris, aged 40, is buried here. His wife Aemilia Alla arranged for this to be made for her husband. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-06900067.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sempronius Nigrinus, erected by his wife Aemilia Alla, with a wish for a peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-06900069.text_edition: \n Mandius Galeria Letondonis Mandicus hic situs est\n
EDCS-06900069.translation: Mandius, son of Letondon, called Mandicus, is buried here.
EDCS-06900069.summary: This funerary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, records the burial of Mandius, son of Letondon, also known as Mandicus.

EDCS-06900070.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Memmio Vettiano annorum XIX filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-06900070.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Memmius Vettianus, aged 19, his most dutiful son.
EDCS-06900070.summary: This funerary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, is dedicated to Lucius Memmius Vettianus, aged 19, by his parents.

EDCS-06900071.translation: Nicetius, a Christian, has been restored. [Text incomplete]
EDCS-06900071.summary: This very fragmentary inscription, likely from late antiquity, mentions Nicetius and a Christian context.

EDCS-07000624.text_edition: \n Sorex Cluniensis annorum XXX hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-07000624.translation: Sorex of Cluny, aged 30, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-07000624.summary: This funerary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, records the burial of Sorex of Cluny, aged 30.

EDCS-07000627.text_edition: \n Titus Antonius Aper domo Nemauso annorum XL hic situs est\n
EDCS-07000627.translation: Titus Antonius Aper, from Nemausus, aged 40, is buried here.
EDCS-07000627.summary: This funerary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, records the burial of Titus Antonius Aper, aged 40, from Nemausus (modern Nîmes).

EDCS-07000628.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus monumentum posuit Cornelius Maternus cives Vadiniensis ex praecepto eorum conviventium Bodero Sdublegino heres amico suo plus minus annorum XLV\n
EDCS-07000628.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cornelius Maternus, a citizen of Vadiniensis, erected this monument in accordance with the instructions of the deceased convival friends, for his friend Bodero Sdublegino, his heir, aged about 45.
EDCS-07000628.summary: This funerary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, records the erection of a monument by Cornelius Maternus for his friend and heir Bodero Sdublegino, aged about 45, who was a citizen of Vadiniensis.

EDCS-07000634.text_edition: \n Matribus Titus Racilius Valerianus ex voto\n
EDCS-07000634.translation: To the Mothers. Titus Racilius Valerianus, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-07000634.summary: This votive inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, records Titus Racilius Valerianus fulfilling a vow to the Mothers (likely Matronae).

EDCS-07000638.summary: This very brief inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, simply records the name Marcus Aemilius Carancus, possibly a tomb marker.

EDCS-07000647.translation: [He/She/They] made [this] for the freedman patron, [name missing].
EDCS-07000647.summary: This fragmentary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, appears to record a dedication made by someone to their patron.

EDCS-07000649.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesiae parentis pientissimae et sibi annorum XX\n
EDCS-07000649.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Caesia, [his/her] most dutiful mother, and for himself, aged 20.
EDCS-07000649.summary: This funerary inscription, likely Roman Imperial period, is dedicated to Caesia, a mother, and to the person dedicating it, who was aged 20.

EDCS-07000652.text_edition: \n Tito Cantio Nasonis filio militi Othoniano\n
EDCS-07000652.translation: To Titus Cantius, son of Nason, a soldier of the Othonian cohort.
EDCS-07000652.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Cantius, a soldier, dating to the 1st century AD.

EDCS-07000653.translation: To Minia Rufina, daughter of a veteran from Emerita.
EDCS-07000653.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Minia Rufina, daughter of a veteran, likely from the Roman province of Lusitania.

EDCS-07000658.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Pullus to his mother, Septumia.

EDCS-07000664.translation: To Terentius Reburrus. To the gods, for eternity.
EDCS-07000664.summary: This is a brief epitaph for Terentius Reburrus, possibly a dedication or memorial.

EDCS-07000667.text_edition: \n Lucio Valerio Severo Valeria Pr heres ob merita\n
EDCS-07000667.translation: To Lucius Valerius Severus. Valeria Pr, his heir, [erected this] for his merits.
EDCS-07000667.summary: This inscription records a memorial erected by Valeria Pr, heir of Lucius Valerius Severus, likely honoring his achievements.

EDCS-07000669.text_edition: \n Caio Vitio Ligiricon Viti f(ilio) Caio Saldi Clouter Toutius Trebaque B\n
EDCS-07000669.translation: To Gaius Vitii Ligiricon, son of Vitius. Gaius Saldi Clouter from Trebaque [erected this].
EDCS-07000669.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Gaius Vitii Ligiricon by Gaius Saldi Clouter from Trebaque.

EDCS-07000670.text_edition: \n Lucius Assellius Niger veteranus Aniensis Caesaraugustanus hic situs est Publius Caninius et Optata Publi Canini liberta de sua pecunia faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-07000670.translation: Lucius Assellius Niger, a veteran from Aniensis and Caesaraugusta, lies here. Publius Caninius and his freedwoman Optata, daughter of Publius Caninius, had this constructed with their own money.
EDCS-07000670.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Assellius Niger, a veteran, and details the commission of his tomb by Publius Caninius and his freedwoman Optata.

EDCS-07000686.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulio Euhodio annorum LXXIII pio in suis \n
EDCS-07000686.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. To Eulogius, aged 73, dutiful to his family.
EDCS-07000686.summary: This funerary inscription honors Eulogius, aged 73, describing him as pious towards his family.

EDCS-07000687.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fideli defuncto nnorum LXXXIII ANATI hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-07000687.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. To Fidelis, deceased, aged 83. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-07000687.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Fidelis, aged 83, with a wish for a light earth.

EDCS-07000688.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Candida Caecinae Severi serva hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-07000688.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Candida, slave of Caecina Severus, lies here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-07000688.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Candida, a slave of Caecina Severus, with a wish for a light earth.

EDCS-07000690.summary: This inscription records ownership by Quintus Aelius Satullus, likely on a tomb or a similar marker.

EDCS-07000691.summary: This inscription indicates ownership by Titus Annius Berus, possibly on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-07000692.summary: This inscription records ownership by Publius Caecilius Popillus, likely on a tomb or monument.
