EDCS-24500948.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Placida, mentioning her great-grandfather and Thallusa, possibly a fellow soldier.

EDCS-24500951.summary: A fragmentary inscription that likely records a dedication or ownership, possibly by Gaius.

EDCS-24500953.summary: This inscription records the name of Gaius Egnatius Marcus Mamilius, likely part of a funerary or commemorative monument, with the end of the name lost.

EDCS-24500957.summary: This inscription fragment names Octavius, potentially part of a longer dedication or funerary text, with significant portions missing.

EDCS-24500960.summary: This very fragmentary inscription begins with a potential praenomen 'Gaius' followed by an initial 'V', suggesting it's part of a name or title on a monument.

EDCS-24500964.text_edition: \n ------ S]etuleio Luci liberto? OCE viro SI\n
EDCS-24500964.translation: Setuleius, freedman of Lucius? OCE, a man [restored details]
EDCS-24500964.summary: This inscription fragment seems to record Setuleius, identified as the freedman of Lucius, with the end of the text describing him lost.

EDCS-24500984.summary: This brief inscription is a standard funerary marker indicating that a person is buried in this location.

EDCS-24600555.translation: Dovidona, daughter of Dovi, willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Hercules.
EDCS-24600555.summary: This dedication records Dovidona fulfilling a vow to the god Hercules, likely on a statue or altar dedicated to him.

EDCS-24600561.translation: Callistrate, most faithful, live in God the Lord. Flavius Profuturus? bought [you].
EDCS-24600561.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Callistrate, purchased by Flavius Profuturus, and reflects a Christian context.

EDCS-24600577.summary: This inscription records the name Talavi and her age, likely a child's tombstone, and possibly indicating a connection to Caesar.

EDCS-24601049.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XXI consul XV censor perpetuus pater patriae \n
EDCS-24601049.translation: Imperator Caesar, son of the deified Vespasian, Domitian Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, acclaimed imperator for the 21st time, consul for the 15th time, perpetual censor, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-24601049.summary: This is a full imperial titulature of Emperor Domitian, listing his numerous titles and honors, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-24700281.text_edition: \n Sextus Numisius locum sanctum Laribus et signa et aram faciund coiravit eisdem dedicavit\n
EDCS-24700281.translation: Sextus Numisius arranged for a sacred place, statues, and an altar to be made for the household gods and dedicated them to them.
EDCS-24700281.summary: This inscription records Sextus Numisius commissioning and dedicating a sacred space, statues, and an altar for the Lares (household gods).

EDCS-24700282.text_edition: \n Clodia Luci liberta Optata patri patrono parentibus viro suo plaquit sic\n
EDCS-24700282.translation: Clodia Optata, freedwoman of Lucius, dedicated this to her father, patron, parents, and her husband.
EDCS-24700282.summary: This epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records a dedication made by a freedwoman to her family members.

EDCS-24700283.summary: This is a simple epitaph from the Roman Imperial period, possibly for a freedwoman named Marcia.

EDCS-24700284.text_edition: \n Quem pietas coluit heic est situs Pontilienus Luci progeniem Publium habes Acheruns ereptum e manibus maiorum luctibus summis quem pudor inginiumque frequens decorabat in aevo puerili florens ut foret ante alios hunc natura potens luctu lacrumeisque levavit at productores omnibus heis honerat\n
EDCS-24700284.translation: Here lies the one whom piety cherished, Pontilienus Lucius. You have Acheron, snatched from the hands of our ancestors, a source of the greatest grief; whom modesty and frequent talent adorned in his youthful years, so that he might be before others. Powerful nature has eased him with grief and tears, but his producers honor him with all these things.
EDCS-24700284.summary: This epitaph from the Roman Imperial period laments the death of Pontilienus Lucius, praising his youthful virtues and the grief of his parents.

EDCS-24700285.text_edition: \n Septumia mulieris liberta Scutia heic sitas viva plaquit salve et vale\n
EDCS-24700285.translation: Scutia, freedwoman of Septumia, lies here. Having set this up while alive, greetings and farewell.
EDCS-24700285.summary: This epitaph from the Roman Imperial period records Scutia, a freedwoman, setting up her own tomb while alive.

EDCS-24700286.text_edition: \n Vinuleia Luci liberta Calena heic sitast filia ut potuit non ut voluit\n
EDCS-24700286.translation: Calena, freedwoman of Lucius Vinuleius, lies here, as a daughter could, not as she wished.
EDCS-24700286.summary: This epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records the death of Calena, a freedwoman, possibly indicating a difficult or untimely end.

EDCS-25200767.text_edition: \n Maecius Cai filius Vetus augur aedilis murum pedes LX faciendum curavit idemque probavit\n
EDCS-25200767.translation: Caius Maecius Vetus, son of Caius, an augur and aedile, saw to the construction of 60 feet of wall and approved it himself.
EDCS-25200767.summary: This inscription from the Roman Republic or early Empire records a public works project undertaken by Maecius Vetus, an augur and aedile.

EDCS-25600530.summary: This very short inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, simply records the name Venitius Augustus.

EDCS-26400601.text_edition: \n Marcus Valerius Marci filius Marcellus annorum XXVIII titulum fecit\n
EDCS-26400601.translation: Marcus Valerius Marcellus, son of Marcus, 28 years old, made this monument.
EDCS-26400601.summary: This epitaph from the Roman Imperial period records the name, age, and maker of Marcus Valerius Marcellus.

EDCS-26400604.text_edition: \n Atilia Eup aram libens animo posuit ex votum sic\n
EDCS-26400604.translation: Atilia Eupa willingly set up this altar out of a vow.
EDCS-26400604.summary: This inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records Atilia Eupa setting up a votive altar.

EDCS-26400635.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Vibio Treboniano Gallo Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II patri patriae consuli II proconsuli et Imperatori Caesari Vibio Afenio Volusiano Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli \n
EDCS-26400635.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the fatherland, consul for the second time, proconsul; and to the Emperor Caesar Vibius Afinius Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul.
EDCS-26400635.summary: This extensive inscription from the mid-3rd century AD honors the emperors Trebonianus Gallus and his co-emperor Volusianus, listing their titles and honors.

EDCS-26400636.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae \n
EDCS-26400636.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-26400636.summary: This is a formal titulary inscription of Emperor Hadrian, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-26400639.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus sacrum Messenia Euphrate an[norum ------\n
EDCS-26400639.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Messenia Euphrate, [aged] ... years.
EDCS-26400639.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Messenia Euphrate, a woman whose age is lost.

EDCS-27800029.text_edition: \n Deo Iovi Iulius votum fecit animo libens\n
EDCS-27800029.translation: Iulius gladly made a vow to Jupiter.
EDCS-27800029.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a vow made by Iulius to the god Jupiter.

EDCS-27800030.text_edition: \n Laribus Vialibus sacrum Iulius Gaianus votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-27800030.translation: Sacred to the Household Gods. Iulius Gaianus gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27800030.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Iulius Gaianus to the household gods.

EDCS-28400089.translation: A funeral monument for Dessuaeona and Nemaios.
EDCS-28400089.summary: This is a very brief epitaph mentioning the deceased Dessuaeona and Nemaios.

EDCS-28400443.text_edition: \n Sexto Pompeio Sexto Appuleio consulibus Kalendis Augustis Caraegius et Abuanus et Caelio magistratus et senatus Maggavienses Amparamum Nemaiecanum Cusaburensim sic civitate honoraria donatus sic libertos posterosque ita vota omnia ei fecerunt finibus Maggaviensium quae civi Maggavensium Sexto Pompeio Sexto Appuleio consulibus Amparamus Nemaioqum Cusaburensis hospitium fecit cum civitate Maggaviensium sibi liberis libertisque posterisque suis eunque sic liberos libertos posterosque eius omnis Maggavienses in hospitium fidem clientelamque suam suorum qui receperunt eademque condicione esset qua civis per magistratus Caelionem et Caraegium et Aburnum actum\n
EDCS-28400443.translation: In the consulship of Sextus Pompeius and Sextus Appuleius, on the Kalends of August. Caraegius, Abuanus, and Caelio, magistrates, and the Senate of the Maggavenses, honored Amparamus Nemaiecanus Cusaburensis with honorary citizenship and with the rights for his freedmen and descendants, thus all vows were made to him. Within the territory of the Maggavenses, as a citizen of the Maggavenses, Amparamus Nemaiecanus Cusaburensis made a treaty of hospitality with the citizenship of the Maggavenses for himself, his children, his freedmen, and their descendants. And thus all his freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants would be under the same condition as a citizen, through the magistrates Caelio, Caraegius, and Aburnus.
EDCS-28400443.summary: This inscription records the granting of honorary citizenship and rights of hospitality to Amparamus Nemaiecanus Cusaburensis by the magistrates and senate of the Maggavenses in the Roman consulship of Sextus Pompeius and Sextus Appuleius.

EDCS-28400447.text_edition: \n Marti Terentia Nesia votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-28400447.translation: Terentia Nesia gladly fulfilled her vow to Mars.
EDCS-28400447.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Terentia Nesia to the god Mars.

EDCS-28400449.text_edition: \n Valeria Sextiana Valeri Asclepiadis filiae annorum XIII hic sita est Valerius Asclepiades et Valeria Euterpe \n
EDCS-28400449.translation: Valeria Sextiana, daughter of Valerius Asclepiades, aged 13, is buried here. Valerius Asclepiades and Valeria Euterpe.
EDCS-28400449.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Sextiana, with her parents Valerius Asclepiades and Valeria Euterpe identified.

EDCS-28400450.text_edition: \n Marti Aemilia Lousannio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400450.translation: Aemilia Lousannio gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Mars.
EDCS-28400450.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Aemilia Lousannio to the god Mars.

EDCS-28500328.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning with a dedication to Tiberius Claudius.

EDCS-28500409.text_edition: \n Abico Casarico(n) patri et Antae annorum LX nati et Caricus frater et Titus Casaricon Saicli Calistratio monumentum faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-28500409.translation: Abico, his son Casarico, his father, and Anta, aged 60, born and his brother Caricus, and Titus Casarico, son of Saicli, saw to it that this monument was built by Calistratio.
EDCS-28500409.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Abico, Casarico, Anta, Caricus, and Titus Casarico, likely in memory of Casarico, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-28500412.text_edition: \n Numini Augusto sacrum Ocilitani arcum erexerunt Numini Imperatoris Domit[i]ani Augusti Germanici Ocilitani arcum erexerunt Numini Imperatoris Traiani Augusti Germanici\n
EDCS-28500412.translation: Sacred to the divine Augustus. The Ocilitani erected this arch to the divine Emperor Domitian Augustus Germanicus; the Ocilitani erected this arch to the divine Emperor Trajan Augustus Germanicus.
EDCS-28500412.summary: This inscription records the erection of an arch by the people of Ocelum (Ocilitani) dedicated to the divine emperors Domitian and Trajan.

EDCS-28500413.text_edition: \n Gaio Valerio Marcelo annorum LXIV Marcela monumentum \n
EDCS-28500413.translation: Marcela had this monument built for Gaius Valerius Marcellus, aged 64.
EDCS-28500413.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Marcela in memory of Gaius Valerius Marcellus, who died at the age of 64.

EDCS-28500414.text_edition: \n Sempronia Flava Nymphis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28500414.translation: Sempronia Flava willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-28500414.summary: This votive inscription records Sempronia Flava fulfilling a vow to the Nymphs, likely for a benefit received.

EDCS-28500415.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi filius Augustus pontifex maximus consul <X=V>III tribunicia potestate XXI pater patriae murum dedit Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa consul III faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-28500415.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the divine one, Augustus, chief priest, consul for the 18th time, holding tribunician power for the 21st time, father of the fatherland, gave the wall; Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa, consul for the 3rd time, saw to its construction.
EDCS-28500415.summary: This inscription records Emperor Augustus dedicating a wall, with its construction overseen by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa during their respective consulships and tribunician powers.

EDCS-28500419.summary: This brief inscription records Burdo giving a 'munus' or gift, possibly for a festival or public office.

EDCS-28700001.text_edition: \n nus Caelo Gabili filio annorum LV obiit Aren Alla f\n
EDCS-28700001.translation: Livinus, son of Caelus Gabili, aged 55, died. Aren Alla, his daughter, [arranged for this].
EDCS-28700001.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Livinus at age 55, with his daughter Aren Alla arranging for the monument.

EDCS-28700002.text_edition: \n Marco Culterico Gustuni filio Augustobrigensi annorum XXXV obiit fine Arcobrigensium Marcelus faciendum curavit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-28700002.translation: Marcelus had this built and dedicated it for Marcus Cultericus, son of Gustunus, of Augustobrigensis, aged 35.
EDCS-28700002.summary: This funerary monument was built and dedicated by Marcelus for Marcus Cultericus, from Augustobrigensis, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-28700035.translation: To Hadrian... willingly and deservedly fulfilled [his vow].
EDCS-28700035.summary: This damaged votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Hadrian.

EDCS-28700496.translation: Gaius Julius [erected this] for the veteran Julius.
EDCS-28700496.summary: This inscription likely records Gaius Julius erecting a monument or dedicating something in honor of a veteran named Julius.

EDCS-28700519.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 17th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul, from Asturica Augusta, 93 miles.
EDCS-28700519.summary: This is a milestone inscription, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (AD 117-138), recording the distance in miles from Asturica Augusta.

EDCS-28700586.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Septimius Hermocrates, a consular beneficiarius, set up this for his well-deserving wife Memmia Coeliana, who lived 17 years, 7 months, and 25 days. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-28700586.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Memmia Coeliana, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period, set up by her husband.

EDCS-28701462.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-28701462.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Carus, who reigned from AD 282 to 283.

EDCS-28800024.translation: Grandfather, aged 50 years, and to his wife.
EDCS-28800024.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a tomb or memorial for a man and his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800026.text_edition: \n f Pe ?D M Q L B S Dis Manibus i Hermetis et Tertullae uxoris\n
EDCS-28800026.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Hermes and Tertulla, his wife.
EDCS-28800026.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Hermes and his wife Tertulla, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800614.text_edition: \n Marcus Baebius Cnaei filius Galeria Faustus Cnaeus Baebius Faustus\n
EDCS-28800614.translation: Marcus Baebius Faustus, son of Cnaeus, of the Galeria tribe. Cnaeus Baebius Faustus.
EDCS-28800614.summary: This inscription names at least two individuals, Marcus Baebius Faustus and Cnaeus Baebius Faustus, likely of the Galeria tribe, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800699.text_edition: \n Decimo Cornelio Celso aedili IIviro Celsae Postumia Philippida mater\n
EDCS-28800699.translation: Decimus Cornelius Celsus, aedile, duovir. His mother Celsia Postumia Philippida.
EDCS-28800699.summary: This inscription records the civic offices (aedile and duovir) of Decimus Cornelius Celsus, and names his mother, Celsia Postumia Philippida, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800708.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the Divine Augustus.
EDCS-28800708.summary: This is a dedication to an emperor, likely Tiberius (son of the deified Augustus), dating to the early Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29200010.translation: To Boiogena. Numerius fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29200010.summary: This is a votive inscription to the deity Boiogena, offered by Numerius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29200011.translation: To Epona. Rennius Patus fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-29200011.summary: This is a votive inscription to the deity Epona, offered by Rennius Patus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29200013.translation: Valerius Saturninus erected this to the unconquered god Sol.
EDCS-29200013.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus by Valerius Saturninus.

EDCS-29200015.translation: The fallen goods of Marcus Gracchus Iucundus, appraised at 3,000 denarii, with interest at 100 asses per [unit], the fallen goods of Sextus Gracchus Iucundus.
EDCS-29200015.summary: A record of the appraisal of fallen goods (bona caduca) and interest, likely related to inheritance or legal proceedings involving members of the Gracchus Iucundus family.

EDCS-29200016.summary: A funerary epitaph identifying Coemea, daughter of Nigrus Alticus.

EDCS-29200018.summary: A funerary epitaph identifying Seggues, son of Pedolus Avitianus.

EDCS-29200019.translation: Arcea Alticona, daughter of Aleoneus, lies here.
EDCS-29200019.summary: A funerary epitaph for Arcea Alticona, daughter of Aleoneus.

EDCS-29200020.translation: Caenives Vemenicus Belvicon, son of [---] Sist[---] is laid here.
EDCS-29200020.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caenives Vemenicus Belvicon, identifying his parentage and stating he is buried here.

EDCS-29200021.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph identifying a woman named Alti[---] and her father Nic[---].

EDCS-29200022.summary: A funerary epitaph identifying Ancoema, daughter of Cabuecon Venistus.

EDCS-29200024.summary: A fragmentary epitaph identifying Caaelia Los[---] as the daughter of Maluca[---].

EDCS-29200025.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the age of a person named Aleon[---].

EDCS-29200026.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying Ambatus and his parentage.

EDCS-29200027.summary: This is a funerary inscription indicating the burial of an individual whose name is mostly lost.

EDCS-29200029.translation: Ancoema Desica, daughter of Segi, aged 21.
EDCS-29200029.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating the name, parentage, and age of the deceased.

EDCS-29200030.translation: Ambata Betuca, daughter of Ambatus, is buried here.
EDCS-29200030.summary: This funerary inscription identifies Ambata Betuca, her father, and states she is buried here.

EDCS-29200031.translation: Atillus arranged for this to be made for Moenius Messorius Secundus, aged 30.
EDCS-29200031.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Moenius Messorius Secundus, commissioned by Atillus, and records his age.

EDCS-29200032.translation: The heir, Secundus, arranged for this to be made for Sempronius Veranus, son of Pecus [?], from Ne[...].
EDCS-29200032.summary: This funerary inscription records that Secundus, the heir, commissioned the monument for Sempronius Veranus.

EDCS-29200033.translation: Marcus [...]gaus [...]o arranged for this to be made for Gaius Sempronius, aged 75.
EDCS-29200033.summary: This funerary inscription records that Marcus [...]gaus [...]o commissioned the monument for Gaius Sempronius, stating his age.

EDCS-29200034.translation: Elaesus Petolus, son of Lougei Petraioskos, aged 45.
EDCS-29200034.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, parentage, and age of Elaesus Petolus.

EDCS-29200035.summary: This funerary inscription likely records the name and parentage of a woman, with a possible alternative name.

EDCS-29200042.translation: Minicia Patera, mother, arranged for this to be made, aged 18.
EDCS-29200042.summary: This funerary inscription records that Minicia Patera, the mother, commissioned the monument for her child, who died at age 18.

EDCS-29200043.translation: To the spirits of the departed; Quintus Caecilius F. made this for his deserving Numerius Popidius Celsinus, a decurion.
EDCS-29200043.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Numerius Popidius Celsinus, a decurion, by Quintus Caecilius F.

EDCS-29200044.translation: Caesarrria Paesica, daughter of Saihus, aged 50, is buried here.
EDCS-29200044.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caesarrria Paesica, daughter of Saihus, who died at the age of 50.

EDCS-29200045.translation: Licinius Vasca­sus, son of Ligirius, aged 40, is buried here.
EDCS-29200045.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Licinius Vasca­sus, son of Ligirius, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-29200046.translation: Talaus Caesarius, son of Ambatus, aged 40.
EDCS-29200046.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Talaus Caesarius, son of Ambatus, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-29200047.translation: Auscus Boutius, son of Vironus, aged 30, is buried here.
EDCS-29200047.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Auscus Boutius, son of Vironus, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-29200048.translation: I ask you, passerby, to say that the earth lies lightly upon Terentius Candidus, son of Aravius (?), aged 60, who is buried here.
EDCS-29200048.summary: This is a funerary epitaph with a plea to the passerby for light earth, for Terentius Candidus, son of Aravius (?), aged 60.

EDCS-29200051.translation: Valeria Crescentina to her father, Gaius Valerius Crescens, aged 72.
EDCS-29200051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Valeria Crescentina for her father, Gaius Valerius Crescens, who died at the age of 72.

EDCS-29200052.translation: Ambata Caelica, daughter of Caius, aged 42.
EDCS-29200052.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ambata Caelica, daughter of Caius, who died at the age of 42.

EDCS-29200057.translation: [...] to his wife, had it made.
EDCS-29200057.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, largely lost, recording that someone had the monument made for his wife.

EDCS-29400202.translation: Visadus Pressus, son of Elaesus Pullianus, aged 40.
EDCS-29400202.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Visadus Pressus, son of Elaesus Pullianus, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-29400205.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Antonio, aged 20. Gaieta. May it be well.
EDCS-29400205.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonio, a young man, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400206.translation: To Lucretius Gaco from Clunia, aged 25.
EDCS-29400206.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucretius Gaco, a man aged 25, possibly from Roman Imperial times.

EDCS-29400207.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name and status of a deceased slave girl, with the date undeterminable.

EDCS-29400208.translation: Elaes [name illegible], son of Quenus Trusrius, aged 60, is placed here.
EDCS-29400208.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Elaes, son of Trusrius, aged 60, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400209.translation: To Firmila, daughter of Sacaricus, aged 18.
EDCS-29400209.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Firmila, daughter of Sacaricus, a young woman aged 18, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400210.translation: To Ambata Medica, daughter of Placidus, aged 75.
EDCS-29400210.summary: This funerary inscription records Ambata Medica, daughter of Placidus, aged 75, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400213.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name of a woman and her father, with the date undeterminable.

EDCS-29400214.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a woman and her father, with the date undeterminable.

EDCS-29400215.summary: This funerary inscription records Sempronius Novus, aged 80, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400216.translation: To Urbanus, freedman of Florinus, aged 50, uncle.
EDCS-29400216.summary: This funerary inscription records Urbanus, a freedman of Florinus and an uncle, aged 50, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400217.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a man named Sabius Flavinus, indicating his age.

EDCS-29400218.translation: To Gaius Valerius Paternus, son of Valerius, aged 25.
EDCS-29400218.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Valerius Paternus, son of Valerius, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-29400220.translation: To Cassius Frontonus, son of Quintus, aged 35.
EDCS-29400220.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cassius Frontonus, son of Quintus, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-29400222.translation: To Gaius Antonius Regipanus, aged 42, grandfather [---]
EDCS-29400222.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Antonius Regipanus, who died at the age of 42 and is identified as a grandfather.

EDCS-29400223.translation: To Arceae Desiae, daughter of Cadaecus.
EDCS-29400223.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arceae Desiae, daughter of Cadaecus.

EDCS-29400225.translation: Feliculus arranged to have this made for her husband Sergius [---] Felicis, aged 60.
EDCS-29400225.summary: This funerary inscription records that Feliculus commissioned a monument for her deceased husband Sergius Felicis, who was 60 years old.

EDCS-29400227.translation: Ambata arranged to have this made for her husband Titus Rutilius [---], son of Rutilius, aged [---] Moenicus [---].
EDCS-29400227.summary: This funerary inscription states that Ambata commissioned a monument for her husband Titus Rutilius, son of Rutilius, with further details about his name and age missing.

EDCS-29400228.translation: To the departed spirits: For Anquema Pesica, wife of Cornelius Lateranus, aged [---], Flaus arranged to have this made for her wife. For Cornelius Montanus, son of Flavius, aged 20, Flaus arranged to have this made for her son.
EDCS-29400228.summary: This double funerary inscription commemorates Anquema Pesica, wife of Cornelius Lateranus, and their son Cornelius Montanus, with both arrangements made by Flaus.

EDCS-29400229.translation: To the departed spirits: For Felicioni, aged 55, slave, to Aemilia Patern(a). To the departed spirits: For Athenaide, aged 75, maidservant, to Aemilia Patern(a), she herself arranged to have this made.
EDCS-29400229.summary: This double funerary inscription records a slave, Felicioni, and a maidservant, Athenaide, both associated with Aemilia Patern(a), with Athenaide commissioning her own monument.

EDCS-29400231.translation: Dovidona, a slave born in the household, for the father of Tubicus.
EDCS-29400231.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial for the father of Tubicus, by a slave named Dovidona.

EDCS-29500005.translation: Aburnus son of C[--] is buried here.
EDCS-29500005.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aburnus, son of C[--], likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500006.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription that likely identifies a person named Viganus and his parentage and origin, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500007.translation: Secius Laturus, aged [--] years, is buried here.
EDCS-29500007.summary: This tombstone inscription records the burial of Secius Laturus, son of Laturus, who died at an unspecified age, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500008.translation: [--] Plandica(?) [son/daughter?] of As[--] arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-29500008.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription detailing that someone arranged for a monument to be made, likely a funerary one, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500009.translation: To Sempronius Festus, son of Lougei, aged 65 years.
EDCS-29500009.summary: This is a funerary dedication honoring Sempronius Festus, son of Lougei, who died at the age of 65, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500010.translation: To Calfero son of Cosegius, aged 50 years.
EDCS-29500010.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Calfero, son of Cosegius, who died at the age of 50, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500012.translation: Elaesius Flavinus, son of Elavius, aged 24 years.
EDCS-29500012.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Elaesius Flavinus, son of Elavius, who died at the age of 24, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500017.text_edition: \n Madigeno Laturo militi couertis sic quintae Braecarorum! \n
EDCS-29500017.translation: To Madigenus Laturus, a soldier of the fifth cohort of the Braecari, [stationed at--]
EDCS-29500017.summary: This inscription honors Madigenus Laturus, a soldier in a specific cohort of the Braecari tribe, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500018.translation: To Sempronius Seranus, soldier of the ala of [--], aged 20 years, from his sister and brother.
EDCS-29500018.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Sempronius Seranus, a soldier in a cavalry unit, who died at age 20, dedicated by his sister and brother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500020.translation: To Lucius Antonius(?), son of Q(uirina?) [--], [who served as?] aedile, duovir(?)...
EDCS-29500020.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring a Lucius Antonius, likely a local magistrate, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500037.translation: To Arcea Ela/nioca, daughter of Paternus, aged 30. Gaius Petelius arranged this for his wife.
EDCS-29500037.summary: A funerary epitaph for Arcea Ela/nioca, set up by her husband Gaius Petelius.

EDCS-29500040.translation: Atta Altica, daughter of Aunius, aged 20.
EDCS-29500040.summary: A funerary epitaph for Atta Altica, daughter of Aunius.

EDCS-29500041.translation: Ancoema Plandica, daughter of Segulethus, aged 70.
EDCS-29500041.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ancoema Plandica, daughter of Segulethus.

EDCS-29500043.translation: To Ambata Aionca, daughter of Titus, aged 55. Paternus (?) arranged this to be made for his mother.
EDCS-29500043.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ambata Aionca, set up by her son Paternus (?).

EDCS-29500045.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Fuscula Cornelia Patera, aged 35. Victor, her husband, arranged this to be made for his wife.
EDCS-29500045.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fuscula Cornelia Patera, set up by her husband Victor.

EDCS-29500046.translation: To Optatilia Festa, vernacul(a) of Candius Baebius, aged 27.
EDCS-29500046.summary: A funerary epitaph for Optatilia Festa, a freedwoman of Candius Baebius.

EDCS-29500047.translation: Arcea [unknown], Auca Ambati, daughter of Terentius, aged 70. The heir arranged this to be made.
EDCS-29500047.summary: A funerary epitaph for Auca Ambati, daughter of Terentius, with arrangements made by her heir.

EDCS-29500049.translation: Pompeius and Pompeia arranged this to be made for their mother.
EDCS-29500049.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed mother, set up by her children Pompeius and Pompeia.

EDCS-29500057.summary: A funerary epitaph for Coemea Plandica, daughter of Pedolus.

EDCS-29500058.summary: A fragment of a funerary epitaph recording a male, aged 20.

EDCS-29500060.translation: Coemea, daughter of Agoliecus, daughter of Apollonius.
EDCS-29500060.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription identifying Coemea, daughter of Agoliecus, and Apollonius.

EDCS-29500067.translation: To Coemea Desica, daughter of Apollonius, aged 75. Lucius Lutatius Pater[nus(?)] [prepared this].
EDCS-29500067.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Coemea Desica, detailing her age and the person who erected the monument.

EDCS-29500068.translation: Satelius Latro [prepared this] for his wife Ambata Aionca, daughter of Lougeius.
EDCS-29500068.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ambata Aionca, erected by her husband Satelius Latro.

EDCS-29500071.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription identifying Arcea, daughter of Marcus Ambatus.

EDCS-29500072.summary: This is a dedication to Aia Matern[a, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-29500074.summary: This inscription records the name, parentage, and age of a woman, possibly a funerary monument.

EDCS-29500077.translation: [3]itio daughter of Segei, of Elae/[3]c Ambatae, [---]
EDCS-29500077.summary: This inscription appears to record the names and parentage of women, possibly from a funerary context.

EDCS-29500081.translation: To the departed spirits of Ceceli[a](?), wife of Doideri.
EDCS-29500081.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Ceceli[a](?) by her husband Doideri.

EDCS-29500082.translation: Rennius Valerianus, his heir by the will, to Lucius Rennius Renni f. Renni, his father.
EDCS-29500082.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Rennius Renni f. Renni, erected by his son Rennius Valerianus.

EDCS-29500083.translation: To the departed spirits of [3]vao son of Titi [3]anon [3]os, who set up [---]
EDCS-29500083.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription, likely a dedication to the departed.

EDCS-29500087.translation: Valerius Pressus, son of Valerian, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Monitucine Mothers.
EDCS-29500087.summary: This is a votive inscription by Valerius Pressus to the Monitucine Mothers, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29500089.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription for Sempronia Amba[ta], a Celtiberian woman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29600358.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul II fecit ab Augustobriga milia passuum \n
EDCS-29600358.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, consul for the second time, built [this road] from Augustobriga for X miles.
EDCS-29600358.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording the construction of a road under Emperor Trajan, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-29600359.text_edition: \n Imperator Nerva Caesar Augustus Traianus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul II restituit\n
EDCS-29600359.translation: Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus Trajan Germanicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, restored [this].
EDCS-29600359.summary: This is an inscription recording a restoration by Emperor Trajan, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-29600360.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis pater patriae consul II refecit\n
EDCS-29600360.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, consul for the second time, repaired [this].
EDCS-29600360.summary: This is an inscription recording a repair by Emperor Trajan, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-29600361.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis pater patriae consul II refecit\n
EDCS-29600361.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, consul for the second time, repaired [this].
EDCS-29600361.summary: This is an inscription recording a repair by Emperor Trajan, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-29600363.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus tribunicia potestate XVIIII consul III pontifex maximus pater patriae a Colonia Caesare Augusta milia passum LXXV\n
EDCS-29600363.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, holder of tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the third time, chief priest, father of the country, [built this road] from Colonia Caesarea Augusta for 75 miles.
EDCS-29600363.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording the construction of a road under Emperor Hadrian, dating to approximately 135 AD.

EDCS-29600367.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Severi Pii filio divi Marci Antonini nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittannico maximo tribunicia potestate XV imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli\n
EDCS-29600367.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Severus Pius, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-29600367.summary: This is a formal titulary inscription for Emperor Caracalla, dating to 212 AD.

EDCS-29600368.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittannico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli\n
EDCS-29600368.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, imperator for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-29600368.summary: This is a formal titulary inscription for Emperor Caracalla, dating to 212 AD.

EDCS-29600378.text_edition: \n Tiberius Caesar divi Augusti filius divi f Iuli sic Augustus pontifex maxumus consul VI VII VIII sic tribunicia potestate XXXIIII milia passuum LXV Tiberius Caesar divi Augusti filius Divi Augustus pontifex maxumus consul VI imperator VIII tribunicia potestate XXXIIII milia passuum LXV\n
EDCS-29600378.translation: Tiberius Caesar, son of the deified Augustus, Augustus, chief priest, consul VI, VII, VIII, holder of tribunician power XXXIIII, [built this road] for 65 miles. Tiberius Caesar, son of the deified Augustus, Augustus, chief priest, consul VI, imperator VIII, holder of tribunician power XXXIIII, [built this road] for 65 miles.
EDCS-29600378.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording road construction under Emperor Tiberius, with duplicate consular and tribunician power notations, dating to approximately 31 AD.

EDCS-29600383.text_edition: \n Tiberius Caesar divi Augusti filius divi Iuli nepos Augustus pontifex maxumus sic consul V imperator VIII tribunicia potestate XXXIIII \n
EDCS-29600383.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, pontifex maximus, consul V, imperator VIII, holding tribunician power for the 34th time.
EDCS-29600383.summary: This is a titulature inscription for Emperor Tiberius, likely adorning a public monument, dating to approximately 32 CE.

EDCS-29600384.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III imperator V pater patriae \n
EDCS-29600384.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul III, imperator V, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-29600384.summary: This is a titulature inscription for Emperor Claudius, likely adorning a public monument, dating to approximately 44 CE.

EDCS-29700006.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, our lord, Flavius Julius Constantinus Maximus, the victorious, triumphant Augustus Caesar, son of the deified Constantine the Great, great-grandson of Claudius. (and so forth for 37 years).
EDCS-29700006.summary: This inscription, likely on a triumphal arch or monument, honors Emperor Constantine I, dating to the 37th year of his reign (around 333-334 CE).

EDCS-29700031.translation: Under the charge of Titus Turionius, a most distinguished man, with skillful execution, [he repaired] 21 miles of road from Asturica.
EDCS-29700031.summary: This inscription records the repair of a road section by Titus Turionius, a high-ranking official, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29700052.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, proconsul IIII, consul II, father of the fatherland; 17 miles of road from Bracara.
EDCS-29700052.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, honors Emperor Decius and records the distance from Bracara, dating to his reign (249-251 CE).

EDCS-29700060.summary: This fragmentary inscription honors Emperor Carus, likely a dedication or milestone, dating to his reign (282-283 CE).

EDCS-29700115.translation: Under Emperor Titus Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, son of the deified Vespasian, grandson of the deified Julius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator XV, father of the fatherland, consul VIII; and Caesar, son of the deified Vespasian, Domitian, consul VII; Gaius Calpurnius Rantius Quirinalis Valerius Festus, legate of the emperor with praetorian authority, [built] the Via Nova 31 miles from Bracara.
EDCS-29700115.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, records the construction of the Via Nova under Emperors Vespasian and Titus, and Domitian, mentioning the legate in charge, dating to around 78 CE.

EDCS-29700163.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli II patri patriae proconsuli Invicto Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio PP Egnatio Valeriano Gallieno Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli II patri patriae proconsuli \n
EDCS-29700163.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul II, father of the fatherland, proconsul, Invictus; To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Valerianus Gallienus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul II, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-29700163.summary: This inscription honors both emperors Valerian I and Gallienus, likely on a public monument or milestone, dating to their joint reign (around 253-260 CE).

EDCS-29700167.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, pontifex maximus, imperator VIII, consul V, holding tribunician power for the 34th time. Bracara Augusta II.
EDCS-29700167.summary: This titulature inscription for Emperor Tiberius, possibly marking a location or structure in Bracara Augusta, dates to approximately 32 CE.

EDCS-29700379.summary: This very brief inscription is a dedication or label for Emperor Vespasian, dating to his reign (69-79 CE).

EDCS-29800003.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 17th time, consul for the 3rd and 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, from Asturica Augusta, 93 miles.
EDCS-29800003.summary: This inscription is a milestone marking a distance of 93 miles from Asturica Augusta and dedicated to Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-29800011.translation: From Asturica, 22 miles, to Gaius Calpetanus Rantius Quirinalis Valerius Festus, legate of the emperor, propraetor.
EDCS-29800011.summary: This inscription is a milestone marking a distance of 22 miles from Asturica, dedicated to Gaius Calpetanus Rantius Quirinalis Valerius Festus.

EDCS-29800012.translation: The new road built from Asturica, to Gaius Calpetanus Rantius Quirinalis Valerius Festus, legate of the emperor, propraetor.
EDCS-29800012.summary: This inscription records the construction of a new road from Asturica, dedicated to Gaius Calpetanus Rantius Quirinalis Valerius Festus.

EDCS-29800035.text_edition: \n Nero Claudius divi Claudi Augusti filius Germanici Caeseris sic Augusti nepos Tiberi Caeseris sic Augusti pronepos divi Augusti abnepos Caeser sic Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate V imperator V consul III CCLIX\n
EDCS-29800035.translation: Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius Augustus, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Augustus, great-great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time. 259.
EDCS-29800035.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Nero, listing his imperial dignities and ancestry.

EDCS-29800055.translation: To Moltius, son of Mordoe; Lucius Caecilius Fuscis, soldier of Legio VIII Gemina Felix, has gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow. The Kalends of June. Under Emperors Vespasian (for the 9th time) and Titus (for the 7th time), consuls. To the Lares Vicinales.
EDCS-29800055.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by a soldier of Legio VIII Gemina Felix to the Lares Vicinales during the consulship of Vespasian and Titus.

EDCS-29900420.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 7th time, father of the country, consul, proconsul. To Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, prince of the youth, son of our lord, Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus. They restored the roads and bridges collapsed by age, under the care of Quintus Decius, legate of the emperors, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-29900420.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by a legate under Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar.

EDCS-29900461.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Constantine the Great, referring to him as Caesar.

EDCS-29900490.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Valerio Severo Pio Felici Invicto Nobilissimo Caesari\n
EDCS-29900490.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Severus, Pius Felix, Invictus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-29900490.summary: This inscription is an address to Caesar Flavius Valerius Severus, using honorific titles.

EDCS-29900491.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino maximo Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-29900491.translation: To our lord Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Maximus, Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-29900491.summary: This inscription is an address to Emperor Constantine the Great, using honorific titles.

EDCS-29900493.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae proconsuli et Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Gallieno Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae proconsuli via Augusta milia passuum CCLV\n
EDCS-29900493.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, and to Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Gallienus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul. Via Augusta, 255 miles.
EDCS-29900493.summary: This inscription is a milestone on the Via Augusta, marking 255 miles and dedicated to Emperors Valerian I and Gallienus.

EDCS-29900494.text_edition: \n Victorinus ENTIU pientissime Caesar SIDPELIN PE Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate \n
EDCS-29900494.translation: Victorinus, most devoted to the deified Emperor Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power.
EDCS-29900494.summary: This inscription is a dedication likely to an emperor, honoring Victorinus, and dates to the period of imperial rule.

EDCS-29900809.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul II refecit\n
EDCS-29900809.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the second time, rebuilt (this).
EDCS-29900809.summary: This is an imperial dedication by Trajan, likely on a public building project, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-30000028.text_edition: \n Valerii r et Lu et Manlia Eufes liberti accepto ab ordine loco\n
EDCS-30000028.translation: Valerii, Lu[cius?], and Manlia Eufes, freedmen, having accepted a place from the council.
EDCS-30000028.summary: This inscription records freedmen who received a plot of land from a local council, likely for burial, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-30000037.summary: This fragment records a dedication to an emperor, likely Augustus, by an aedile, dating from the early imperial period.

EDCS-30000117.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Caro invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-30000117.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus, the unconquered Augustus.
EDCS-30000117.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Carus, likely on a monument or building, dating to his reign (282-283 AD).

EDCS-30000118.summary: This is a tombstone recording the name and age of a young individual named Bucco, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-30000119.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for a woman named Cloutina, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-30000122.translation: To Acca Deocena, daughter of Quoronicus Cadanius.
EDCS-30000122.summary: This is a dedication, possibly funerary, to Acca Deocena, daughter of Quoronicus Cadanius, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-31600001.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus [Cornelius?], aged thirty-five years, is buried here.
EDCS-31600001.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus, who died at 35, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-31600022.translation: Voconia Hermais willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-31600022.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Voconia Hermais, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-31600039.translation: Here lies Aliomo, son of Victorius, of the Vadinienses, aged 30 years.
EDCS-31600039.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aliomo, recording his name, parentage, tribal affiliation, and age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500202.translation: To the departed spirits. Acida to her deserving husband. Monument of Alla to Principi, deserving.
EDCS-32500202.summary: This text records a funerary monument dedicated by Acida to her husband and by Alla to Principi, indicating a burial site from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32602215.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Publius Coelius Pistus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32602241.summary: This is a dedication, likely funerary, to Julia Gemina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32602242.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Sextus Sarrionius Parthenius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32800160.translation: From the kilns of Domitia Domitiana, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-32800160.summary: This inscription records the origin of pottery from the kilns of Domitia Domitiana during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus, likely in the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-33700066.text_edition: \n Flaus et Caucius filii sui patri suo munimentum\n
EDCS-33700066.translation: Flaus and Caucius to their son, as a memorial.
EDCS-33700066.summary: This funerary monument was set up by Flaus and Caucius for their son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33700071.text_edition: \n getae uxsori! Crispinus Crispi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-33700071.translation: Crispinus had this made for his wife Geta.
EDCS-33700071.summary: This inscription records Crispinus commissioning a monument for his wife Geta, likely a funerary dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33700075.translation: I have placed this altar to the god Lari Berobreus.
EDCS-33700075.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the placement of an altar to the god Lari Berobreus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33700084.summary: This inscription identifies Quintus from Tolosa (Toulouse), possibly a tombstone or dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33700091.text_edition: \n Gaius Iulius annorum XI hic situs est sit terra levis Iulia Iagena hic sita est sit terra levis\n
EDCS-33700091.translation: Gaius Iulius is buried here, aged 11. May the earth lie lightly on him. Iulia Iagena is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on her.
EDCS-33700091.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius and Iulia Iagena, likely family members, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33700095.summary: This is a short epitaph or greeting, possibly for Iulius Sticus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33700096.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Iulius Marci filius Galeria Aemilianus colonia Salaria IIvir bis flamen Romae et Aug nnum XXXXV pius in suis hic situs est sit tibi terra levis Aelia Secundina uxor posuit\n
EDCS-33700096.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Iulius Aemilianus, son of Marcus, of the Galerian tribe, former duovir and twice flamen of Rome and the Emperor, aged 45, pious in his own affairs, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you. His wife Aelia Secundina set this up.
EDCS-33700096.summary: This is a detailed funerary inscription for Marcus Iulius Aemilianus, a local magistrate and priest, who died at 45, set up by his wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800056.text_edition: \n Caius Annius heic sic situs est\n
EDCS-33800056.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Caius Annius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800058.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Callistus hic situs est vixit annos XXI sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-33800058.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Callistus is buried here. He lived 21 years. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-33800058.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Callistus, who died at 21, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800059.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus sacrum Messeniae Eupliae annorum XX \n
EDCS-33800059.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Messenia Euplia, aged 20.
EDCS-33800059.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment for Messenia Euplia, who died at 20, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800060.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription or ownership marker for Marcus Porcius Firmus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800146.translation: To Caia Plancia Romana, presented with a gift of property in the territory of Accitus for her well-performed service to the state. Gaius Longinus, consular governor, offers this alone.
EDCS-33800146.summary: This inscription records the dedication of land by Gaius Longinus, a consular governor, to Caia Plancia Romana in recognition of her public service, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34300440.translation: Sacred. M. A. I. Maternus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-34300440.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Maternus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34300625.translation: To the departed spirits. Velia Procula is buried here, aged 30, cared for by her parents.
EDCS-34300625.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Velia Procula, aged 30, set up by her parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34300636.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or identification related to two individuals named Philorgos and Philocalus, possibly on a tomb or a personal object.

EDCS-34300673.translation: Lucius Appius made the mold; Lucius Valerius Plomvarius
EDCS-34300673.summary: This inscription records the work of Lucius Appius in making a mold, with Lucius Valerius Plomvarius also mentioned, likely related to a craft or construction project.

EDCS-34800098.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of Gallus Avitus; Luporus, freedman.
EDCS-34800098.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of Gallus Avitus by his freedman Luporus, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800104.translation: Calistratio fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-34800104.summary: This votive inscription records Calistratio fulfilling a vow, likely to a deity, with the phrase 'v(otum) s(olvit) l(ibens) m(erito)' indicating willing and deserved fulfillment.

EDCS-34800105.translation: Peicacomae, daughter of Ma[...], Longinus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-34800105.summary: This votive inscription records a fulfillment of a vow by Longinus, possibly related to Peicacomae, daughter of Ma..., indicating a personal religious dedication.

EDCS-34800110.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Preserver. Valerius Camillus(?) and Servata [dedicated this].
EDCS-34800110.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Preserver, made by Valerius Camillus(?) and Servata, likely on an altar or temple.

EDCS-34800115.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Caelia At[...
EDCS-34800115.summary: This is the beginning of a funerary inscription for Caelia At[...], dedicated to the Divine Spirits.

EDCS-34800118.translation: ...and A[...]; ... and Titus Gaius Ant[...], father, arranged for it to be made.
EDCS-34800118.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record the arrangement for a monument's construction by Titus Gaius Ant[...] (father), possibly related to A[...].

EDCS-34800120.translation: Gaius Appius Seranus, son of Aronis, aged 15, having been crushed by a cistern, died. His parents Appius (fecerunt) made this.
EDCS-34800120.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gaius Appius Seranus, aged 15, due to a cistern accident, and was made by his parents.

EDCS-34800121.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Latro Cornelius(?), to his most devoted wife Antonia Philis, aged 35, placed this.
EDCS-34800121.summary: This funerary inscription records Latro Cornelius(?) dedicating a monument to his most devoted wife, Antonia Philis, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-34800130.translation: Gaius Caecilius Colisi, son of, died at the age of 38. His sister Atta had this made.
EDCS-34800130.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Gaius Caecilius Colisi at age 38, with the monument erected by his sister.

EDCS-34800136.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Valerio Goliarae militi in Germania legionis XIIX sic Octavia Elae marito optimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-34800136.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Octavia Ela had this made for her husband Titus Valerius Goliaras, a soldier in the 18th Legion in Germania, a most excellent husband.
EDCS-34800136.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Titus Valerius Goliaras, a soldier, by his wife Octavia Ela.

EDCS-34800138.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Materna, mother, had this made for her daughter Valeria Flavina, age 16, a most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-34800138.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Flavina, erected by her mother Materna.

EDCS-34800143.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or funerary text, mentioning Gaius Anonius and Titula.

EDCS-34800145.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Domitia Fidelia, mother, placed this for her son Sulpicius and for herself.
EDCS-34800145.summary: A funerary epitaph for a son Sulpicius, erected by his mother Domitia Fidelia, who also intended it for herself.

EDCS-34800147.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Sempronius, father, [placed this] for Valeria, our most dutiful mother, and for Aemilia Lupa, our daughter, and for Aemilia Ursa, our dearest wife, age 14 more or less.
EDCS-34800147.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Sempronius for his wife Aemilia Ursa and daughter Aemilia Lupa, and also his mother-in-law Valeria.

EDCS-34800164.translation: For Manius Octavius Novatus, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe, former prefect of the engineers. Quintus Valerius Argaelus dedicated this.
EDCS-34800164.summary: A dedication by Quintus Valerius Argaelus to Manius Octavius Novatus, a prefect of engineers, possibly on a public building.

EDCS-34800165.translation: Lucius Junius Vitulus, son of Titus, from Crastunum, of Uxama, age 23, lies here.
EDCS-34800165.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Junius Vitulus, a young man from Uxama, dating likely to the Roman period.

EDCS-34801101.translation: Bandua, daughter of [...] LI VICLES, placed this willingly and deservedly in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-34801101.summary: A votive inscription dedicated by Bandua, likely a local deity or spirit, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-34900178.text_edition: \n Nymppae Sica votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-34900178.translation: To the Nymph Sica, he fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-34900178.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to the nymph Sica, recording the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-34900192.translation: To Ambatae Plandidae, Domitia Doidena and Domitius Reburrus for their mother.
EDCS-34900192.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Domitia Doidena and Domitius Reburrus for their mother, Ambatae Plandidae.

EDCS-34900195.text_edition: \n Laribus Vialibus Amauca pro Helvia Marcella votum solvit\n
EDCS-34900195.translation: Amauca paid her vow to the Household Gods for Helvia Marcella.
EDCS-34900195.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the Household Gods made by Amauca for Helvia Marcella.

EDCS-34900199.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription likely naming Lucius Sempronius Longinus and identifying his father.

EDCS-34900202.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or a funerary text, mentioning names that are largely lost.

EDCS-34900793.text_edition: \n Uduniaeco Iunius Silanus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-34900793.translation: Iunius Silanus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the local gods.
EDCS-34900793.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Iunius Silanus to the local gods.

EDCS-34900794.translation: Sacred to Diana. Quintus Tullius Maximus, legate of Augustus of the VII Gemina Felix Legion, enclosed the fields and dedicated them to the gods, and erected a temple for you, Diana, thrice-formed virgin. Tullius, commander of the Iberian legion from Libya, so that you could fly, take mountain goats, strike horned deer, wild boars, and the progeny of forest horses, so that you could compete in a race, so that you could carry weapons. The tusks of boars which Maximus felled, let him dedicate to Diana, a beautiful honor of his valor. The horns of the high-antlered stag which Tullius conquered, carried on a fierce steed, let him dedicate to Diana.
EDCS-34900794.summary: This lengthy inscription records the dedication of a temple and surrounding fields to Diana by Quintus Tullius Maximus, a legate, who also dedicated trophies from his hunts, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-34900859.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Corolla, daughter of Fuscinus, aged [about] thirty years. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Reburrina, daughter of Reburrus, aged ten years.
EDCS-34900859.summary: This is a pair of funerary inscriptions for a mother (Corolla) and daughter (Reburrina), providing their names, parentage, and ages.

EDCS-34900992.translation: For Gudia, daughter of Arconis, aged 40 years.
EDCS-34900992.summary: This funerary inscription records the deceased Gudia, daughter of Arconis, and her age at death.

EDCS-35200375.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Themistocles, aged 50 years. Encolpia made this for her father, who deserved well.
EDCS-35200375.summary: This funerary inscription records the deceased Themistocles, his age, and his daughter Encolpia as the dedicator.

EDCS-36400167.translation: [---] Pupius Censor willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-36400167.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording a votive offering made by Pupius Censor.

EDCS-36400170.text_edition: \n Geminia Aciliana pro salute Valeri Firmini ex voto Tutelae donum dedit\n
EDCS-36400170.translation: Geminia Aciliana gave this gift to Tutela for the well-being of Valerius Firminus, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-36400170.summary: This is a votive offering dedicated by Geminia Aciliana for the health of Valerius Firminus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37500111.summary: This is likely a funerary inscription or marker for Aemilia Creusis, possibly indicating her status or an abbreviated dedication, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37500115.translation: Nympho. Lucius Argyrinus, son of Lucius, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-37500115.summary: This inscription records Lucius Argyrinus fulfilling a vow to the deity Nympho, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37500117.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Rufinus [---]tius.
EDCS-37500117.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the gods of the underworld, likely for Rufinus and another individual, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37500136.translation: Gaius Geminius Cilo is buried here.
EDCS-37500136.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of Gaius Geminius Cilo, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37600967.text_edition: \n Pulcae votum libens solvit animo merito\n
EDCS-37600967.translation: Pulca willingly fulfilled her vow with a deserving spirit.
EDCS-37600967.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Pulca's voluntary fulfillment of a vow, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37600968.summary: This inscription seems to be a dedication or a memorial by Valerius Seranus, perhaps questioning or asserting merit, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37601068.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Optata and Endeice, servants, are buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-37601068.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two servants, Optata and Endeice, dedicated to the gods of the underworld, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-37601069.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Atetus, son of Berci[---]caliq(um) and Allo, 40 years old, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-37601069.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Atetus, son of Berci[---]caliq(um) and Allo, who died at the age of 40, dedicated to the gods of the underworld, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38100013.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or honorific text mentioning Caesar and a divine figure, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38100016.translation: Licinia Moenicu, daughter of Paternius Maganius, is buried here. A() Faccius Curavius had this made, and for himself. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-38100016.summary: This is a tombstone, possibly commissioned by A. Faccius Curavius for his wife Licinia Moenicu and himself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38100275.translation: Lucius Annius, son of Alpetus, from Toledo, who lived 50 years, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-38100275.summary: This is a tombstone recording the burial of Lucius Annius, who died at the age of 50, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38100276.text_edition: \n Maeso Burri filio Avilensis hic situs est\n
EDCS-38100276.translation: Maeso, son of Burri, from Avilia, is buried here.
EDCS-38100276.summary: This inscription records the burial of Maeso, son of Burri, from Avilia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38100277.translation: Festiva, servant of Lucius Aiosegus, who lived 20 years, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-38100277.summary: This is a tombstone for Festiva, a slave of Lucius Aiosegus, who died at age 20, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38100283.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Quintus Vasius V[...] fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-38100283.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, dedicating a fulfillment of a vow by Quintus Vasius V[...], likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38700070.translation: [... lived 50] years and 2 [days]. Arcadius lived seven years and rested in peace on the fourth day before the Nones of December. They were servants of the holy martyr Vincentius.
EDCS-38700070.summary: This inscription records the ages at death of two individuals, Arcadius and another, who were servants of Saint Vincent, and indicates they died on December 2nd, likely dating to late antiquity.

EDCS-38700096.summary: This inscription simply records the name and title of Februarius, bishop, likely dating to late antiquity or the early medieval period.

EDCS-39300090.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, referring to Lucius Licinius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39501895.translation: Sempronia Secundina, who lived 45 years, is buried here.
EDCS-39501895.summary: This is a tombstone recording the burial of Sempronia Secundina, who died at the age of 45, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-41700236.translation: [...]rai[---] / [---]euterus [---] / [---] lived [---] / [1]1 years / [---] honest, loving / [his] lord, faithful to friends / [---] and to [---] bones are placed here / [---]aius Pa[---]rnac[---] / [---] made [this] for his Hieronymus.
EDCS-41700236.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone, likely for a freedman named Hieronymus, recording his age, virtues, and the fact that his bones are buried here, commissioned by someone named Parnacius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-42700022.translation: To Marcus Cornelius Caesianus; Cornelia Terentulla [dedicated this] for her husband, herself, and her sister Cornelia Tertina.
EDCS-42700022.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Cornelia Terentulla to her husband and sister, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-42700024.translation: To Gaius Laetilius Fortunatus and Verginia Maxima; Laetilia [dedicated this].
EDCS-42700024.summary: This appears to be a funerary dedication, possibly by a daughter named Laetilia, to her parents Gaius Laetilius Fortunatus and Verginia Maxima, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-42700025.translation: To Lucius Licinius Caecilianus, son of Lucius; Licinius Severus [dedicated this] to his most dutiful father.
EDCS-42700025.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Licinius Severus to his father, Lucius Licinius Caecilianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-42700027.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its purpose or date, but it likely records names.

EDCS-42700044.summary: This inscription is fragmentary and appears to record the name Cornelius Tem[---] and an age, likely from a funerary context in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43100038.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-43100038.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae pontifice maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVIII consule IIII Licinius Abascantio in re publica Castulonensi VIviratu functus ex indulgentia splendidissimi ordinis quos faciendos in honoresce IIIIIIviratus editis ludis in theatro gladiatoriis circensibus spectaculorum diebus IIII item in theatro festivis acroamatibus frequenter editis statuam Imperatoris Antonini Augusti patris patriae optimi maximique privata pecunia accepto loco a re publica Castulonensium ob honorem VIviratus donum dedit\n
EDCS-43100038.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, pontifex maximus, in his 18th year of tribunician power and 4th consulship, Licinius Abascantus, having served the VIvirate in the state of Castulo for six years, through the indulgence of the most splendid order, out of private funds, presented a statue of Emperor Antoninus Augustus, father of the country, the best and greatest, as a gift to the state of Castulo in place of the honor of the VIvirate, given the honor of the VIvirate through games given in the theatre, gladiatorial and circus spectacles on four days of festivals, and frequent performances of comedies and musical entertainments.
EDCS-43100038.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue by Licinius Abascantus to Emperor Antoninus Pius, commemorating his service as a VIvir in Castulo, and detailing the games he sponsored, dating to the reign of Antoninus Pius (138-161 AD).

EDCS-44100111.translation: To our Lord Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus semper Augustus.
EDCS-44100111.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, likely on a public building or monument, dating to his reign (306-337 AD).

EDCS-44200003.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Corneli Iuniani Vibius Eutychus\n
EDCS-44200003.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of Cornelius Iunianus; Vibius Eutychus [dedicated this].
EDCS-44200003.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Vibius Eutychus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the well-being of Cornelius Iunianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44200004.summary: This is a simple funerary or ownership inscription naming Inventus, a freedman of Caius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44200006.text_edition: \n E annorum plus minus ssius Luci \n
EDCS-44200006.summary: This inscription is highly fragmentary, but appears to record the name Lucius and an age, possibly funerary, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800120.translation: To Ilaes, son of [3]lius, a soldier aged 25 in the Ninth Spanish Legion, his mother saw to the construction.
EDCS-44800120.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier named Ilaes, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, set up by his mother.

EDCS-44800498.translation: Marcus Valerius set up [this monument] for Navia for her merits, having made a vow.
EDCS-44800498.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Valerius honoring Navia, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-44800499.translation: [3]pius, [son of] Manius(?), [3], his wretched mother saw to the construction.
EDCS-44800499.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE, for a person whose name is largely lost, set up by his mother.

EDCS-45000085.translation: Lucius Cornelius set this up for Lucius Cornelius Maternus, aged 25.
EDCS-45000085.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Cornelius Maternus, aged 25, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-45000086.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Sempronius Sulla, aged 70, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-45000087.translation: To Acido Cornutus, son of Latturius, aged 15.
EDCS-45000087.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Acido Cornutus, aged 15, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-45000088.translation: To Milo Eburenius, son of Ambatus, aged 31.
EDCS-45000088.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Milo Eburenius, aged 31, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-45000089.translation: To Iulia Vigannica, daughter of Lascivus, aged 80.
EDCS-45000089.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Vigannica, aged 80, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-45000117.text_edition: \n Rhodine Publi Corneli Iuniani liberta annorum XXVI hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-45000117.translation: Rhodine, freedwoman of Publius Cornelius Iunianus, aged 26, is placed here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-45000117.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rhodine, a freedwoman, dating likely to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-45100116.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest.
EDCS-45100116.summary: This is a titulature inscription for Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-45100122.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name of Marcus and his parentage.

EDCS-45100123.translation: Reburrianus, son of Reburrus, aged 25, is laid here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-45100123.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Reburrianus, who died at age 25, with a common wish for a light burial.

EDCS-45100393.translation: Junius Capito, aged 10, is laid here. Viria Flavina arranged for this to be made for her son.
EDCS-45100393.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Junius Capito, aged 10, and records that his mother, Viria Flavina, commissioned the monument.

EDCS-45500003.translation: Manius Valerius Spantamicus, son of Lucius, is laid here.
EDCS-45500003.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of Manius Valerius Spantamicus.

EDCS-46000001.translation: Aeiodaici(n)o, Tautia Martia fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-46000001.summary: This is a votive inscription recording that Tautia Martia fulfilled a vow to a deity, likely Aeiodaici(n)o.

EDCS-46000010.translation: Sempronius, son of Lucius, erected this, having fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-46000010.summary: This is a votive inscription recording that Sempronius erected a monument after fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-46000013.translation: [...], Paternus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-46000013.summary: This is a votive inscription, though incomplete, recording that Paternus fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-46400787.translation: My heart aches to search for my children after death. I, Nigella, servant of Manius Valerius Vitulus, your mother, overcome by illness, succumbed to the fates. I am better resting peacefully beneath this pious earth than having my body ravaged by disease. Thus, heavy for myself and unbearable for others, I now rest securely under the light turf. Oh you whom our care now touches, oh my wife, dearer than life, farewell. May the earth lie lightly upon me, and may the gods be favorable to you. May Phoebus, as before, be gentle to this poet.
