For this reason, hear me willingly and may there be abundant forgiveness for my confession; I believe I have deserved evil, but you are a good judge. Take away, alas, may I not see your terrible face and voice menacingly. May I not be placed in regions of Eden devoured by flames. But may your long, eternal grace be mine. Ascaricus.
EDCS-34300787.summary: This is a personal religious appeal inscribed by Ascaricus, likely a votive offering or prayer to a goddess, possibly relating to a difficult life experience or seeking divine favor in the afterlife.

EDCS-34300921.summary: This inscription records the names Uge, Rebe, and Trasanci, possibly a funerary or honorary dedication.

EDCS-34300922.translation: Valerius Cuntarioni of the cohort Cohvetene, willingly paid his vow for Bonono.
EDCS-34300922.summary: This inscription records Valerius Cuntarioni, a member of the cohort Cohvetene, fulfilling a vow for someone named Bonono, likely a military or civic dedication.

EDCS-34300949.translation: To Lucius Valerius Maternus Boletanus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, the heir by will.
EDCS-34300949.summary: This inscription records the heir of Lucius Valerius Maternus Boletanus, likely on a tombstone or a related monument.

EDCS-34300950.translation: To the Divine Shades. Turrania Aeniola, a most devoted mother, made this for her daughter Cypriola, aged 26.
EDCS-34300950.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a mother to her deceased daughter, including her age at death.

EDCS-34301043.translation: For Calpurnia Pesica, who died at the age of 21.
EDCS-34301043.summary: This inscription records the death of Calpurnia Pesica at the age of 21, likely on a tomb.

EDCS-34700238.translation: To the provident [deity], Maternus, son of Flaccus, freed from his vow willingly and deservedly, as seen in a vision.
EDCS-34700238.summary: This is a votive inscription by Maternus Flacci, fulfilling a vow made after a divine vision.

EDCS-34700239.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantinus Magnus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, author of peace and quiet, restorer of freedom, and conqueror of enemies, Aemilius Maximus, a consular man, [made this] in the 1500th year [of the city?].
EDCS-34700239.summary: This is a grand imperial dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great by Aemilius Maximus, a former consul, likely celebrating military victories or stability.

EDCS-34700240.translation: From the Ulia guild. Inca Lavasus, of the Fabia tribe, from the guild, aged 70.
EDCS-34700240.summary: This inscription records Inca Lavasus, a member of the Fabia tribe and the Ulia guild, who died at the age of 70.

EDCS-34800038.summary: This is a simple name inscription, likely for a tombstone or marker.

EDCS-34800051.summary: This inscription likely indicates the ownership or name of a workshop.

EDCS-34800064.summary: This inscription indicates the ownership or name of a workshop belonging to Ingenuus.

EDCS-34800068.summary: This is a personal name inscription, possibly identifying an individual on a monument or object.

EDCS-34800096.translation: Caelius willingly fulfilled his vow to Vacocaburius.
EDCS-34800096.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caelius to the deity Vacocaburius.

EDCS-34800097.translation: L. V. Mi[...]ulus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-34800097.summary: This is a personal votive inscription with an abbreviated name.

EDCS-34800107.translation: Quintus Caecilius Titius, son of Titius, a soldier of the VII Gemina Felix legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Fortuna.
EDCS-34800107.summary: This is a military votive inscription dedicated to Fortuna by a soldier of the VII Gemina Felix legion.

EDCS-34800111.translation: Herculentus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to his son Caius.
EDCS-34800111.summary: This is a personal votive inscription, possibly indicating a vow made on behalf of a son.

EDCS-34800112.translation: Certius, slave of the duumvir T. Caecilius Duiris Ordaecis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the health of [Titus Caecilius Duiris Ordaecis?].
EDCS-34800112.summary: This votive inscription was set up by a slave for the health of his master, who was a duumvir.

EDCS-34800113.translation: Vendius, [son of] Valerius Flavius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-34800113.summary: This is a personal votive inscription by Vendius.

EDCS-34800116.translation: Sempronius Lupus and Sempronia Ide made this [monument] while alive for their most beloved daughter Sempronia Elpidotina and for themselves.
EDCS-34800116.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Sempronius Lupus and Sempronia Ide for their daughter and themselves.

EDCS-34800128.translation: [...]ci or Ho[3] / [3] Atto C() A[3] / [3]e Iava so[3] / [3]amio Acco[3] / [3] Atto I M[3] / had it made (?).
EDCS-34800128.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely funerary or votive, with several names and uncertain content.

EDCS-34800129.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Anterus, son of Marcus Ammonius, who died abroad at the age of 50. His son Gaius had [this monument] made.
EDCS-34800129.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Anterus, set up by his son Gaius.

EDCS-34800134.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lacitus, aged 7, lies here. Lupus and Canton had [this monument] made for their son.
EDCS-34800134.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lacitus, set up by his parents Lupus and Canton.

EDCS-34800135.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Avilius, aged [---].
EDCS-34800135.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a person named Avilius, with his age partially preserved.

EDCS-34800141.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Airovius Calcocum, with his age partially preserved.

EDCS-34800148.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius D. had this made for his father and mother and brother, who lie here, aged 30 years.
EDCS-34800148.summary: This funerary epitaph, possibly from the late Roman Republic or early Empire, records a son's commission of a monument for his deceased parents and brother.

EDCS-34800149.translation: To the spirits of the departed. His father-in-law Terto and Fabia Leta, aged 25, most dutiful parents-in-law, and for himself, Licinius Apicius placed this.
EDCS-34800149.summary: This funerary epitaph records Licinius Apicius setting up a monument for his parents-in-law, Terto and Fabia Leta, and himself.

EDCS-34800150.translation: Marcus Laturicon, son of Lucius, had this made for his mother Alionica and her son.
EDCS-34800150.summary: This funerary epitaph records Marcus Laturicon commissioning a monument for his mother Alionica and her son (presumably Marcus himself or a sibling).

EDCS-34800151.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arcoles placed this for Firminus, son, aged 21, and for Sortiocus Serma, sister.
EDCS-34800151.summary: This funerary epitaph records Arcoles setting up a monument for his son Firminus (aged 21) and his sister Sortiocus Serma.

EDCS-34800152.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Preiecta, the living mother, [had this made] for her most dutiful son, aged 30.
EDCS-34800152.summary: This funerary epitaph records a mother, Preiecta, commissioning a monument for her dutiful son who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-34800153.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph, likely recording a parent's dedication for their son.

EDCS-34800156.translation: Caius Aureus, son of Caius, aged 25, died; Aura Cantia had this made for her brother.
EDCS-34800156.summary: This funerary epitaph records Aura Cantia commissioning a monument for her deceased brother, Caius Aureus, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-34800157.summary: This is a short funerary epitaph for Iulius Gemelus, recording his name and age at death.

EDCS-34800159.translation: To Sabinia Tranquillina, wife of our lord Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-34800159.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sabinia Tranquillina, wife of Emperor Gordian III, authorized by local councillors.

EDCS-34801090.translation: Quintius willingly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter.
EDCS-34801090.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter.

EDCS-34801093.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, 31 miles from Bracara.
EDCS-34801093.summary: This inscription marks a distance of 31 miles from Bracara, dedicated to the Emperor Caesar.

EDCS-34801104.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, our lord, no...
EDCS-34801104.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription likely intended as a dedication to an Emperor Caesar.

EDCS-34801354.translation: To Furia Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of our lord Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-34801354.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Furia Sabinia Tranquillina, wife of Emperor Gordian III, authorized by local councillors.

EDCS-34801355.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextus Felicio for Electra, his most devoted wife, aged 50 years, 5 months, and 1 day. She lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34801355.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Electra, wife of Sextus Felicio, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900018.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred. Leon, son of Leon, of Leovasicon. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900018.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Leon, son of Leon, of Leovasicon.

EDCS-34900019.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred. Raecius Festus, aged 55 years. He lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900019.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Raecius Festus, aged 55.

EDCS-34900020.translation: Aphrodite, aged 20 years. She lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900020.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Aphrodite, aged 20.

EDCS-34900021.translation: Atta, aged 80 years. She lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900021.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Atta, aged 80.

EDCS-34900022.translation: Ambatus of Ianua, aged 26, lies here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900022.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ambatus, who died at the age of 26, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900023.translation: Priscus, son of Arron, aged 65, may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900023.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Priscus, son of Arron, who died at the age of 65, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900159.translation: Alubura, daughter of Carisius, and Carisius, son of Camalus, lie here.
EDCS-34900159.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of two individuals, Alubura and Carisius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900160.translation: Bloena, daughter of Camalus, Vala of Bricensis, lies here.
EDCS-34900160.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Bloena, daughter of Camalus and Vala of Bricensis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900162.translation: Urbana, freedwoman of Iunia Marci, lies here.
EDCS-34900162.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Urbana, the freedwoman of Iunia Marci, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900163.translation: To Materna, my dearest and most devoted daughter, who lived 19 years. You abhor old age with me, deceived.
EDCS-34900163.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Materna, daughter of Paterna, who died at 19, written by her parent and expressing grief, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900164.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Sullia Materna, aged 80.
EDCS-34900164.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the Divine Spirits for Sullia Materna, who died at the age of 80, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900165.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Vibius Placidina, aged 35.
EDCS-34900165.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the Divine Spirits for Vibius Placidina, who died at the age of 35, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900168.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Lucius Paternus Ovit.
EDCS-34900168.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the Divine Spirits for Lucius Paternus Ovit, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900169.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. No Ac
EDCS-34900169.summary: This is an incomplete funerary epitaph fragment dedicated to the Divine Spirits, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900170.translation: To Alicia Onna, aged 22. Amma Salmio, her mother.
EDCS-34900170.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Alicia Onna, aged 22, set up by her mother.

EDCS-34900171.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Felicula, aged 50, most dutiful mother. Aprilia.
EDCS-34900171.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Felicula, aged 50, described as a most dutiful mother, by Aprilia.

EDCS-34900172.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Fuscus, aged 18. Decimus Aponius Ater arranged for this to be made for his most dutiful father.
EDCS-34900172.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Fuscus, aged 18, and was arranged for by his son, Decimus Aponius Ater, for his most dutiful father.

EDCS-34900173.translation: For Gaius Memmius Quirinus, son of Atta, aged 70. Catonia Flavina for her great-grandfather.
EDCS-34900173.summary: This epitaph records Catonia Flavina setting up a monument for her great-grandfather, Gaius Memmius Quirinus, who was 70 years old.

EDCS-34900174.translation: To my husband, aged 30. Appia Boutta.
EDCS-34900174.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a husband, aged 30, dedicated by Appia Boutta.

EDCS-34900175.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. For Lucius Valerius Silon, aged 50. Valeria Paterna, his daughter, and Lucius Valerius Asellus, his son, set this up for their father. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900175.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Valeria Paterna and Lucius Valerius Asellus erected this monument for their father, Lucius Valerius Silon, aged 50, and includes a wish for the deceased.

EDCS-34900179.translation: Uraitonis of the Polecensians freely and deservedly paid [this] for the well-being of Cabuniaeginus Doidera Tridiana.
EDCS-34900179.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Uraitonis of the Polecensians for the well-being of Cabuniaeginus Doidera Tridiana.

EDCS-34900180.translation: Gaius Licinius Cissus, in fulfillment of a vow, freely and deservedly [dedicated] a temple to the Mother of the Gods.
EDCS-34900180.summary: This inscription records Gaius Licinius Cissus dedicating a temple to the Mother of the Gods as fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-34900181.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aemilia set up [this] in memory.
EDCS-34900181.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Aemilia set up a monument in memory of someone.

EDCS-34900182.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. For Bibia Placina, his most dutiful wife, aged 20.
EDCS-34900182.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Bibia Placina, aged 20, described as a most dutiful wife.

EDCS-34900183.translation: To the spirits of the departed; I, Messorina, erected this monument for my husband Severus and Victor.
EDCS-34900183.summary: A funerary monument erected by Messorina for her husband Severus and Victor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900185.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Valerius Crescens erected an altar, willingly and deservedly fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-34900185.summary: A votive altar dedicated to Jupiter by Valerius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900187.translation: To the spirits of the departed; Fuscinus erected this for his brother Marcus Sempronius Fuscus, son of Oculatus, aged 55. He lies here.
EDCS-34900187.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Sempronius Fuscus, erected by his brother Fuscinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900188.translation: Valleria had this made for her dearest husband, aged 35, and for herself.
EDCS-34900188.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Valleria for her husband and herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900191.translation: To the spirits of the departed; Marcus Antonius Fuscus, aged 60, and Aurelia Pia, aged [incomplete].
EDCS-34900191.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Antonius Fuscus and Aurelia Pia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900206.translation: Aemilia Privata, aged 45, lies here. May the earth be light for you, my daughter; her daughter made and dedicated this.
EDCS-34900206.summary: A funerary inscription for Aemilia Privata, erected by her daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900209.summary: A simple epitaph for Constanta from Bracara Augusta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900211.translation: To the Invictus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, from Bracara, 15 miles.
EDCS-34900211.summary: A milestone indicating a distance of 15 miles from Bracara, possibly related to an imperial cult or a specific emperor, likely from the Late Roman Empire.

EDCS-34900213.translation: To Emperor Flavius Julius Constantius, twice consul, Caesar.
EDCS-34900213.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Constantius, likely from the 4th century AD.

EDCS-34900214.translation: Of Emperor Marcus Aurelius, holding power for the third time, consul, from Bracara Augusta, 32 miles.
EDCS-34900214.summary: A milestone indicating a distance of 32 miles from Bracara Augusta, honoring Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-34900215.translation: To our lord Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the unconquered, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-34900215.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Galerius Valerius Maximianus, likely dating to his reign in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-34900217.summary: This is an ownership mark or epitaph for Publius Turullius Labeo, whose date is uncertain without further context.

EDCS-34900218.translation: The slave of Capilianus Rodanus made this.
EDCS-34900218.summary: This records that Capilianus Rodanus's slave created the object, possibly a funerary monument or a personal item, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-34900221.translation: Of Gaius Lucretius Primus, son of Marcus Lucretius Celsus.
EDCS-34900221.summary: This is a fragment possibly indicating ownership or a funerary inscription for Gaius Lucretius Primus, son of Marcus Lucretius Celsus, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-34900223.summary: This is an ownership mark or epitaph for Sextus Remmius Fortis, whose date is uncertain without further context.

EDCS-34900229.translation: To Aurelius, son of Iaftius, soldier.
EDCS-34900229.summary: This is a dedication or epitaph for Aurelius, son of Iaftius, a soldier, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-34900230.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Salutaris arranged for the construction of a monument for his well-deserving wife, Atticia Primitiva, aged 50 years.
EDCS-34900230.summary: This funerary inscription records Salutaris dedicating a monument to his wife Atticia Primitiva, who died at the age of 50, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-34900231.summary: This inscription identifies Tullia Talantea, a freedwoman of Lucius, likely an epitaph or ownership mark from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34900241.translation: Boutius, son of Talogus, aged 35 years, may the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-34900241.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Boutius, son of Talogus, aged 35, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34900247.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the health of Aurelius Anoninus and Aurelius Verus, Augusti, and their descendants, the soldiers of the ninth legion Hispana, under the care of Egenius Pater, prefect of the same cohort, and Lucius Paccius Paene, centurion, and Aulius Avianus, signifer of the same ninth legion, on the 9th day before the Ides of June.
EDCS-34900247.summary: This dedication records a group of soldiers from the Ninth Legion Hispana and their officers making an offering for the health of the emperors Aurelius Anoninus and Aurelius Verus, dated to June 5th in the reign of these emperors (likely mid-2nd century CE).

EDCS-34900260.translation: ELL RI, daughter of Ambatus, to her husband.
EDCS-34900260.summary: This is a funerary dedication by ELL RI, daughter of Ambatus, to her husband, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900795.summary: This inscription records the name and age of Arrena, daughter of Cloutus, likely on a tombstone from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900812.translation: To Asturio, son of Logeus, aged 95.
EDCS-34900812.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Asturio, son of Logeus, who lived to be 95 years old, likely from a tombstone of the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900813.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Ouriso, son of Toncetamus, likely on a tombstone from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900814.summary: This inscription records Casia, aged 25, and her husband Eburius, likely on a tombstone from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900816.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Licinia, daughter of Licinus, aged 20.
EDCS-34900816.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Licinia, daughter of Licinus, aged 20, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900817.translation: To Optata Sabina, servant of Aelius, aged 27.
EDCS-34900817.summary: This inscription records the death of Optata Sabina, a freedwoman of Aelius, at the age of 27, likely from a tombstone of the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900818.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the goddesses Dovitero and Ulbogenia, likely a religious dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900819.translation: To Flaccilla, daughter of Flaccus, aged 40.
EDCS-34900819.summary: This inscription records the name and age of Flaccilla, daughter of Flaccus, likely on a tombstone from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900820.translation: To Flavia, daughter of Flavius, aged 60. Sacred to the departed spirits. To Reburrina, daughter of Reburrus, aged 50.
EDCS-34900820.summary: This inscription contains two funerary dedications, one to Flavia, daughter of Flavius (aged 60), and another to Reburrina, daughter of Reburrus (aged 50), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900822.translation: Banae, in fulfillment of a vow to Fortunatus Coltnatus.
EDCS-34900822.summary: This is a votive dedication to the deity Fortunatus Coltnatus, likely from a woman named Banae.

EDCS-34900839.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop owned by Valerius Taurus, likely functioning as a business sign.

EDCS-34900841.summary: This is a simple inscription naming Valerius Paternus, possibly a tombstone or ownership marker.

EDCS-34900972.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Calpurnia. To the Divine Shades. For Anna, daughter of Lentinius, aged 70. To the Divine Shades. For Cornelius.

EDCS-34900976.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription recording the age of a son.

EDCS-34900980.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes of Reburrina, daughter, aged 70. Sacred to the Divine Manes of Anna, daughter, aged 45.
EDCS-34900980.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of two women, Reburrina and Anna.

EDCS-34900981.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription recording the age of a son.

EDCS-34900993.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Talaus, son of Docius.

EDCS-34900995.translation: son of Cloutius, aged 12. // son of Pentilius, aged 70. // son of Cloutius, aged 20.
EDCS-34900995.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment recording the ages of three individuals, possibly sons of the same father.

EDCS-34900998.summary: This inscription records the name of a workshop belonging to Numacianus.

EDCS-35001049.translation: May the earth lie lightly on Mu here.
EDCS-35001049.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for someone named Mu, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-35001051.translation: Lanciensis Traqudanus is buried here, Bracarus PIL.
EDCS-35001051.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Lanciensis Traqudanus, with an unclear abbreviation, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-35200270.translation: You will be covered by earth in this tomb, you who were the leader snatched away by death hostile to its peoples; who, by living a holy life in body through your merits, will be believed to have the day of heavenly light. The causes of the wretched seek you, the prayers of the grieving seek you, you whom you always supported with voice, hand, and tears. Let this passing not deprive them of you alone, or rather, those whose misfortune they now complain of, you have departed. He departed on the 16th day before the Kalends of July, in the year 362 (AD 306) in peace.
EDCS-35200270.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Sefronius, an 'antistes' (priest or leader), who died on June 16th, 306 AD, and was known for his charitable work.

EDCS-35200272.translation: We have enclosed the tender body in precious marble for the eternal resting place of the Ithacian's name.
EDCS-35200272.summary: This is a funerary inscription for someone with the surname Ithacius, describing their burial in a marble sarcophagus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-35200413.translation: This urn holds your venerable body, Vincent, abbot, but your sacred soul holds the heavenly kingdom. You, a priest, exchanged this life for a better one. The example of the martyr signifies this: that the index, guided by God, found your sacred remains. He had lived fifty-two years ministering to the mysteries of Christ with a sincere mind. Suddenly snatched away to the eternal breezes, his spirit arrived. Thus, the advent of the Lord's spirit simultaneously removed his office and his life. The holy man lived in a pious region and placed his soul there. Soon all these were to be lifted from the flames. He died in peace on the 5th day before the Ides of March, in the year 306 (AD 305) in peace.
EDCS-35200413.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Abbot Vincent, who died on March 10th, 305 AD, after 52 years of service, and is described as a martyr figure and a minister of Christ's mysteries.

EDCS-36400162.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fortunatus, tax collector, for Decimus Statorius, the Princeps, and his freedman Felix.
EDCS-36400162.summary: This is a dedication to the Divine Manes for Decimus Statorius and his freedman Felix, by Fortunatus, a tax collector, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36400163.translation: Setus Ulpius placed this for the household gods, by vow.
EDCS-36400163.summary: This is a dedication to the household gods (Laribus Vialibus) by Setus Ulpius, made in fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36400165.translation: Michael, Gabriel, Cecitiel, Oriel, Raphael, Ananiel, Marmoniel.
EDCS-36400165.summary: This is a list of seven angelic names, possibly from a religious context, dating to the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-37500048.translation: Lucius Terentius, freedman of Sextius Lucius Terentius Titianus, in honor of the six-man magistracy which he held, under the consulship of Apronianus and Mauricus.
EDCS-37500048.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Terentius, a freedman, honoring his term as a VI vir (member of a college of six) during the consulship of Apronianus and Mauricus, likely in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-37500051.translation: Co-oni, V bos, N. Andr., Amb.
EDCS-37500051.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a reliable translation or summary, possibly related to a boundary marker or a very abbreviated dedication.

EDCS-37500065.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Titus Aemilius Aemilianus, aged 22. Terentia Faustina, most dutiful to her son.
EDCS-37500065.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Aemilius Aemilianus, set up by his mother, Terentia Faustina.

EDCS-37500067.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. For Sadina, aged 70. A sweet husband to his most dutiful wife. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-37500067.summary: This epitaph for Sadina, aged 70, was set up by her husband.

EDCS-37500071.translation: For Victor, aged 36. Ulpianus, slave. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-37500071.summary: This tombstone for Victor, aged 36, was erected by his slave Ulpianus.

EDCS-37500095.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Pro Gaius F. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-37500095.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pro Gaius F.

EDCS-37500099.translation: Valeria Marcella gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to the Matres.
EDCS-37500099.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Matres by Valeria Marcella.

EDCS-37500103.translation: To Marcello Babicun, son of Titus, a husband of CH simi.
EDCS-37500103.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcello Babicun, son of Titus.

EDCS-37500114.translation: Calpurnia Germana, aged 30, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-37500114.summary: This tombstone for Calpurnia Germana, aged 30, marks her burial place.

EDCS-37500131.translation: SQBQ workshop of Aulus C. Pas
EDCS-37500131.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely referring to a workshop and its owner.

EDCS-37600151.translation: For the honor of Saint Peter the Apostle. Pelagius, priest and abbot, a distinguished man, restored this church in the year 1000.
EDCS-37600151.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a church by Pelagius, a priest and abbot, in the year 1000.

EDCS-37900042.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Claudius Goethicus Augustus Germanicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, imperator for the second time. S
EDCS-37900042.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Emperor Marcus Aurelius Claudius Goethicus, likely from the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-38000031.translation: After the senatorial dogma, as taught by the Bishop of Lyon, which now resounds throughout the world as the reign of Christ, and after the sacred pen of Paul, by which the world's council was struck and finally stupified by the silence of the Sophists, a path opens to you, Martin, in the furthest northern reaches, and the way of faith, impassable for the land, lies open; through the signs of virtues and the praise of your merits, it stirs the spirit of frigid Germany. Inflamed by the fire of the divine spirit, you loosen the immense and varied frosts bound by the hostile north, incorporating into a holy covenant of Christ the Alemanni, Saxons, Thuringians, Pannonians, Rugi, Slavs, Naristi, Sarmatians, Dații, Ostrogoths, Franks, Burgundians, Dacians, and Alans. Rejoicing, your Suevian followers, admiring your signs, learn by what path of faith they should proceed and, devoted to your merits, have raised this temple of Christ with a clear pinnacle, where you, Martin, chosen by the grace of your signs, are proclaimed to be present by vows; and Gaul, rejoicing, holds you as its own pastor; may all of Galicia hold you as its patron.
EDCS-38000031.summary: This long inscription, likely from a church dedicated to Saint Martin, celebrates his missionary work in converting various Germanic and Slavic peoples in the 6th or 7th century, attributing the temple's construction to the Suevi.

EDCS-38000033.translation: Born a Pannonian, crossing vast seas, driven by divine will into the bosom of Galicia, Saint Martin, consecrated in this hall, I, Martin, your servant of the same name, have established worship and the rites of sacred ceremonies, following you as patron; not by merit do I rest here in Christ's peace.
EDCS-38000033.summary: This epitaph records the burial of a cleric named Martin, who was born in Pannonia and served in Galicia, likely in a church dedicated to Saint Martin, placing his death in the 7th century based on the style and language.

EDCS-38000034.translation: Remisnuera rests here on the Kalends of May, in the year 656 (518 AD), on the second day of the week, in peace. Amen.
EDCS-38000034.summary: This is a simple tombstone inscription recording the death of Remisnuera on May 1st, 518 AD.

EDCS-38000035.translation: On the third day before the Kalends of May, Pelagius completed three atria in honor of Saint Mary.
EDCS-38000035.summary: This inscription indicates that Pelagius completed three porticoes or courtyards dedicated to the Virgin Mary, likely on April 29th, although the year is missing.

EDCS-38000036.translation: Here lie interred the relics of Saint Marina, Saint Cecilia, Saint Acisclus, Saint Christopher, and Saint Columba.
EDCS-38000036.summary: This inscription lists the relics of several saints that were interred in this location, likely a church or reliquary.

EDCS-38000038.translation: This sacred structure rises again by divine command, with modest work, clearly and brightly displayed to the eyes of the faithful, figuratively showing the sign of the holy cross; may this hall, consecrated under the trophy of the cross, be pleasing to Christ, which Faffila the servant thus founded with prompt faith, along with his wife Froiliuba and their offspring, the pledges of their line; may they receive abundant grace from you, Christ, for these gifts, and after the course of this life, may your abundant mercy reach them. The altar was consecrated here by the seer Asterius to Christ, in the 775th year of the era, the sixth hour running.
EDCS-38000038.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a church or chapel in 737 AD, founded by Faffila and his wife Froiliuba, with the consecration performed by Bishop Asterius, to commemorate the cross and seek divine favor.

EDCS-38000039.translation: O you who penetrate the innermost thoughts of all men, Abbot Ildemud, you are the true architect of this earthen dwelling. You who form souls with divine breath, and again bid the fleeting things of earth return to the light. Be merciful to him who lies buried here, so that he may never feel the wrath of hell, and when the supreme trumpet blast shakes the world, he may arise on the right hand. Know, O reader, that Brabium strove with all diligence to achieve this. He was handsome, eloquent, pious, and modest; he acted justly and taught wisely, this protector. He was hope and support for widows and orphans, he drove away temptations and led the monastic life; thus he stood as a father to the monks in a paternal bond. Remember him, Christ.
EDCS-38000039.summary: This epitaph praises Abbot Ildemud, buried here, for his virtues and spiritual leadership, requesting mercy for him and emphasizing his role as a protector and guide for monks, widows, and orphans, likely dating from the 8th century.

EDCS-38000043.summary: This very brief inscription records the name Pelmio and the year 862 AD, likely marking a property or a date of death.

EDCS-38000044.translation: Whoever you are, hurrying here from the west, whoever you are from the east, you have the gift you venerate in this tomb. Look upon the talent endowed with heavenly grace, which the ark of his heart always holds God, the pinnacle of religion, the glory of life, the armor of salvation. Victorinus, wise in his merits, is present, exercising a worthy work, who, with fruitful labor, did not live for himself alone in the world, pouring forth many swarms of monks throughout the land, he has made sweet the eternal flowers like a bee. His powerful tongue, his piety, his constant prayer were such that he seemed wholly to be with the stars. Often granting many miracles for beneficial deeds, his blessed life gave faith. He ruled the monastery for ten periods of five years with paternal moderation, a rich pastor, pleasing to God, following the straight path, in sacred footsteps. Now he enjoys the sight he longed for, by love.
EDCS-38000044.summary: This inscription praises Abbot Victorinus, who died around 890 AD (ten lustra or 50 years of abbacy), for his spiritual leadership, charitable work, founding of monasteries, and his virtuous life which led him to heaven.

EDCS-38000045.translation: Here rests in the tomb one upon whom fifteen periods of ruling monks fell, following the example of Christ who taught and showed the way, he filled Iberia and Gaul with great growth of monks, and appointed worthy elders in sanctity to them, whom he himself, the first man, Abbot Victorinus, appointed. He completed his blessed course of life, nearly eighty years, in peace. Buried in the merits of Paul and Antony, he ascended to the aether, in the year of King Atanagild's reign, the seventh, on the Saturday before the Ides of January.
EDCS-38000045.summary: This epitaph records the death of Abbot Victorinus, who ruled monks for seventy-five years and lived to be nearly eighty, and was buried on January 11th in the seventh year of King Atanagild's reign (likely 567 AD).

EDCS-38000048.translation: Lady Cixilia founded this temple; may she have a worthy fate and joyfully sing hymns forever with the highest citizens in heaven.
EDCS-38000048.summary: This inscription is a dedicatory poem for a temple founded by Cixilia, likely from the late antique or early medieval period.

EDCS-38000049.translation: Aspidia, servant of God, lived for more or less 30 years, rested in peace on the day before the Ides of March, in the era 502 (indiction 1).
EDCS-38000049.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aspidia, a servant of God, dated to the era 502 (likely 464 AD).

EDCS-38000050.translation: Sagenis, servant of God, lived for 50 years, departed in peace on the 2nd day before the Ides of April, in the era 600.
EDCS-38000050.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sagenis, a servant of God, dated to the era 600 (likely 562 AD).

EDCS-38000051.translation: Immafrita Imarita, servant, lived for more or less 35 years, rested in peace on the sixth day before the Nones of November, in the era 617; a burial plot was provided for 3 gold solidi.
EDCS-38000051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Immafrita Imarita and the purchase of her burial plot, dated to the era 617 (likely 579 AD).

EDCS-38000053.translation: The priest Amator, at the age of 43, encountered the heavenly king on the 5th day of the Ides of February, in the prosperous era 652, in the first year and 10 months of the reign of King Sisebut; he rested in peace. Amen.
EDCS-38000053.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Amator, a priest, detailing his age, death date within the reign of King Sisebut, and episcopal tenure, dated to the era 652 (likely 614 AD).

EDCS-38000055.translation: The body of Epenetus the bishop.
EDCS-38000055.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying the burial place of Epenetus, a bishop.

EDCS-38000056.translation: In the name of the Lord, Acrusminus, bishop of the church of Bigastrensis, consecrated this basilica of Saint Vincent in the third year of his pontificate.
EDCS-38000056.summary: This inscription records the consecration of the basilica of Saint Vincent by Bishop Acrusminus of Bigastrensis.

EDCS-38000061.translation: Saint Pauline, may you live and flourish and always be seated.
EDCS-38000061.summary: This is a devotional invocation or prayer addressed to Saint Pauline.

EDCS-38000079.translation: For the honor of Saint James the Apostle, Adefonsus the prince, with his wife, protects this sign; this pious work is completed. They offer, servants of God. Queen Scemena is protected by this sign from the enemy, in the twelfth year of the era 900.
EDCS-38000079.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a work by Prince Adefonsus and Queen Scemena for the honor of Saint James the Apostle, dated to the era 912 (likely 874 AD).

EDCS-38000080.translation: Where Abraham offered his son Isaac as a burnt offering to the Lord. Saint Paul the Apostle, Saint Peter the Apostle.
EDCS-38000080.summary: This inscription references the biblical story of Abraham and Isaac and invokes the apostles Saint Paul and Saint Peter, likely associated with a religious site or dedication.

EDCS-38000081.translation: Where Daniel was sent into the lions' den, Saint Thomas, Saint Philip the Apostle, Emmanuel.
EDCS-38000081.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented biblical or devotional reference, possibly related to a religious building or artwork, but its context and purpose are unclear due to its brevity and lack of grammatical structure.

EDCS-38000082.translation: In this place rests the servant of God Oraca Casta, beloved wife of Novembr. For her soul [may rest].
EDCS-38000082.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Oraca Casta, wife of Novembr, likely from the early medieval period, expressing a wish for her soul's repose.

EDCS-38000084.translation: This place, anciently dedicated in honor of Michael the Archangel, was constructed with brief work, then after ruins was abolished, remained ruined for a long time, until Adefonsus the abbot, coming with companions from his homeland of Cordoba, rebuilt the ruin of the church. Under the valiant and serene Prince Adefonsus, with the number of monks increasing, finally this beautiful temple, amplified from its foundation with marvelous work, is erected, not by imperial command or the oppression of the populace, but by the diligent vigilance of Abbot Adefonsus and the brothers, completed in twelve months. Garsea, ruling the sceptres of the kingdom, and Mumadonna with the queen, in the year 951 (AD 893), consecrated this temple by Bishop Iennadius on the 12th day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-38000084.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and consecration of a monastery church dedicated to St. Michael the Archangel by Abbot Adefonsus and his community, with royal patronage from Garsea and Mumadonna, completed and consecrated in 951 (AD 893).

EDCS-38000085.translation: In honor of Saint Michael the Archangel, in the year 78 after the thousand (AD 1078), while reigning our most serene prince Lord Ferdinand the King and Queen Sancia, under the virtue of Christ, Cyprianus, bishop by the grace of God, in [the church of] Holy Mary, under the mercy and grace of the Lord, Sabaricus the abbot with all his brothers and companions, following the fear of the Lord in the church.
EDCS-38000085.summary: This inscription, dated to 78 (AD 1078), commemorates the honoring of St. Michael the Archangel within the church of Holy Mary, under the reign of King Ferdinand and Queen Sancia, with the participation of Bishop Cyprianus and Abbot Sabaricus.

EDCS-38000087.translation: In the name of Christ, I Peter, bishop of Holy Mary, carried out the restoration on the day of Saint Michael, the 5th day before the 17th day before the Kalends of July, in the year 1076 (AD 1018), King Adefonsus, Suero Alvarie abbot.
EDCS-38000087.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a church or chapel by Bishop Peter of Holy Mary, with the involvement of Abbot Suero Alvarie and King Adefonsus, dated to 1076 (AD 1018).

EDCS-38000088.translation: Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews. Ferdinand the King, Sancia the Queen.
EDCS-38000088.summary: This inscription appears to be a royal dedication or statement of patronage by King Ferdinand and Queen Sancia, possibly related to a religious building or artifact, referencing Jesus the Nazarene.

EDCS-38000089.translation: In the name of the Lord. Urraca, [daughter] of Ferdinand.
EDCS-38000089.summary: This is a simple inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, identifying Urraca and her father Ferdinand.

EDCS-38000090.translation: This hall which you see, of Saint John the Baptist, was formerly muddy, which the most excellent King Fredenandus and Queen Sancia recently built of stone. Then they brought the body of Saint Isidore, bishop, from the city of Hispalis to this dedication of this temple on the 12th day before the Kalends of January, in the year 1051 (AD 1013). Afterwards, in the year 1053 (AD 1015), on the 6th day before the Ides of May, they brought the body of Saint Vincent, brother of Sabina, here from the city of Avila. And in that same year, the aforementioned king, returning from the enemies from the city of Valencia, died here on the day of the Sabbath, on the 3rd day before the 6th day before the Kalends of January, in the year 1053 (AD 1015). Sancia the queen, dedicated to God, completed it.
EDCS-38000090.summary: This inscription details the construction of a hall dedicated to St. John the Baptist by King Ferdinand and Queen Sancia, the relocation of the relics of St. Isidore and St. Vincent, and the death of King Ferdinand in 1053 (AD 1015), dating the events between 1013 and 1015.

EDCS-38000091.translation: In the year 1036 (AD 998), the basilica of Saint Christopher was consecrated by Bishop Peter in the day of Saint Mary, at Leon.
EDCS-38000091.summary: This inscription records the consecration of the basilica of Saint Christopher by Bishop Peter, dated to 1036 (AD 998) in Leon.

EDCS-38000092.translation: In the name of the Lord, I Ciprianus the abbot, consecrated this.
EDCS-38000092.summary: This is a brief dedication inscription by Abbot Ciprianus, likely marking the consecration of a religious structure.

EDCS-38000098.translation: James Micael the cleric died on the 3rd day before the Nones of [January], era 3.
EDCS-38000098.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of James Micael the cleric, dated to era 3 (likely a post-Roman dating system).

EDCS-38000100.translation: Blessed Tirse, pray for me that all my sins may be forgiven by the Lord through him who chose you for himself. Whoever here in this basilica shall do penance for their faults...
EDCS-38000100.summary: A prayer inscribed in a basilica, likely for personal salvation, written in a post-Roman era.

EDCS-38000101.translation: Watch in the Lord Jesus Christ, O most pure Virgin Mary, Scemena.
EDCS-38000101.summary: An invocation to watch in Christ, mentioning the Virgin Mary and a person named Scemena, likely a funerary or devotional inscription from the post-Roman period.

EDCS-38000102.translation: O Christ, son of God, who entered the virginal womb of the blessed Mary without human conception and departed without corruption, who through your servant Ranimirus, the glorious prince, and his queen consort Paterna, renewed this dwelling consumed by excessive age, and for them built this altar of the glorious blessing of Mary in this holy place, hear them from your heavenly dwelling and forgive their sins, you who live and reign for ever and ever. Amen. The 9th day before the Kalends of July, era 886.
EDCS-38000102.summary: A dedication inscription for an altar of the Virgin Mary, recounting the renewal of a building by Prince Ranimirus and Queen Paterna, dated to era 886 (likely a post-Roman dating system).

EDCS-38000104.translation: May this house be eternal, may it always be the temple of the Lord, and all our offerings made here we have consecrated to you. May he who favors me and keeps it, may he who wishes to tear down vows, be deprived of Christ's light while living, and may the earth swallow him in mendicity and leprosy, may he keep his lineage. From the servant of the saviour Sti. [---]
EDCS-38000104.summary: A curse inscription protecting a sacred house or temple, warning against its desecration, likely from the post-Roman period.

EDCS-38000105.translation: The servant of God Severus died on the 6th day before the Kalends of April, era 965.
EDCS-38000105.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Severus, identified as a servant of God, dated to era 965 (likely a post-Roman dating system).

EDCS-38000107.translation: In the name of the Lord, the temple of Saint James the Apostle was consecrated by the servant of God Ariane, bishop of Ourense, era 1121. Froila founded this during the reign of the glorious King Adefonsus in León.
EDCS-38000107.summary: A consecration inscription for the temple of Saint James the Apostle by Bishop Ariane of Ourense, with a founding by Froila under King Adefonsus of León, dated to era 1121 (likely a post-Roman dating system).

EDCS-38000109.translation: In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, in era 989, Dominic the priest restored this basilica. O you priests and all men, pray for me.
EDCS-38000109.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a basilica by Dominic the priest, with a plea for prayers, dated to era 989 (likely a post-Roman dating system).

EDCS-38000110.translation: Lord Ramoranus Sescudo, soldier, pray for me, the entrusted star.
EDCS-38000110.summary: A personal dedication or plea for prayer from a soldier named Ramoranus Sescudo, likely from the post-Roman period.

EDCS-38000112.translation: In era 1014, thus entered Fredenandus the bishop.
EDCS-38000112.summary: An inscription recording the entry of Fredenandus the bishop, dated to era 1014 (likely a post-Roman dating system).

EDCS-38000113.translation: Adefonsus the Confessor ordered it to be renewed and restored, as it had been destroyed for a long time, and for such a labor, may Saint Martin be his helper and protector before God, so that he may have a good reward for this deed.
EDCS-38000113.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a building, commissioned by Adefonsus the Confessor, with a prayer for divine reward and protection from Saint Martin.

EDCS-38000114.translation: In this altar are deposited the relics of Saint Savior, Saint Mary, Saint Martin the bishop, Saint John the Baptist, Saint Andrew, Saint Tirsus, Saint Felix, Saint Romanus, Saint Eulalia, Saint Pelagius. This temple was restored by Adefonsus the Confessor on the fourth day before the Ides of October in the era 989.
EDCS-38000114.summary: This inscription records the deposition of relics and the restoration of a temple by Adefonsus the Confessor, dated to the era 989 (1051 AD).

EDCS-38000115.translation: I implore you, servants of God, who enter this tomb, do not hesitate to pray for me, Adefonsus, if you possess the kingdom of Christ without end. Here rests the servant of God Adefonsus the Confessor, who died on the fourth day, the sixth feria, before the Kalends of August in the era 1007.
EDCS-38000115.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Adefonsus the Confessor, who died on the fourth day, sixth feria, before the Kalends of August in the era 1007 (969 AD), asking for prayers from those who enter his tomb.

EDCS-38000116.translation: By this sign the pious is protected, by this sign the enemy is conquered. Adefonsus made this and may God save him.
EDCS-38000116.summary: This is a votive inscription by Adefonsus, likely related to a military context, expressing faith in divine protection.

EDCS-38000117.translation: Christ, Son of God, who sits at the right hand of the Father in the heavenly seat, grant me, Adefonsus, eternal rest.
EDCS-38000117.summary: This is a personal prayer inscription by Adefonsus, asking Christ for eternal rest.

EDCS-38000118.translation: Our Savior, who are salvation and resurrection and life, grant me, Adefonsus, confidence, so that my soul may be led before your judgment without confusion.
EDCS-38000118.summary: This is a personal prayer inscription by Adefonsus, asking Christ for confidence and salvation for his soul.

EDCS-38000124.translation: The servant of God Gunimirus died in the era 943.
EDCS-38000124.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription recording the death of Gunimirus, a servant of God, in the era 943 (905 AD).

EDCS-38000125.translation: In the name of the Lord. Eclare. Placed here in the head and body. Here rests the servant of God Arogontia the Confessed, and she died on the fourth feria, the Kalends of September, in the year one thousand and forty-four.
EDCS-38000125.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arogontia the Confessed, who died on the Kalends of September in the era 1044 (1006 AD), with a prayer or statement about her burial.

EDCS-38000128.translation: In the era 900, the road was made, giving glory to the Lord, by Froila with his brother Didacus.
EDCS-38000128.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road in the era 900 (862 AD) by Froila and his brother Didacus, attributing the glory to God.

EDCS-38000129.translation: In the era 1016. Elmismi memory. Corobes the abbot and Adulfus made this.
EDCS-38000129.summary: This inscription, dated to the era 1016 (978 AD), records a memorial or construction made by Corobes the abbot and Adulfus.

EDCS-38000131.translation: In the name of the Lord. I, Manuto.
EDCS-38000131.summary: A very short inscription with a personal name, likely a tomb inscription.

EDCS-38000132.translation: My name is Belatea, pray for me, a sinner.
EDCS-38000132.summary: A devotional epitaph where the deceased, Belatea, requests prayers.

EDCS-38000134.translation: In the name of God. I, Munio, son of M, in the year 1062.
EDCS-38000134.summary: An epitaph of Munio, dated by the era to 1062.

EDCS-38000135.translation: In the name of God. Here rests the servant of God Tulgas, who died the day before the Kalends of December in the era 1083. Whoever reads these letters, pray Our Father and Hail Mary, ever Virgin.
EDCS-38000135.summary: An epitaph of Tulgas, dated by the era 1083, requesting prayers from the reader.

EDCS-38000138.translation: Resting is blessed Eulalia, martyr of Christ, who suffered in the city of Barcelona under Dacian the governor on the 13th day before the Ides of February, and was found by Frodoino, bishop, with his clergy in the house of Saint Mary on the Kalends of November. Thanks be to God.
EDCS-38000138.summary: An inscription detailing the martyrdom and discovery of relics of Saint Eulalia in Barcelona, found by Bishop Frodoino.

EDCS-38000140.translation: Saint Paul, Saint Peter, this is the gate of the Lord. I am the way for all. Come, Richard sent VII Benedictines for himself and the soul of his wife Raimunda in this monastic hall.
EDCS-38000140.summary: An inscription likely from a monastery gate, mentioning Saint Paul and Saint Peter, and a donation by Richard for himself and his wife.

EDCS-38000141.translation: Beneath hard turf lies the body of Gescafred, a monk, once son of Seniofred and brother of Ramoni Bor, who died on the 6th day before the Ides of January in the era 987, in the year 939 of our Lord, in the 3rd year of King Ludoic. Intercede for him, blessed Cucufas. Amen.
EDCS-38000141.summary: An epitaph of the monk Gescafred, dated by consul era and regnal year, requesting intercession from Saint Cucufas.

EDCS-38000142.translation: In this sarcophagus lies the small body of the former Levanto, which was joined to the earth. Died on the 6th day before the Ides of June in the era 14 after the thousandth, 976 of our Lord, in the 22nd year of King Leutario. May God forgive him. Amen. Gomarellus P.
EDCS-38000142.summary: An epitaph within a sarcophagus for Levanto, dated by era and regnal year, with a prayer for forgiveness and signed by Gomarellus.

EDCS-38000149.translation: The authors of this work: Rodericus the bishop. Through this gate the way to heaven becomes passable for every faithful person. The angel, the great king of the sabbath, God, is here and is called the Lamb of God, the messenger. Behold the Lamb of God, John the Baptist. I am death, I am called death, I am the strong lion.
EDCS-38000149.summary: An inscription, possibly on a church or related to a bishop named Rodericus, featuring complex theological and symbolic language referencing Christ, John the Baptist, and death.

EDCS-38000150.translation: Here lies Ra-, Gaudiosa, wife of Rs, Pelagius.
EDCS-38000150.summary: A fragmentary epitaph identifying Gaudiosa as the wife of Pelagius.

EDCS-38000233.translation: Shamael, son of Hagai; Shamael, son of Hagai; Shamael, son of Hagai.
EDCS-38000233.summary: This is a trilingual inscription repeating the name Shamael, son of Hagai, possibly as a dedication or personal marker.

EDCS-38100001.summary: This is an imperial titulary, likely from the reign of Augustus.

EDCS-38100002.translation: To the departed spirits: Arrius to his wife, Airra; to the departed spirits: Placidus to his father, Allius, aged 70.
EDCS-38100002.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Airra, wife of Arrius, and Allius, father of Placidus.

EDCS-38100003.translation: To Frontonius, freedman of Ruppatianus, from V Rupina.
EDCS-38100003.summary: This is a dedication for Frontonius, a freedman, likely by his patroness.

EDCS-38100004.translation: To Reburinus, son of Boutius, aged 19.
EDCS-38100004.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Reburinus, aged 19.

EDCS-38100005.translation: To Rufinus, son of Rufus, aged 90.
EDCS-38100005.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Rufinus, aged 90.

EDCS-38100006.translation: Claudius Latturus Duillis fulfilled his vow willingly, deservedly, after a vision.
EDCS-38100006.summary: This is a votive inscription by Claudius Latturus Duillis, fulfilling a vow made after a divine vision.

EDCS-38100007.translation: A slave fulfilled his vow willingly, deservedly.
EDCS-38100007.summary: This is a votive inscription by a slave who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-38100008.translation: He took care to have the bronze made.
EDCS-38100008.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription indicating someone commissioned the creation of a bronze object.

EDCS-38100010.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a definitive summary, but appears to mention a 'true brother'.

EDCS-38100012.translation: To my most dutiful son-in-law, a soldier exempt from service.
EDCS-38100012.summary: An epitaph for a soldier exempt from service, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-38100014.translation: Aemilius, son of Titus. Aemilia, daughter of Marcus. Aemilius, son of Marcus. Aemilius, son of Marcus. Aemilius.
EDCS-38100014.summary: A fragmentary inscription listing members of the Aemilia family, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-38100284.translation: Arruntius placed this monument for Boutia, daughter of Cabrunus.
EDCS-38100284.summary: A funerary monument set up by Arruntius for Boutia, daughter of Cabrunus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-38100313.translation: Corn... (aged) 61 years, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-38100313.summary: An inscription recording the construction of something, possibly a public work or tomb, by an individual named Corn... (aged) 61, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-38100314.translation: Julia, daughter of Pintamus, (dedicated this) to her grandmother Pintamis of Peicana, after death.
EDCS-38100314.summary: A funerary dedication by Julia for her grandmother, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-38100315.summary: A dedication to Hercules, likely a votive offering, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-38100317.translation: For the well-being of Tiberius Claudius Anisus, Hermes freed his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-38100317.summary: A votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Hermes for the well-being of Tiberius Claudius Anisus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-38100318.translation: Rufus Grovius (made) a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-38100318.summary: A votive inscription recording a vow made by Rufus Grovius to Jupiter, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-38100319.translation: Sacred to Emperor Augustus, son of the divine (Julius Caesar), publicly... ulius.
EDCS-38100319.summary: A public dedication to Emperor Augustus, dating to his reign (27 BC - 14 AD).

EDCS-38100322.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Circeiana, daughter of Arron, aged 75, lies here.
EDCS-38100322.summary: A tomb inscription for Circeiana, daughter of Arron, who died at the age of 75, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-38700054.translation: Alepius departed in the name of Christ at the age of 18, ten days before the Kalends of November, in the year 522.
EDCS-38700054.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Alepius at age 18 in the year 522.

EDCS-38700055.translation: Avitus died at the age of 38.
EDCS-38700055.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Avitus at age 38.

EDCS-38700057.translation: Savinus at the age of twenty-two.
EDCS-38700057.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Savinus' age at death as twenty-two.

EDCS-38700058.translation: In the name of the Lord, savior of the world, here are the relics of Saint Romanus the monk, Saint Martin the bishop, Saint Marina the virgin, Saint Peter the apostle, Saint John the Baptist, Saint Acisclus, and of a number of other saints.
EDCS-38700058.summary: This inscription lists the relics of various saints housed in this location.

EDCS-38700059.translation: This stone, the ashes of Peter, receives the soul. Petra made this division for herself by Christ in the year 4.
EDCS-38700059.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph, possibly a dedication by a woman named Petra.

EDCS-38700061.translation: Flainus the abbot offers in honor of the holy apostles of God Peter and Paul.
EDCS-38700061.summary: This is a dedication by Flainus the abbot in honor of the apostles Peter and Paul.

EDCS-38700062.translation: May the servant of God, Iustus, rest here in the year 3, seven days before the Kalends of November.
EDCS-38700062.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iustus, a servant of God, with a date in the year 3.

EDCS-38700063.translation: I am Felis. I will pass by in the year 10.
EDCS-38700063.summary: This is a brief inscription, likely funerary, mentioning 'Felis' and a date in the year 10.

EDCS-38700065.translation: Moses, Aaron, Incratius, Zo, Zaco, Peter, Paul, Facceus, Xustus, Izo, Aaron, Incratius, Peter, Floria, Paul, Syrophoenissa, Incarnatio, Peter, Mary, Paul, the blind Galilean, and eighteen other martyrs; pray for us.
EDCS-38700065.summary: This inscription lists numerous names, likely of saints and martyrs, with a plea for prayers.

EDCS-38700066.translation: In the name of the Lord, Benenatus, son of Cit, this monument.
EDCS-38700066.summary: This is a monumental inscription recording Benenatus, son of Cit.

EDCS-38700071.summary: This inscription records a royal offering, likely in a religious context, by King Reccesvinthus.

EDCS-38700072.translation: In the name of God, Sonnica offers (this) to Saint Mary in Sorbaces.
EDCS-38700072.summary: This inscription records a religious offering by Sonnica to Saint Mary at a place called Sorbaces.

EDCS-38700074.translation: The abbot Theodosius offers a small gift to Saint Stephen.
EDCS-38700074.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Abbot Theodosius to Saint Stephen.

EDCS-38700075.translation: In the name of the Lord, in the name of the Saint, Bishop Lucetius offers (this).
EDCS-38700075.summary: This inscription records a religious offering by Bishop Lucetius, invoking the name of God and a saint.

EDCS-38700077.translation: Here are the tombs of the holy priests: I.D. Bishop Nigrinus, Bishop Sefronius.
EDCS-38700077.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of two holy priests, Bishops Nigrinus and Sefronius.

EDCS-38700084.translation: Syagrys, servant of Christ, lived and was buried in peace for more or less X days before the Kalends of March.
EDCS-38700084.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and burial of Syagrys, a servant of Christ, with a date indicated by the Roman calendar.

EDCS-38700087.translation: Euticius, priest of the city of Albitanae, is buried in this place.
EDCS-38700087.summary: This inscription marks the burial site of Euticius, a priest from the city of Albitanae.

EDCS-38700088.translation: Agila, priest of God; Dexter, priest; Froila; Gamius; Gamio; Lautarius; Reswentus; Hunyruc; Sallomon; Sena; Widigelus; Zerimundo.
EDCS-38700088.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly related to a religious community or church, including two priests.

EDCS-38700121.translation: In this tomb rests the servant of God Hermenegildus, who died on the fifth day of the week, the Nones of November, in the era 981; brothers and sisters, pray for us.
EDCS-38700121.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Hermenegildus, a servant of God, with a specific date and a plea for prayers from his brethren.

EDCS-38700122.translation: It is fitting that a bright life should come to the altar; let him offer for the crime of his life so that the people may be gentle. For the honor of the Holy Savior of Cellenova, Bishop Rudesindus and Abbot Petrus ordered me to be made. Here is poured forth blood by which the serpent's venom is expelled. For the honor of the Holy Trinity, the memory of Iohannes. Grant peace, Lord, in our days, for there is no other who...
EDCS-38700122.summary: This inscription, likely from a religious building, records its commission by Bishop Rudesindus and Abbot Petrus in honor of the Holy Savior and Holy Trinity, and includes poetic verses with religious and possibly symbolic meaning.

EDCS-38700124.translation: You, God, are believed to be the author of this work; forgive my sins, Christ, to all praying here. Your present memory urges on your unworthy servant Froila, who died and is joined with you in the Lord. Good people, as you read, say that you may remember me, a sinner, in your holy prayers.
EDCS-38700124.summary: This inscription is a prayer and epitaph for a deceased individual named Froila, likely from a church context.

EDCS-38700125.translation: Here lies the servant of God, P. Bigili, a priest, who began this church.
EDCS-38700125.summary: This is an epitaph for the priest P. Bigili, recording that he initiated the construction of a church.

EDCS-38700126.translation: Here rests in a blessed fate, Naustia, bishop and priest, joyful for the heavens, with a devout mind he brought his pontifical fullness to the Conimbriga see for 45 years, resting in this tomb on the 10th day before the Kalends of December, in the year 950.
EDCS-38700126.summary: This is an epitaph for Bishop Naustia, recording his death date and his long tenure in the see of Conimbriga.

EDCS-38700128.translation: Here rests the servant of God, Hintila, who died on the 10th day before the Kalends of January in the year 928.
EDCS-38700128.summary: This is an epitaph for Hintila, recording his death date.

EDCS-38700130.translation: With God's help, Frankila the abbot founded this work in the year 956.
EDCS-38700130.summary: This inscription records the founding of a work (likely a building or religious institution) by Abbot Frankila.

EDCS-38700132.translation: Behold the Lord of lords, and the gates of heaven: the church, spread out and not divided, of the mother of the Lord, Saint Mary. In her honor, Lord Virmundus the king ordered this to be made in the year 1024.
EDCS-38700132.summary: This inscription dedicates a church to Saint Mary and records its commission by King Virmundus.

EDCS-38700133.translation: Let it be an example to all that the venerable temple was given by King Ordonius, who lies here, this pious man. He made this seat, which he had previously made an aedicule for the virgin, who shines in the pontificate; he sustained it with gifts, through her the city of Legion shines. We beg therefore, by God's grace, may He pardon him. Amen. This king, following the footsteps of his father King Alfonsus, prudently and justly governs the kingdom. He captured Talavera and routed the Arabs at the castle of Saint Stephen, and subjugated to himself the provinces of Lusitania and Baetica, the land of the Arabs which is called Sincilla, he subdued with great slaughter. He began Anagara and its vicariate, and in the eighth year of his reign, with six months completed, struck by illness at Zamora, he departed from this world in the year 932.
EDCS-38700133.summary: This epitaph commemorates King Ordonius, detailing his pious acts, military achievements in capturing cities and defeating Arabs, his just rule, and the circumstances of his death.

EDCS-38700134.translation: This hall of Christ, dedicated in the name of Saints Adrian and Natalia, was built by the servant of God, Gisando, with his wife Levvina, in the year 958. May your purest vow, O Lord, be acceptable to you, which your servants have prepared for you with eager devotion in honor of your witnesses. May the prayers of the miserable be received by you, pious God. Whoever enters here sad, let him return from here happier from prayer. The temple was consecrated by the bishops Cixila, named Frunimius, and Fortis, on the 4th day before the Ides of October in the year 958.
EDCS-38700134.summary: This inscription dedicates a church hall to Saints Adrian and Natalia, records its builders Gisando and Levvina, and mentions its consecration by bishops.

EDCS-38700135.translation: Whoever enters this hall of God without good intentions, neither vows nor gifts are of avail. Therefore, let those entering set aside evil intentions. Here lie the sacred bones of two holy martyrs, through whose virtue God performs many miracles daily. In the hall of our Lord Jesus Christ, the holy savior, reigning Lord Ramiro, king of Galicia, Ermenegildo the abbot, as if unworthy, under the grace of Christ God, Sisnando the bishop, Gino made it in the year 1018. Dominicus noted it.
EDCS-38700135.summary: This inscription records the relics of two martyrs housed in a hall of God and mentions its construction during the reign of King Ramiro by Abbot Ermenegildo and Bishop Sisnando.

EDCS-38700136.translation: The praiseworthy, blessed Fructuosus, after founding the monastery of Complutum under the name of Saint Peter, made a small oratory in this place. After him, Valerius, no less in merit, expanded the work of the church. Most recently, Gennadius the priest, with twelve brothers, restored it in the year 933, having become pontiff. He raised it again from the foundations as beautifully as can be seen, not by oppression of the populace, but by the generosity of price and the labor of the brothers of this monastery. This temple was consecrated by four bishops: Gennadius of Astorga, Sabarius of Dumio, Frunimius of Leon, and Dulcidius of Salamanca, under the year 914, on the 9th day before the Kalends of November.
EDCS-38700136.summary: This inscription details the history of a monastery, recounting its founding by Fructuosus, expansion by Valerius, restoration by Gennadius, and its final consecration by four bishops.

EDCS-38700138.summary: A private inscription recording the maker of an object, identified as Silo, a chief.

EDCS-38700139.translation: May this, undertaken with good will, remain in honor of God; Adefonsus, humble servant of Christ, offers it. Whoever dares to take this away from me shall perish by divine lightning, unless he gives it willingly of his own volition. This work was completed in the year 816 (of the Spanish Era). By this sign, the pious is protected; by this sign, the enemy is conquered.
EDCS-38700139.summary: A votive inscription by Adefonsus, a servant of Christ, dedicated to God, likely marking a religious building or object, and dated to 816 (778 AD).

EDCS-38700140.translation: Renimirus the king, of divine memory, died on the Kalends of February in the year 888 (of the Spanish Era). May all of you who are about to read this pray for his repose.
EDCS-38700140.summary: An epitaph for King Renimirus, recording his death date as February 1st in the year 888 (850 AD) and requesting prayers for his soul.

EDCS-38700141.translation: May this, undertaken with good will, remain in honor of the Lord; the servants of Christ, Adefonsus the chief and Scemena the queen, offer it. Whoever dares to bring harm to these our offerings shall perish by divine lightning. This work was completed and granted to Saint Savior of the see of Oviedo. By this sign, the pious is protected; by this sign, the enemy is conquered. It was worked on in the castle of Gauzon in the twelfth year of our reign, in the year 916 (of the Spanish Era).
EDCS-38700141.summary: A dedication inscription by Prince Adefonsus and Queen Scemena for Saint Savior of Oviedo, likely for a building or altar, dated to 916 (878 AD).

EDCS-38700142.translation: This royal tomb collects Ramiro, king, descended from noble blood, son of Adephonsus. He died the day before the Kalends of April in the year 967 (of the Spanish Era).
EDCS-38700142.summary: An epitaph for King Ramiro, son of Adephonsus, recording his death date as March 31st in the year 967 (929 AD).

EDCS-38700143.translation: Ordonius, that prince whom fame will speak of, and to whom, I believe, no equal will the ages bring; mighty in counsel and in warlike deeds, and all-powerful. May He not render your due to your sins. He died on the sixth day before the Kalends of June in the year 904 (of the Spanish Era).
EDCS-38700143.summary: An epitaph for Prince Ordonius, recording his death date as May 27th in the year 904 (866 AD) and praising his qualities.

EDCS-38700144.translation: In honor of Saint James the Apostle, the servants of God offer it: Adefonsus the chief with his wife Scemena the queen. By this sign, the pious is protected. By this sign, the enemy is conquered. This work was completed in the year 900 (of the Spanish Era), the twelfth.
EDCS-38700144.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Saint James the Apostle by Prince Adefonsus and Queen Scemena, dated to 900 (862 AD).

EDCS-38700145.translation: Place, O Lord, a sign of salvation in these houses, so that you may not permit the destroying angel to enter in the name of Christ. Adefonsus the chief and his wife Scemena decreed that this hall be built in the year 913 (of the Spanish Era).
EDCS-38700145.summary: A dedication inscription by Prince Adefonsus and Queen Scemena for a hall they built, dated to 913 (875 AD), invoking divine protection.

EDCS-38700146.translation: Here rests Urraca the queen, servant of God, and wife of Lord Ranimirus the chief. She died on the second feria (Monday), the eleventh hour, the ninth day before the Kalends of July in the year 964 (of the Spanish Era).
EDCS-38700146.summary: An epitaph for Queen Urraca, wife of Prince Ranimirus, recording her death date as June 23rd in the year 964 (926 AD).

EDCS-38700147.translation: Paulus, Petrus, Iohannes, Iacobus, Andreas, Iacobus, Sanctus, Simon, Sanctus Thadeus, Thomas, Philipus, Bartolomeus, Mateus, Michael, the Cherubim, the Seraphim. Ascending on high, Christ led captivity captive. Michael the archangel fighting with the Dragon. Iohannes, Petrus, Iacobus, Andreas, Philipus, the Evangelist, Mateus, and Bartolomeus, Thomas. Sun, Moon, Ihesus Nazarenus, King of the Jews. Saint Mary. Iohannes the Baptist. Dismas the robber, Liispas the robber. Let the whole assembly of the people worthy of God know that here are venerated the glorious relics: namely, from the wood of the cross of the Lord, from His garment which was divided by lot, from His tomb and most holy shroud and blood, from the Holy Land which He trod with pious footsteps, from the garments of His mother Mary, and from Her milk which is very wondrous. Joined with these are also certain of the most outstanding saints, whose names we have subscribed as best we could: that is, of Saint Peter, of Saint Thomas, of Saint Bartholomew, of the bones of the Prophets, of all the Apostles, and of very many other saints whose names only God's knowledge gathers. The excellent King Adefonsus, endowed with humble devotion, made this reliquary for the holy relics, gilded with silver, adorned outside with not inexpensive works, through which, after his life, he may purchase fellowship with them in heaven and be aided by the prayers of the saints. For this indeed is salvation and restoration, as every province in the land knows without doubt. And therefore, our hands and the industry of the clerics and bishops who have gathered for this, with the said Prince Adefonsus and with his most joyful sister named Urraca, to whom the Redeemer of all may grant indulgence and pardon of their sins through these most holy relics of the Apostles and of Saint Justus and Pastor, Cosmas and Damian, and Saint Eulalia, virgin, Martin, Maximinus, Germanus, Baubilius, Pantaleon, Cyprian and Justina, Facundus and Primitivus, Christopher, Cucufatus, Felix, Sulpicius.
EDCS-38700147.summary: A monumental inscription detailing a reliquary constructed by King Adefonsus and his sister Urraca, listing numerous saintly relics contained within, and invoking divine favor and salvation for the donors.

EDCS-38700148.translation: May this devotion to God remain peacefully in this hour, which the servants of Christ Froila and Nunlo, surnamed Scemena, offer; this work has been completed and granted to Saint Savior of Oviedo. Whoever takes away these our gifts from Simseritelmine shall perish. In the year 946.
EDCS-38700148.summary: This inscription records a donation of a work by Froila and Nunlo to Saint Savior of Oviedo, dated to 946.

EDCS-38700149.translation: This tomb collects Ramiro, the king, son of Alfonso, born of royal blood. He died on the day before the Kalends of April in the year 967.
EDCS-38700149.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of King Ramiro, son of Alfonso, in 967.

EDCS-38700150.translation: Beware the stones that enclose him whom you see here, within this sacred chamber, resting beloved by God, Taraisa, dedicated to Christ, born of noble parentage, daughter of King Veremund and Queen Geloyra, more illustrious by merit, she led a splendid life. As the rule holds, if you wish to be good, may you imitate this example. She died on the 7th day before the Kalends of May, on the 4th feria, at the middle of the night, in the year 1077, after the completion of the ages of the world in order. Christ, grant forgiveness, I pray, pardon, amen.
EDCS-38700150.summary: This epitaph for Taraisa, daughter of King Veremund and Queen Geloyra, commemorates her life and death in 1077.

EDCS-38700151.translation: In the name of God, our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, and of all his saints, of the glorious Holy Virgin Mary, and the twelve apostles, and other holy martyrs, for whose honor a temple was built in this place, in Oviedo, by the late religious Prince Adefonsus, and from his departure until now, the fourth from his lineage, succeeding in the kingdom by a similar name, Adefonsus, son of King Ordoño of divine memory, decreed the construction of this fortification, with his wife Scemena and their two children, as a refuge for the treasury hall of this holy church, to remain unharmed, so that it may not appear that anything is lost, should the pirates of the naval gentile attack with their army. This work, offered by us, is granted by eternal right to the same church.
EDCS-38700151.summary: This inscription details the construction of a fortification in Oviedo by Prince Adefonsus, son of King Ordoño, and his family, for the protection of a church treasury, likely during a period of potential pirate threats.

EDCS-38700152.translation: In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, Bishop Gundisalvus ordered me to be made. These are the relics.
EDCS-38700152.summary: This inscription, commissioned by Bishop Gundisalvus, states that it contains relics.

EDCS-38700153.translation: May your abundant piety, O God, shine forth, where you save and often confess the impious. The multitudes of men everywhere applaud, confessing these things; may you be favorable to the wretched, pardon beyond merit, O good one, through the clemency by which you prevail. Be favorable to the wretched, for dire deaths crush me, and guilt tortures me. May your fruitful grace now shine forth, O merciful one, which may lift up the crushed. May your piety now shine forth, may it assist and protect all under heavenly protection, saving them.
