EDCS-56800025.summary: This funerary monument was erected by family members for Lucius Valerius Tempestivus and others, with a complex arrangement of inheritance and financial contributions, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-56800026.translation: To Lucius Lucretius Montanus, Seviri Augustalis, magistrate of the Lares, Lucius Lucretius [dedicates this].
EDCS-56800026.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Lucretius to Lucius Lucretius Montanus, a Seviri Augustalis and magistrate of the Lares, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-56800027.translation: [For Gaius Sempronius], magistrate of the Lares, Gaius Sempronius Diophanes, freedman, [dedicates this] to his patron and himself.
EDCS-56800027.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Gaius Sempronius Diophanes, freedman, to his patron Gaius Sempronius and himself, likely as a freedman honoring his patron and securing his own memory, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-56800028.translation: To Lucius Helvidius Arta, freedman of Lucius, magistrate of the college, and of Anui, freedwoman of Quintus, by testament of Marcus T...
EDCS-56800028.summary: This inscription records a testamentary dedication to Lucius Helvidius Arta, who served as magistrate of a college, and to Anui, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-56800030.translation: To Fabia Colenda, by Eros, her freedman.
EDCS-56800030.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Eros, freedman of Fabia Colenda, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-56800032.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription with a partial dedication, the full context and date are indeterminate.

EDCS-56800039.translation: To Lucius Quadra, son of Polyclidus.
EDCS-56800039.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, likely for a person named Lucius Quadra, son of Polyclidus.

EDCS-56800074.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Aemilius Achillaeus ordered this to be written for himself while alive. Thus I was not; then I was; now I am not.
EDCS-56800074.summary: This is a self-commemorative funerary epitaph of Gaius Aemilius Achillaeus, expressing reflections on life and death.

EDCS-56800078.translation: To Annia, daughter of Nysus Proculus, [aged] ... years.
EDCS-56800078.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Annia, whose father was Nysus Proculus.

EDCS-56800080.translation: Flavius made this for his wife.
EDCS-56800080.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Flavius commissioned a monument for his wife.

EDCS-56800085.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely funerary, dedicated to Urbana, a freedwoman.

EDCS-56800086.translation: In memory of Cissia, daughter of Messimus.
EDCS-56800086.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Cissia, daughter of Messimus.

EDCS-56800093.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Gaudentius, dearest son, who lived 13 years, 11 months, and ... days.
EDCS-56800093.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaudentius, a son who died at the age of 13 years, 11 months, and some days.

EDCS-56800094.translation: [Fragmentary text, possibly related to a Gel/diacus family.]
EDCS-56800094.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription with very little legible text, making its purpose unclear.

EDCS-56800097.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Marcus Horatius.

EDCS-56800099.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Iunia Iocista.

EDCS-56800104.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iunia Cyriace [erected this] for her husband Minicius Festivus, who deserved well, who lived 41 years and 15 days. To my sweetest Panprepius.
EDCS-56800104.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her deceased husband, with a mention of another person or a pet.

EDCS-56800107.translation: [To my] beautiful [daughter], aged 3 years. [To my] sweetest daughter.
EDCS-56800107.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young daughter, likely from a parent.

EDCS-56800109.translation: [Son] of Marcus, Secund[us]. [Daughter] of Marcus, Tertul[la]. And you...
EDCS-56800109.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of a dedication or funerary text mentioning two individuals and possibly addressing a third.

EDCS-56800115.translation: Gaius Tettius, son of Gaius, Herenul[...]
EDCS-56800115.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription potentially recording the name of a Roman citizen and his father, possibly for a funerary or commemorative purpose.

EDCS-56800147.translation: Lucius, son [of X], by his will.
EDCS-56800147.summary: This inscription fragment indicates a bequest or disposition made by a person named Lucius through his will.

EDCS-56800148.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that seems to mention a freedman of someone named Marcus.

EDCS-57900008.translation: Lucius Turellius Lucius filius Geminus, aedile, [erected this] at his own expense for Drusus Caesar, son of Tiberius, grandson of Augustus, great-grandson of the divine [Augustus].
EDCS-57900008.summary: This is a public dedication by an aedile for a member of the imperial family, likely dating to the late 1st century BC or early 1st century AD.

EDCS-57900011.translation: Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus Traianus Germanicus, high priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, restored [this].
EDCS-57900011.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a public work by Emperor Trajan, likely during his second consulship.

EDCS-57900077.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning a name that begins with 'Lucius Fu-'.

EDCS-57900080.summary: This funerary epitaph fragment records the age and possibly the name or origin of the deceased.

EDCS-58100096.translation: To Gaius Ampius Fir, for his well-deserved service.
EDCS-58100096.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Ampius Fir, likely for a service he rendered.

EDCS-58100099.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Nico, invoking the divine spirits of the underworld.

EDCS-58100102.translation: To the Divine Manes. Wounded in the Sertorian War, he was immediately struck down by a Calagurritan. In order to regain his health, he vowed to Aesculapius at Petti in Barcino that a temple be built. He intended to erect one, but death intervened prematurely, depriving him of both health and fame, and pitifully abandoning the youth. He was a cavalry officer, son of Publius Pompeianus.
EDCS-58100102.summary: This is a complex funerary dedication commemorating a young cavalry officer who died from wounds sustained in the Sertorian War and a subsequent illness, with his father vowing a temple to Aesculapius.

EDCS-58100103.translation: I, Gaius Valerius Castritius, son of Quintus, today on the third day before the Ides of August, as I die, establish my son, Valerius, my heir, to the extent of my entire estate, provided that my dearest daughter, Valeria, is provided for, if he has offspring from my wife Fasbiana, and also provided that the first tower of the temple of Aesculapius, the god, which is in the city of Barcino, is restored. This was acted under Publius Romulius Cit, Proconsul, and during the reign of Domitian, son of Vespasian, the Emperor.
EDCS-58100103.summary: This is a will inscribed on stone by Gaius Valerius Castritius, who leaves his estate to his son on the condition that his daughter and the temple of Aesculapius in Barcino are cared for.

EDCS-58100104.translation: To Diana Invicta Augusta, preserver of the most pious and August colony of Iulia Faventina Barcinonensis. Publius Pupius Latinus, son of Publius, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, from Faventia, faithfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-58100104.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Diana by a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion for the protection of the colony of Barcino.

EDCS-58100105.translation: To the August genius, preserver of the most pious and August colony of Iulia Faventia Barcinonensis. Marcus Gavius Salluus, son of Marcus, of the tribe 'Tromen', a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from Faventia, faithfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-58100105.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the genius of the emperor by a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion for the protection of the colony of Barcino.

EDCS-58100106.translation: For the August virtue and for the colony of Iulia Faventia Barcinonensis. Quintus Hirius Lepidus, son of Quintus, of the tribe 'Tromen', a scout of the Third Augustan Legion, dedicated and consecrated this.
EDCS-58100106.summary: This is a dedication by Quintus Hirius Lepidus, a scout of the Third Augustan Legion, to the virtue of the emperor and the colony of Barcino.

EDCS-58100113.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger dedication or epitaph, mentioning individuals named Titus Octavius and Quintus Augur Caius.

EDCS-58200225.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to a son.

EDCS-58800028.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. This monument was erected by Ai[a] to her most pious mother, Alla Calpurnia, daughter of [?].
EDCS-58800028.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Ai[a] for her mother, Alla Calpurnia.

EDCS-59500202.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording a dedication or a funerary notice, possibly of a person named Saecuntos.

EDCS-59500209.translation: Ambatus, aged 60 years, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-59500209.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ambatus, stating his age and including a common wish for the deceased.

EDCS-59500213.translation: Marcus Roscies gave (this) to Salaeco, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-59500213.summary: This inscription records a dedication or gift made by Marcus Roscies to his freedman Salaeco.

EDCS-59500364.translation: Meidutius, son of Mebd, is buried here.
EDCS-59500364.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for Meidutius, son of Mebd.

EDCS-59500365.translation: Albinus, son of Quadratus, arranged for (this monument) to be made for his wife, tinanum, who is buried here.
EDCS-59500365.summary: This funerary inscription records that Albinus commissioned a monument for his wife, tinanum.

EDCS-59500366.translation: Maxumus, son of Albinus, aged 6 years; Albinia, daughter of Albinus, aged 15 years; Albinus, grandson, aged 4 years.
EDCS-59500366.summary: This inscription lists the ages of three individuals, likely family members, possibly as part of a funerary monument or record.

EDCS-59500367.translation: Ugeta Maconia fulfilled (her vow) to Luatis, as was deserved.
EDCS-59500367.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow made by Ugeta Maconia to the deity Luatis.

EDCS-60300022.summary: This is a brief inscription, likely funerary, stating the name and age of Ambatia.

EDCS-60300023.translation: Caburena, daughter of Tritus, aged 10 years.
EDCS-60300023.summary: This is a brief inscription, likely funerary, stating the name, parentage, and age of Caburena.

EDCS-60300087.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name Titus Valerius and possibly his age, likely from a funerary context.

EDCS-60300089.translation: To Titus Valerius, son of Titus, and to Aelia T... .
EDCS-60300089.summary: This is a fragmented inscription possibly recording a dedication to Titus Valerius and Aelia T...

EDCS-60300091.translation: Firmus, son of C. N. V. N. E. .
EDCS-60300091.summary: This is a fragmented inscription recording the name Firmus and his parentage or affiliation.

EDCS-60300100.translation: To Lucius Helvidius, freedman of Lucius, master of the Vergilia college; from the legacy of Quinta, freedwoman of Arta, of Marcus Titus.
EDCS-60300100.summary: This is a funerary dedication recorded on a monument, specifying the legatees and the testator.

EDCS-60300139.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Laelia, aged...
EDCS-60300139.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Departed) for Laelia.

EDCS-60300140.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Fausta, from Isipone.
EDCS-60300140.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' for Valeria Fausta, indicating her origin.

EDCS-60300161.summary: This inscription likely honors Gnaeus Magnus, possibly referring to the emperor Pompey the Great.

EDCS-60300212.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely a dedication or funerary monument to Julia Marcellina.

EDCS-60300214.translation: Flavinus erected this altar to his son Flavus, aged more or less...
EDCS-60300214.summary: This is a funerary monument where Flavinus dedicates an altar to his deceased son, Flavus.

EDCS-60300241.summary: This is a simple dedication or maker's inscription by Gaius Julius.

EDCS-60300257.translation: Felix Placidus, live and hunt for many years.
EDCS-60300257.summary: This is a 'vive et ama' (live and love) type inscription, wishing Felix Placidus a long life of enjoyment, possibly hunting.

EDCS-60300258.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Navara by Anna.

EDCS-60300260.translation: To the god Lar E Berberus, I have set up an altar for (my) health.
EDCS-60300260.summary: This is a dedication to the god Lar E Berberus, likely by a private individual seeking good health.

EDCS-60300262.translation: To the god Lar ERE, an altar.
EDCS-60300262.summary: This is a very brief dedication to the god Lar ERE, recording the erection of an altar.

EDCS-60300266.translation: Cladus Mentonianus won. (Gladiator) Hoplomachus. Mentonianus won.
EDCS-60300266.summary: This is a victory inscription for a gladiator named Claudius Mentonianus, likely from a tombstone or arena record.

EDCS-60300273.translation: In the consulship of Senecio and Apollinaris, Gaius Tautius Semanus, the aedile, [dedicated this].
EDCS-60300273.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Gaius Tautius Semanus, an aedile, during the consulship of Senecio and Apollinaris.

EDCS-60300290.translation: Of Quintus Vireius, son of Caius.
EDCS-60300290.summary: This is a simple ownership or inheritance inscription referring to Quintus Vireius.

EDCS-60300322.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Urbicus, aged 3 years. Urbica his mother and Eugenius his father buried (him) here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-60300322.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young child named Urbicus, set up by his parents.

EDCS-60300436.summary: This appears to be a fragment of a larger inscription, possibly referring to ownership or dedication by Tiberius Claudius Amiantus.

EDCS-60300447.translation: To Iov(e) and through [---] to his wife, aged 54 years, and to Celsus, son of [---].
EDCS-60300447.summary: This fragmented inscription is likely a dedication, possibly funerary, mentioning a wife and a son named Celsus.

EDCS-60600006.translation: Serva, wife of H(---) T(---) O(---) E(---).
EDCS-60600006.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record the name and familial connection of a woman named Serva.

EDCS-62900060.translation: Place the slave of Eutychus here
EDCS-62900060.summary: A funerary inscription likely indicating the burial place of a slave owned by Eutychus.

EDCS-62900061.translation: Place the slave of Eutychus here
EDCS-62900061.summary: A funerary inscription likely indicating the burial place of a slave owned by Eutychus.

EDCS-62900062.translation: The boys of Eutychus, wear it
EDCS-62900062.summary: An inscription possibly related to a group of boys belonging to Eutychus, the meaning of 'gere' is uncertain without further context.

EDCS-62900204.summary: An ownership or origin marker, identifying an individual named Cinna from Castulo.

EDCS-62900208.translation: Bathe [him] in the year of Consul Domitianus, XVI
EDCS-62900208.summary: An instruction possibly related to a ritual bath, dated by the consulship of Domitianus, potentially from the 1st century AD.

EDCS-62900209.translation: Farewell, wife; farewell, sons; farewell, Tibur, my last homeland; these are the decrees of the dying Q. Laelius: my house in the suburbs and my estate touching the city walls are to be the home of Marcellina, my faithful wife, and Publius and Quintus, my most beloved sons; may they be my co-heirs.
EDCS-62900209.summary: A funerary inscription with testamentary wishes from Q. Laelius, dedicating his suburban property to his wife and sons.

EDCS-62900210.translation: To L. Baebia Romana, noble, from Pardus of Saguntum, to his dearest friend and his best benefactor.
EDCS-62900210.summary: A dedication from Pardus of Saguntum to his friend L. Baebia Romana, likely a funerary or honorary epigram.

EDCS-62900211.translation: Traveler, if you have any piety, turn your gaze here; here lie the ashes of C. Canius, the poet, who was dear to all in Rome until his fourth Olympiad; afterward, having returned to Hispania, he harmed no one; when he wished to return to Latium to join his old friends, cruel fate snatched him away too soon, and he died on the journey; Albinus, his subordinate from Hispania, covered him with this marble.
EDCS-62900211.summary: A funerary epitaph for the poet C. Canius, erected by his subordinate Albinus, detailing his life and travels between Rome and Hispania.

EDCS-62900212.translation: I, M. Aelius Patritius, servant of Christ, lived 31 years. He rests in perpetual peace of God on the 6th day before the Kalends of July, in the year 508 [of the Spanish Era, i.e., 470 AD].
EDCS-62900212.summary: A Christian funerary inscription for M. Aelius Patritius, dating to 470 AD.

EDCS-63000137.summary: A boundary marker, likely indicating a limit or division related to imperial property or authority.

EDCS-64000113.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. To Valerius Severinus.
EDCS-64000113.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Valerius Severinus, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100261.translation: Lucia Obidoqum, wife of Accon, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you. Acco Obidoqum, son of Valerius, lies here, aged 40. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-64100261.summary: This is a double funerary inscription for Lucia Obidoqum and her husband Acco Obidoqum, providing their names, relationships, and ages, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100264.translation: Titus Irrico, son of Rufus, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-64100264.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Titus Irrico fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64100280.translation: Space 60 feet long, 5 feet wide. For the use of Opius.
EDCS-64100280.summary: This inscription appears to mark out a plot of land, possibly for burial or another purpose, with dimensions and a potential owner or user, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500434.summary: This fragmented inscription mentions a possible VIvir Augustalis, a civic or religious official, with illegible parts, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500438.summary: This very fragmentary inscription possibly refers to the Emperor, with illegible preceding and succeeding text, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64600128.translation: Of Flavia Demetriana, of Flavia Demetriana, of Annia Cassia, of Annia Cassia.
EDCS-64600128.summary: This inscription appears to be a repetition of names, possibly related to inheritance or ownership, with illegible context, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64600290.translation: Publius Longus fulfills his vow to the Ogeresii willingly and deservedly, and to the Boric Gates.
EDCS-64600290.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Publius Longus fulfilling a vow to the Ogeresii and the Boric Gates, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64600291.translation: Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power.
EDCS-64600291.summary: This inscription records titles of Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, including his role as pontifex maximus and his tribunician power, dating to his reign (96-98 AD).

EDCS-64600292.summary: This is a funerary inscription, possibly marking a tomb or offering for a person named Julia.

EDCS-64600327.translation: Lucius Sempronius Ceuis [states]: from this corner, here, this is how many feet [it measures]: 11 3/4 asuncia, 9 1/2 asuncia, 13 3/4 asuncia, 13 1/4 asuncia.
EDCS-64600327.summary: This inscription appears to be a land measurement record or boundary marker, detailing distances in fractional units of asuncia.

EDCS-64600362.translation: Felixs of the Ambiani, aged 4, is buried here.
EDCS-64600362.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of a young boy named Felixs.

EDCS-64600379.translation: Marcus Publius Roscius, son of Marcus, of the Maecia tribe.
EDCS-64600379.summary: This is a Roman citizen's inscription, likely part of a longer record such as a tombstone or a formal dedication, identifying Marcus Publius Roscius by name, father, and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-64600423.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, Imperator for the 7th time, Father of the Fatherland, Consul, Proconsul, and to Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, Germanicus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, son of our Lord Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, for roads and bridges.
EDCS-64600423.summary: This is a monumental inscription dedicating public works (roads and bridges) to Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximinus Junior, likely dating to their joint rule between 236-238 AD.

EDCS-64600438.translation: Firmus, son of C, and N, and E.
EDCS-64600438.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of a person, Firmus, and the names of his father and possibly other relatives, though the relationships are unclear.

EDCS-64600488.translation: To Avelco, son of Vanutus, aged 60.
EDCS-64600488.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Avelco, son of Vanutus, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-64600489.translation: To Ao, son of Esalai, aged 25.
EDCS-64600489.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Ao, son of Esalai, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-64600520.summary: This inscription is a simple Christian declaration of faith, possibly an epitaph or devotional marker.

EDCS-64600524.summary: This inscription from an unknown location records the service of Titus Aelius Domitianus as agoranomos, likely during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-65000083.translation: Paebion, a slave, here, aged 13. May the earth lie lightly on you. Caius Pri Ma saw to its construction.
EDCS-65000083.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a 13-year-old slave named Paebion, commissioned by Caius Pri Ma.

EDCS-65600125.translation: Tiberius Iunius Quadratus, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, from Rome.
EDCS-65600125.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying Tiberius Iunius Quadratus, son of Tiberius, from Rome.

EDCS-65800001.translation: To the departed spirits. C. Flaminius paid willingly for the bridge, the street, the litabrum.
EDCS-65800001.summary: This inscription records C. Flaminius paying for a bridge, street, and possibly a ceremonial object.

EDCS-65800068.translation: Aprilis, to his son Vitalis, aged 16.
EDCS-65800068.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Aprilis for his son Vitalis, who died at age 16.

EDCS-65800069.translation: To Iunius Aemilianus, son of Nero, of the Quirina tribe, aged 25. Cnaeus Larus made it.

EDCS-65800070.translation: To the Divine Shades. Secundianus [erected this] for his pious wife Atlicilla, aged 20 years.
EDCS-65800070.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young woman named Atlicilla, erected by her husband Secundianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65903889.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a possessive label or part of a larger text, referring to Gnaeus Lentulus Augur, a Roman public figure.

EDCS-66000059.translation: To the Divine Shades. Quintus Celtisius, son of Quintus, Ambustus, a duovir and priest of Endobellus, and his wife Porcia erected this for themselves. May the earth rest lightly upon him.
EDCS-66000059.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Celtisius Ambustus, a local magistrate and priest, and his wife Porcia, from the Roman province of Hispania.

EDCS-66000060.translation: Aurelia, a woman, 12 years old, wife of the duovir and of Aurelia Caena Vor, and for O. Q. Terno, and Terentius, son of Cecilius Vitellius.
EDCS-66000060.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented list or dedication, possibly related to family members or benefactors, with individuals named Aurelia, Terno, and Vitellius.

EDCS-66000062.translation: To the holy genius of the city. Marcus Aurelius Pompeianus, Flamen Divi Augusti from Toletum, dedicated [this] to the divine emperors, a future consul, on the Ides of January, under Emperor Antoninus Augustus and Balbus, consuls.
EDCS-66000062.summary: This is a dedication to the city's genius by a local priest and politician, likely dating to the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-66000064.translation: By the authority of our Lords, the eternal emperors Diocletian and Maximian, most pious, fortunate, ever-Augusti. Lucius Mussidius Longus, from Corduba, legatus pro praetore Daciani, a former governor, curator of the bank and shores of the river Tagus and of the sewers of Toletum, marked the bank with the next stone, 44 feet, where the sewers discharge into the river, and in an underground place [dedicated to] the sewer goddess, but some people of a superstitious cult dedicated [something] to her.
EDCS-66000064.summary: This is a monumental inscription detailing the work of Lucius Mussidius Longus, a governor and curator, in defining river banks and sewer outlets in Toletum, likely dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-66000066.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly a funerary monument or dedication, mentioning the names Augustus, Lucius Caecilius, and Valeria.

EDCS-66000077.summary: This is a very brief funerary epitaph for a person named Vera.

EDCS-66000078.translation: Mamilius Arrenus paid his vow to Tutela willingly and with a good heart.
EDCS-66000078.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Mamilius Arrenus fulfilling a vow to the goddess Tutela.

EDCS-66000092.translation: Manlius Norbanus, by his own hand.
EDCS-66000092.summary: This is a brief inscription, possibly an ownership mark or a signature, attributed to Manlius Norbanus.

EDCS-66000106.translation: To Asclepiodotus, freedman of the patron of the city, honored by the people of Moneten, for his perpetual service as aedile, the community of Carpeta has erected this monument.
EDCS-66000106.summary: A funerary monument erected by the community of Carpeta to their patron Asclepiodotus for his service as aedile, likely dating to the 1st century CE.

EDCS-66000112.summary: A simple funerary inscription identifying the deceased, Marsus, and his father, Acilius.

EDCS-66000122.translation: This is for Sempronius, freedwoman of Lucius. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-66000122.summary: A funerary inscription for Sempronius, a freedwoman, with a common epitaph wishing her a light burial.

EDCS-66000125.translation: To Munius, son of Quintus Cinnius, as *iivir* and *qq*, the most distinguished order of Bercicallienses has erected this monument with good will.
EDCS-66000125.summary: A dedication from the town council of Bercicallienses to Munius, an official of the town, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-66000126.translation: To the shades of the departed. This tomb is the property of Anixus, son of Anter Mummius of Bercicalli, a freedman of Quintus.
EDCS-66000126.summary: A funerary inscription for Anixus, son of Mummius, identifying his family and origin, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-67000022.translation: Kypselos wrote. Arkyllos sent me to my dear friend. Arkyllos dedicated me to Meries.
EDCS-67000022.summary: A bilingual inscription (Greek text and Latin graffiti) where Arkyllos dedicates a statue or object to his friend Meries, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67000190.translation: All those beyond Aristarchus: Aristarchos, Aristoteles, Sosidemos, Epikorus, Parmenon, Kaustrios, Demetrios, Pythogenes.
EDCS-67000190.summary: A list of names, possibly attendees at a meeting or participants in an event, mentioning Aristarchus and a group of others.

EDCS-67000191.translation: Poseidon of the Tintini. Heorton, Sosipatros, Hermokaikos, Parmenon.
EDCS-67000191.summary: A list of names, likely belonging to individuals from the Tintini family or associated with them.

EDCS-67100113.translation: Emperor Caesar Flavius Constantinus Augustus, cultivator of peace and justice, founder of religion and faith, decreed that the province of Betula, inside the mountains of Castulonensis, which had been repeatedly ravaged, should be restored, in the year 333.
EDCS-67100113.summary: An inscription from Emperor Constantine I, dating to 333 CE, recording the restoration of the province of Betula.

EDCS-67200293.translation: Behold your son, behold your mother: Luke, Matthew, Mark, John.

EDCS-67200294.translation: Jesus Christ, Matthew, John, Mark, Luke, Mother of God.
EDCS-67200294.summary: This inscription is a Greek religious text, potentially devotional or funerary, mentioning Jesus Christ and apostles.

EDCS-67400297.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Rin us made this for his son Silvanus, the best, aged 9 years.
EDCS-67400297.summary: This is a Roman funerary inscription dedicating a monument to a young boy named Silvanus by his father Rin us.

EDCS-67400299.summary: This is a Latin inscription, likely a label or a very short record, possibly related to food or provisions.

EDCS-67500090.translation: Publius Pedanius Rufus willingly fulfilled his vow to the Nymphs.

EDCS-68200009.translation: Valerius and Parcis willingly fulfilled their vow.

EDCS-69100255.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Terentia Barbara, daughter, had this made for her parent, Terentius Attius, aged 80.

EDCS-70200026.translation: To the Manes. For Pomponia Agrippina, dearest daughter, who lived 6 years, 6 months, and 23 days. Caius Pomponius Quirinus, her father [made this].

EDCS-70200187.translation: Sacred to the Manes. For Iulius.

EDCS-70300223.translation: From the workshop of Claudius Sota for Caius Pompeius Festinus.

EDCS-70600113.summary: This inscription records a partial personal name, likely of a Roman citizen, possibly with an incomplete gentilician or cognomen.

EDCS-70900687.translation: To Acconius, son of edaes Ambatus, aged 60.
EDCS-70900687.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Acconius, son of edaes Ambatus, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-70900688.summary: This inscription appears to record two personal names, likely of Iberian origin.

EDCS-70900689.translation: To Segontius Caecus, son of Ablonus, aged 75.
EDCS-70900689.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Segontius Caecus, son of Ablonus, who died at the age of 75.

EDCS-71100126.translation: To Sempronia Marcella, daughter of Tautia Ides.
EDCS-71100126.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Sempronia Marcella, daughter of Tautia Ides.

EDCS-71500032.translation: The army fled from Marcus Lepidus in fear.
EDCS-71500032.summary: This is a fragmented inscription possibly related to military events involving Marcus Lepidus.

EDCS-72400203.translation: Molmanio from Molman, to Terentius, son of Ar, to Terentius Ta, from Segontia, the chief.
EDCS-72400203.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or record of individuals and their origins, possibly related to local elites in Segontia.

EDCS-72500077.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription, likely part of a longer text recording a name and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-72600035.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Camala.

EDCS-72600036.translation: Marcus Scaevius Vaelio, a standard-bearer of the cohort, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-72600036.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Marcus Scaevius Vaelio, a military officer, to the Nymphs.

EDCS-72600052.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Valeria Aemiliana, his wife, placed this for Valerius Qu[--], aged 38, and for Aemelio Quietus, aged 101.
EDCS-72600052.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a wife for her husband and possibly another individual, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72800079.translation: Of Cassia Ambada, daughter of Festus, aged 70.

EDCS-72800080.translation: Of Ambaia Doghica, daughter of Vigani, aged 83.

EDCS-72800082.translation: To Segilo Etanicus, son of Hortensius Segontius, aged 26.
EDCS-72800082.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Segilo Etanicus, son of Hortensius Segontius, stating his age at death.

EDCS-73900040.translation: To Valerius Novimbrus, aged 11, [from?] Valeria Firmilla.
EDCS-73900040.summary: This funerary epitaph records the name and age of Valerius Novimbrus, likely dedicated by Valeria Firmilla.

EDCS-73900059.translation: Petrus, Andreas, Iacobus, Ioannes, Philippus, Bartholomaeus, Paulus.

EDCS-74000008.translation: To Lucius Marcius, aedile, twice as aedile, flamen.
EDCS-74000008.summary: This inscription records the official career of Lucius Marcius, mentioning his positions as aedile and flamen.

EDCS-74100018.translation: Annius Aelius Zosimus, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-74100018.summary: This inscription is a very short epitaph for Annius Aelius Zosimus, possibly a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-74100019.translation: Faustus, son of Astlumis, and Quarta Cassia Medugeni, daughter, by her own will, are buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon them.
EDCS-74100019.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Faustus and Quarta Cassia, expressing a wish for peace in the afterlife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74100240.translation: To the Nymphs, having fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-74100240.summary: This is a brief dedication to the Nymphs, indicating the fulfillment of a vow, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74200163.translation: To the Divine Manes of Iullius.
EDCS-74200163.summary: This is a dedication to the spirits of the deceased Iullius, a common funerary formula.

EDCS-74200232.translation: You will die here, in the prime of your youth, you who deserved to live many years. With outstanding character, you rise to the world like a titan; at mid-course the Fates cut your threads. In you, piety, devotion, and faith shone. Generous to the poor, tenacious of justice, whoever counts your years would say you are young, counting your virtues would say you are old. Boys and youths and old men mourn you with tears. The city of Toledo calls you its father. You are called king of kings in your kingdom, where there is continuous peace and cloudless days. By a sepulchral lot, Tulga Leocadia, a virgin, is associated with you, always a friend and companion on earth, and a faithful companion above the heavens. The king rejoices in your goodness. You are snatched from the earth, prince, as if to tread among the stars. Your virtues have cleared the way for you.
EDCS-74200232.summary: This lengthy epitaph commemorates Tulga Leocadia, a virgin associated with a figure named Tulga, possibly a king, lamenting her untimely death and praising her virtues, dating likely to late antiquity or the early medieval period in Toledo.

EDCS-75900448.summary: This is a simple tombstone recording the burial of a person named Osca.

EDCS-75900469.translation: Leda of Teneius, daughter of Turiacus, willingly paid her vow and joyfully erected an altar for her freedwoman Maia.
EDCS-75900469.summary: This is an altar dedication by Leda for her freedwoman Maia, likely a private religious offering.

EDCS-75900487.translation: To the divine spirits of Julia Flavinia, daughter of Flavius, aged 50.
EDCS-75900487.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julia Flavinia, daughter of Flavius, who died at the age of 50.

EDCS-75900489.translation: To the divine spirits. Antonius Flavinus had this made for his daughter Antonia Flavinia, aged 12. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-75900489.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Antonius Flavinus for his daughter Antonia Flavinia, who died at age 12.

EDCS-75900490.translation: Lucius Sempronius Flavinus, father, had this made for his most dutiful son Paterno, aged [unknown].
EDCS-75900490.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Lucius Sempronius Flavinus for his son Paterno.

EDCS-75900491.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Lucius Antonius Paternus.

EDCS-75900493.translation: Antonia Anquema, daughter of Pius, aged 30.
EDCS-75900493.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Antonia Anquema.

EDCS-76100201.translation: Aurelia, a married woman, aged 13, and [wife?] of Caeno, and a member of the extended family of T. Ternus, son of Teliius, son of Caecilius Vitellus.
EDCS-76100201.summary: This inscription likely records the age and family connections of a woman named Aurelia.

EDCS-76100202.translation: The famous king Recesvintus is buried here; he died in the year 672.
EDCS-76100202.summary: This epitaph records the burial of King Recesvintus and the year of his death.

EDCS-76100210.summary: This inscription records a date according to the era system.

EDCS-76100215.translation: To Quintus Flavius Severinus, son of Gaius, of the tribe Galeria. Gaius Iulius Se-
EDCS-76100215.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Iulius Se- to Quintus Flavius Severinus, son of Gaius, of the tribe Galeria.

EDCS-76100226.translation: To Hercules, father, the god, protector and healer, to whom the two magistrates and the citizens of Toletum built a temple on the highest ridge of the municipium, with stage plays and a banquet, the triumviri of the Triunchenses grant this as a gift.
EDCS-76100226.summary: This inscription records a temple built by the magistrates and citizens of Toletum to Hercules, protector and healer, with games and a banquet provided by the triumviri of the Triunchenses.

EDCS-76100228.translation: To Gnaeus Pompeius Faustus, son of Gnaeus Pompeius Peregrinus, citizen of Toletum, consul, who laudably administered the redemptory games for them and on his birthday, the fourth day before the Ides of June, the birthday of Hercules, the tutelary god, having given twenty pairs of gladiators and eight quail and thirty turtledoves, the citizens of Triunchenses [offer] stage plays annually as a worthy gift.
EDCS-76100228.summary: This inscription honors Gnaeus Pompeius Faustus, a former consul from Toletum, for his generous provision of gladiatorial games and other gifts on Hercules' birthday, as a gift from the citizens of Triunchenses.

EDCS-76200009.summary: This is a very brief inscription identifying Caonius as a bishop, possibly referring to a priestly office.

EDCS-76200210.translation: Valeria Atota rests here, aged 24. Farewell.
EDCS-76200210.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Valeria Atota, who died at the age of 24.

EDCS-77600004.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lavia, who died at the age of 42.

EDCS-77900016.translation: Freely according to his vow, Gaius Atilius Cupitus.
EDCS-77900016.summary: This is an votive inscription by Gaius Atilius Cupitus, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-78200091.translation: To the Nymphs, Valerius Servatus willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-78200091.summary: This is a votive inscription by Valerius Servatus to the Nymphs, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-78200098.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Acia had this made for her husband Agileus, aged 49.
EDCS-78200098.summary: This is a tombstone inscription commissioned by Acia for her husband Agileus, who died at the age of 49.

EDCS-78200123.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aemilius Maternus, son of Aemilius, from Uxama, aged 19, rests here.
EDCS-78200123.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aemilius Maternus, son of Aemilius, from Uxama, who died at the age of 19.

EDCS-78200124.translation: Appius Prescarieonus set up this monument to his friend Elanus, son of Arao, aged 20, who lies here.
EDCS-78200124.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Appius Prescarieonus for his friend Elanus.

EDCS-78200126.translation: To Ambatus, son of Terentius Segi, aged 60.
EDCS-78200126.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Ambatus, son of Terentius Segi.

EDCS-78200130.translation: Dovidona, daughter of Desicsa and Ammbatus.
EDCS-78200130.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Dovidona, daughter of Desicsa and Ammbatus.

EDCS-78200131.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Ambatus [son of...]
EDCS-78200131.summary: This is a fragmented funerary dedication, likely to Ambatus.

EDCS-78200133.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly a dedication by Plotius and Plotia.

EDCS-78200136.translation: His heirs had this made according to his will, to Sextus Uttedius, son of Titus, from the Stellatina tribe, named Seranus.
EDCS-78200136.summary: This funerary monument was erected by the heirs of Sextus Uttedius, from the Stellatina tribe, in accordance with his will.

EDCS-78300589.translation: Quintu Vireius, son of Caius, of the Stellatina tribe.
EDCS-78300589.summary: This is likely a funerary dedication for Quintus Vireius, son of Caius, from the Stellatina tribe.

EDCS-78300590.translation: Quintu Vireius, son of Caius, of the Stellatina tribe.
EDCS-78300590.summary: This is likely a funerary dedication for Quintus Vireius, son of Caius, from the Stellatina tribe.

EDCS-78300607.translation: Lucius Scipio, son of Gnaeus, son of Gnaeus, son of Silas, son of Gnaeus; from the Magonia tribe; Publius Fonteius, son of Publius.
EDCS-78300607.summary: This inscription lists names and tribal affiliations, possibly related to a public work or family.

EDCS-78300608.translation: Hadrian, emperor, august, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, [ordered] the road from Tarraco for the Lusitanian and Baetican legions.
EDCS-78300608.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian's order for a road to be built from Tarraco for the Lusitanian and Baetican legions.

EDCS-78300609.translation: To Jupiter Medullius Laricus, from L. Licinius Deius.
EDCS-78300609.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Medullius Laricus by L. Licinius Deius, likely an individual offering.

EDCS-78300610.translation: The Emperor Nerva Trajan, Pontifex Maximus, made the road.
EDCS-78300610.summary: This inscription records Emperor Nerva Trajan as the maker of a road, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-78300611.translation: The August sacred order, the decurions of Amphilochus, dedicated this.
EDCS-78300611.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the local council of magistrates (decurions) of Amphilochus.

EDCS-78300612.translation: Segada was here; sacred to the colony, to Patricius.
EDCS-78300612.summary: This is a dedication related to the colony and possibly a figure named Patricius, mentioning a person named Segada.

EDCS-80000090.translation: Lucius Porcius Partenius, grandfather, saw to its construction.
EDCS-80000090.summary: This inscription records Lucius Porcius Partenius, identified as a grandfather, who commissioned the construction of a monument.

EDCS-80000091.summary: This is a simple inscription likely indicating ownership or dedication related to Marcus Anicius Strenomus.

EDCS-80200116.translation: Gaius Aquilius Verus, an evocatus of the VII Gemina Felix legion, dedicated (this) to Health Augusta, to the Genius of the Campus Martius, to Minerva, and to Victory.
EDCS-80200116.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Aquilius Verus, a soldier of the VII Gemina Felix legion, to several deities including Augusta, the Genius of the Campus Martius, Minerva, and Victory, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-80400011.translation: To our lord Emperor Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-80400011.summary: This is an honorific inscription addressed to Emperor Constantius Caesar (likely Constantius I or II), dating to the 4th century AD.

EDCS-80900018.summary: This inscription names VAS and Acutius, with 'cinaedus' possibly referring to a type of performer or an epithet.

EDCS-80900098.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription containing partial names, possibly belonging to the Licinia and Galeria families.

EDCS-80900099.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary epitaph or dedication, with incomplete personal names. The date is uncertain due to the damage.

EDCS-80900226.translation: Marcus Aurelius Celer Abolodeneco fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-80900226.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aurelius Celer Abolodeneco, likely from the Roman Imperial period, indicating the fulfillment of a vow to a deity.

EDCS-80900227.translation: Lucius Iulius for Rea Banduga, according to his vow.
EDCS-80900227.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Iulius dedicated to Rea Banduga, implying a religious offering or dedication in fulfillment of a vow, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-80900235.translation: Reburricanus Sorivio, servant of Augusti Enemeo.
EDCS-80900235.summary: This inscription identifies Reburricanus Sorivio as a servant of 'Augusti Enemeo', likely a freedman or official associated with the imperial household, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-80900273.translation: Erected to the Divine Spirits. For the slave Coreidaecon, aged 35, son of Cominemerus. For Ar. Tas Sanius, aged 18. Iu...
EDCS-80900273.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the ages and familial relationships of two individuals, Coreidaecon (a slave) and Ar. Tas Sanius, intended for the 'Divine Spirits', probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-80900274.translation: Camalielus had this made for Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the god, after a vision.
EDCS-80900274.summary: This is a votive inscription by Camalielus, who commissioned a religious structure or offering for Jupiter Optimus Maximus after receiving a divine vision, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81000014.translation: Publius Baebius Hermes gladly fulfilled his vow to the Dracones.
EDCS-81000014.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Baebius Hermes, who willingly offered a vow to deities referred to as 'Dracones', likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81100006.translation: For Quemia Taurometa, daughter of Balbus, aged 30.
EDCS-81100006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quemia Taurometa, daughter of Balbus, providing her age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81100008.translation: Longinus and Val for their parents. For Semplicia Mat, aged 70, 60. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-81100008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Longinus and Val for their parents, mentioning Semplicia Mat and her ages, with a standard Latin epitaphial phrase, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81200059.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Magilo as the son of Esarus, possibly an epitaph or ownership mark, with an uncertain date but likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-81200183.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Licinius Chilo willingly fulfilled his vow for his husband.
EDCS-81200183.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter by Lucius Licinius Chilo.

EDCS-81200184.translation: Quintus Le, according to his vow.
EDCS-81200184.summary: This inscription records a fulfillment of a vow by Quintus Le.

EDCS-81200187.translation: In the name of the Lord, Sicorius.
EDCS-81200187.summary: This is a short inscription mentioning Sicorius in the name of the Lord, likely Christian.

EDCS-81200191.translation: Lucius Aurelius Ursinus ordered this to be set up for himself in his will.
EDCS-81200191.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Aurelius Ursinus's posthumous arrangement for his monument.

EDCS-81200213.summary: This is a very abbreviated inscription, possibly a dedication or identification by Gaius Licinius Adin(---).

EDCS-81400116.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus expressing gratitude.

EDCS-81500075.translation: Marcellus Gaius, may we have happy years, thanks to the lord CN.
EDCS-81500075.summary: This inscription offers thanks to a lord CN for happy years, attributed to Marcellus Gaius.

EDCS-81700060.translation: To the god Lari Bero Breo, I set up an altar for my health.
EDCS-81700060.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to the gods Lari Bero Breo for the donor's health.

EDCS-81700061.translation: To the god Lari Bero Breo, I set up an altar for my health.
EDCS-81700061.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to the gods Lari Bero Breo for the donor's health.

EDCS-81700062.translation: To the god Lari Bero Breo, I set up an altar for my health.
EDCS-81700062.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to the gods Lari Bero Breo for the donor's health.

EDCS-81700063.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar for his own health.
EDCS-81700063.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari for his health.

EDCS-81700064.translation: Bero Breo erected this altar for the health of D.
EDCS-81700064.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo for the health of an individual named D.

EDCS-81700065.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar for his health.
EDCS-81700065.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari for his health.

EDCS-81700066.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar.
EDCS-81700066.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari.

EDCS-81700067.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar for the health of the state.
EDCS-81700067.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari for the health of the state.

EDCS-81700068.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar for his health.
EDCS-81700068.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari for his health.

EDCS-81700069.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo.
EDCS-81700069.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Lari by Bero Breo.

EDCS-81700071.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar for his health.
EDCS-81700071.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari for his health.

EDCS-81700072.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar.
EDCS-81700072.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari.

EDCS-81700073.translation: To the god Lari, Bero Breo erected this altar.
EDCS-81700073.summary: This inscription records an altar erected by Bero Breo to the god Lari.

EDCS-81700075.translation: I dedicated an altar to the god Bero for health.
EDCS-81700075.summary: This is an altar dedicated by an unknown individual to the god Bero for the sake of their health.

EDCS-81700076.translation: To the god Laris, Bero [dedicated this].
EDCS-81700076.summary: This is a dedication to the god Laris by Bero.

EDCS-81700077.translation: To the god Laris, Bero [dedicated this].
EDCS-81700077.summary: This is a dedication to the god Laris by Bero.

EDCS-81700078.translation: I dedicated an altar to the god Laris for health.
EDCS-81700078.summary: This is an altar dedicated by an unknown individual to the god Laris for the sake of their health.

EDCS-81700079.translation: To the god Laris, Bero [dedicated this].
EDCS-81700079.summary: This is a dedication to the god Laris by Bero.

EDCS-81700080.translation: To the god Laris, Bero [dedicated this].
EDCS-81700080.summary: This is a dedication to the god Laris by Bero.

EDCS-81700083.translation: I dedicated an altar for health.
EDCS-81700083.summary: This is an altar dedicated by an unknown individual for the sake of their health.

EDCS-81700084.translation: I dedicated an altar for health.
EDCS-81700084.summary: This is an altar dedicated by an unknown individual for the sake of their health.

EDCS-81700087.summary: This is an altar dedicated by Breo.

EDCS-81700088.summary: This is an altar dedicated by Bero.

EDCS-81700089.translation: To the god Lari, Bero dedicated an altar.
EDCS-81700089.summary: This inscription is an altar dedication by Bero to the god Lari.

EDCS-81700090.translation: Bero set up an altar for [my/his/her] well-being.
EDCS-81700090.summary: This inscription records Bero setting up an altar for well-being.

EDCS-81700096.summary: This inscription records Breo setting up an altar.

EDCS-81700264.translation: To the god Lari, Bero and Breo.
EDCS-81700264.summary: This inscription names the dedicants Bero and Breo and the god Lari.

EDCS-81700340.summary: This inscription is likely part of a funerary monument for a girl named Avitina.

EDCS-82000110.translation: To Lucius Fufidius Palma and Orestes, his son, Aemilia Quinta, daughter of Marcus, his wife, by his will.
EDCS-82000110.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her deceased husband and son, established by will.

EDCS-82000154.translation: Sacred to Apollo Augustus. L. Arsius Annulus, son of L., magistrate of the Augustales, patron of the colony of Quirites Augustani Valeriani, willingly and deservedly placed this.
EDCS-82000154.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Apollo Augustus by L. Arsius Annulus, an important local official, likely on a public building or monument.

EDCS-82000155.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Paternianensis, Quintia Marcella, his most beloved wife, willingly and deservedly placed this.
EDCS-82000155.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, Marcus Aurelius Paternianensis.

EDCS-82000156.translation: C. Caecilia Quieta, wife of Paternia, priestess, placed this monument willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-82000156.summary: This funerary inscription records C. Caecilia Quieta, wife of Paternia and a priestess, dedicating a monument.

EDCS-82000157.translation: Hostarius Geminus, of Paternus, aged 36, lies here; willingly and deservedly placed this.
EDCS-82000157.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Hostarius Geminus, aged 36.

EDCS-82000161.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely names a relative, possibly a grandfather, of an individual named Julius.

EDCS-82000204.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple epitaph identifying Lucius as the son of Proculus.

EDCS-82000207.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to name an individual named Vita, son of Alius.

EDCS-82000220.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan, son of the Divine Nerva, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune of the Plebs...
EDCS-82000220.summary: This inscription fragment records titles of Emperor Trajan, likely from an official monument.

EDCS-82000227.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a meaningful translation or summary beyond the partial word 'Israel'.

EDCS-82100001.summary: This is a short, potentially epigrammatic or personal inscription, possibly referring to a pet or an insect.

EDCS-82100005.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Maximianus, son of Marcus, grandson of Marcus, by choice of the Ulians, for his public services, and especially for having fashioned this life-like statue of a huge dragon, with the intercession of the most august virgin and martyr, our patroness, who brought victory to the republic when it was troubled; to their most deserving patron, an equestrian statue, by decree of Gaius Junius Cassius, Gaius Valerius Felix, duooviri, and Ovinius and Marianus, consuls.
EDCS-82100005.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an equestrian statue to Marcus Aurelius Maximianus by the town of Ulpia, honoring his public service and his creation of a dragon statue, with the intercession of a patroness, during the consulship of Cassius and Felix in an unspecified year.

EDCS-82100013.translation: Workshop of Aulus Cornelius Pas[...] of SQBQ.
EDCS-82100013.summary: This inscription appears to record the workshop of Aulus Cornelius Pas, possibly associated with the acronym SQBQ.

EDCS-82100019.translation: To Lucius Mitidius, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, Capito, Roman equestrian, curator of the colony of Augusta Iulia, having been admitted to the order of decurions, citizen of Augusta, augur, and duoovir, and iivir, iur d[icundo], patron of the fullers' guild, by his own means and at his own expense, a donation by decree.
EDCS-82100019.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Mitidius Capito, a Roman equestrian and prominent citizen of Augusta Iulia, who served in various civic and religious offices and was honored as patron of the fullers' guild.

EDCS-82100036.translation: To Lucius Terentius Pompeius Bassus, son of Gnaeus, of the Papiria tribe, from Toletum, quaestor, former quaestor, former aedile, perpetual flaminius, and patron of the municipality of Lamas, because he restored the baths and the Marian road as far as the 70th milestone, and ordered the hanging baths in his homeland on Mount Coelius, the baths on Mount Fulvius, the baths at the Depressed Valley, and the Sacred road, the Flaminian road, and the road of Lamas to be repaired; to their patron, with the mark of good will, an equestrian statue, by decree of Sextus Pompeius, Sextus Apuleius, consuls.
EDCS-82100036.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an equestrian statue to Lucius Terentius Pompeius Bassus, patron of Lamas, for his restoration of public works including baths and roads, during the consulship of Sextus Pompeius and Sextus Apuleius.

EDCS-82100037.translation: Vespasian Augustus, having conquered the province of Arelate, subdued the disobedient.
EDCS-82100037.summary: This inscription, likely from the Flavian period, commemorates Emperor Vespasian's victory and subjugation of the province of Arelate.

EDCS-82100039.translation: To Gaius Licinius, the father of Macedon, and to Flaminia, his wife. Sempronius [erected this?].
EDCS-82100039.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Gaius Licinius and his wife Flaminia, possibly erected by Sempronius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82100040.translation: Gaius Cortelius Carrius and Lucius Heius Labeo, as duovirs, carried out the repair of the aqueduct at the expense of the state, by decree of the decurions, with their own funds.
EDCS-82100040.summary: This inscription records the completion of repairs to the aqueduct by the duovirs Gaius Cortelius Carrius and Lucius Heius Labeo, likely dating to the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-82100041.translation: Gaius Cornelius Carus and Lucius Heius Labeo, as duovirs, carried out the repair of the aqueduct at the expense of the state, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-82100041.summary: This inscription records the completion of repairs to the aqueduct by the duovirs Gaius Cornelius Carus and Lucius Heius Labeo, likely dating to the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-82100047.translation: Crispus, the overseer of the grain supply, [dedicated this?].
EDCS-82100047.summary: This very brief inscription appears to name Crispus, possibly an official responsible for the grain supply (horrearius or similar), with a likely dedication, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82100053.translation: Who, having served in the army for many years, and fighting bravely, brought great glory to himself and his fatherland. Erected and dedicated by the best citizens of the municipality of Alarium.
EDCS-82100053.summary: This dedication honors a soldier who served with distinction, recognizing his bravery and the glory he brought to himself and his homeland, erected by the citizens of Alarium in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82100068.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Gaius Allius Victor, son of Gaius, a tribune of the military of the 19th legion, who overcame the Illyrians and the Illygetae, vanquished by fate, lived 32 years and 3 months. His father, Gaius Allius Victor, Optatus, [erected this?] and [dedicated it?].
EDCS-82100068.summary: This funerary inscription honors Gaius Allius Victor, a military tribune who died at the age of 32 after serving in the 19th legion and fighting against the Illyrians and Illygetae, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82100102.summary: This short inscription is a Christian blessing, wishing for Christ's presence within the individual, likely dating from the Late Roman or Early Byzantine period.

EDCS-82100106.translation: To Terginus? and Eguanus? May they live.
EDCS-82100106.summary: This inscription is a short dedication or wish for well-being addressed to individuals named Terginus and Eguanus, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82800033.translation: Titus Aulius Arcisus placed this willingly, from a vision, with his mind, for Diana.
EDCS-82800033.summary: This votive inscription records Titus Aulius Arcisus dedicating an offering to the goddess Diana, motivated by a vision, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.
